Histopathological image analysis is the gold standard for cancer diagnosis but is time-consuming, requires a high level of expertise, and is subject to inter-observer variability. The advancement of Digital Pathology enables the application of deep learning models for automating and enhancing diagnostic accuracy. In particular, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for identifying morphological features in histopathological images, achieving accuracy comparable to that of medical experts in specific tasks such as tissue classification. Within the framework of this study, an interpretable CNN model is developed and evaluated for classifying gastric histopathological images as benign or malignant using the GasHisSDB dataset. The Monte Carlo Dropout method is applied to estimate the uncertainty of the model's predictions, while the Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GradCAM) method is combined with quantitative image feature analysis towards enabling the spatial localization of image regions that influence the model's predictions. The proposed approach achieved an AUC score of 98.6 % while maintaining low computational cost and architectural simplicity. The generated interpretations yielded useful insights into spatially important image regions and their associated nuclear morphological characteristics.

 Deep Learning Convolutional Neural Networks Gastric Cancer Histopathology Image Classification Uncertainty Quantification Interpretability Grad-CAM Monte Carlo Dropout \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{0131}{\ifmmode\imath\else{$\imath$}\fi} Introduction

Histopathology, which involves the microscopic examination of tissues and cells, remains the gold standard for diagnosing a wide range of diseases, including cancer [1]. Accurate classification of histopathological images is crucial for effective diagnosis, treatment planning, and prognosis. Traditionally, this task has relied on visual assessment of digital microscopy slides by expert pathologists. However, this process is time-consuming, subjective, and susceptible to inter-observer variability.

Advancements in artificial intelligence have enabled the increasing application of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) methods, such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), in histopathological image classification [2]. These models are trained to recognize morphological features and patterns associated with either normal or pathological conditions [3], thereby reducing diagnostic subjectivity and accelerating the diagnostic process through the detection of subtle differences in cellular or histological characteristics, which may escape human observation.

Gastrointestinal (GI) cancer is a common form of malignancy, often asymptomatic in its early stages, and constitutes the fifth most frequently diagnosed type of cancer, accounting for 18 % of total cancer-related deaths [4]. Histopathological examination remains essential for GI cancer diagnosis, typically involving the microscopic analysis of tissue sections from suspicious lesions, conducted by experienced pathologists [5].

The successful integration of deep learning (DL) methods into histopathological workflows has demonstrated its potential to achieve expert-level diagnostic performance while improving time and cost efficiency and scalability [6]. In the domain of GI cancer, a wide range of DL techniques have been explored for the classification of histopathological images. Most studies adopt pretrained CNNs such as VGG16, ResNet50, and DenseNet121, which are fine-tuned on medical image datasets to extract complex morphological features. Beyond these, hybrid and ensemble models have also been developed to boost performance through methods such as attention mechanisms, including the Multi-Channel Attention Mechanism (MCAM) for gastric cancer [7] and stacking ensembles combining multiple CNNs [8]. In parallel, Vision Transformers (ViTs) and CNN-Transformer hybrids, such as GasHis-Transformer [9] and CCDNet, have emerged as promising alternatives, particularly due to their ability to capture global spatial relationships in complex tissue structures. However, despite their impressive accuracy, these approaches often come with substantial computational demands, high model complexity, lack of interpretability, and limited suitability for deployment in resource-constrained environments or realtime clinical settings.

To address these challenges, the present study proposes an interpretable CNN model, based on a lightweight architecture, for the classification of gastric cancer histopathology images into benign and malignant categories, using the GasHisSDB dataset [10]. To enhance model transparency and user trust, uncertainty quantification is performed using Monte Carlo Dropout [11], enabling assessment of the confidence of the model's predictions, while the Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) method [12] is employed to visualize the model's focus on specific spatial regions during inference. Furthermore, a nuclei-level interpretability analysis is conducted, combining Grad-CAM activation heatmaps with quantitative cellular features extracted via HistomicsTK [13], with the aim of providing deeper insights into the biological relevance of the model's decision-making process. An overview of the proposed approach is presented in Figure 1. By focusing on architectural simplicity, reliable performance, and transparency, this work aims to address some of the key challenges that remain open in the current landscape of DL-based histopathology [14].

Overview of the proposed pipeline for the classification of gastric histopathology images and the generation of interpretations.

 Methods Dataset

The development and evaluation of the proposed model was based on the use of the GasHisSDB dataset, which contains 245,196 histopathology image patches, categorized as Abnormal (97,076 patches) and Normal (148,120 patches) [10]. The dataset was constructed through a twostage process. Initially, 600 high-resolution $(2048 \times 2048)$ gastric tissue images were annotated as Normal or Abnormal by four pathologists at Longhua Hospital. These images were then partitioned into sub-images of varying resolutions by biomedical researchers at Northeastern University and further verified by two expert pathologists from Liaoning Cancer Hospital, ensuring label consistency and diagnostic accuracy. Within the framework of the present study, the $120 \times 120$ pixel resolution was selected, corresponding to a subset of 65,261 image patches, with the aim to strike a balance between preserving sufficient morphological information and ensuring computational efficiency.

 Proposed Cnn Model

Model Architecture: The proposed model's architecture was based on a custom 8-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), consisting of four convolutional blocks. Each block contained two Conv2D layers with ReLU activation and $3 \times 3$ kernels, followed by Batch Normalization, and a MaxPooling2D operation. The number of filters doubled with each block (from N to 8N), enabling progressive feature abstraction. During training, Dropout was applied after each block with progressively increasing rates (0.25 to 0.4) to prevent overfitting as the network deepened.

The resulting feature maps were flattened and passed through two fully connected (Dense) layers with 512 and 256 neurons, each followed by Batch Normalization and Dropout (applied during training). The final classification layer was a single neuron with sigmoid activation, producing a probability for classifying an image patch as abnormal. This architecture was lightweight yet expressive, leveraging hierarchical feature extraction to capture cancerrelevant patterns. To ensure convergence and generalization, the model integrated regularization techniques (Dropout, Batch Normalization) and was trained using the Adam optimizer.

2) Hyperparameter Tuning: Hyperparameter optimization was performed using Grid Search over a predefined parameter space. The grid featured the number of filters [32], [64], kernel size [(3,3), (5,5)], dropout rate [0.3, 0.5], and learning rate [$0.001,0.0001$]. Each configuration was evaluated on a fixed validation set.

 Uncertainty Estimation

The Monte Carlo Dropout (MC Dropout) method was applied for quantifying prediction uncertainty. After training, each test image was passed through the model 50 times with dropout enabled during inference, producing a distribution of stochastic outputs. The mean of the predicted probabilities from these forward passes was used as the final classification output: \begin{equation*} \mathbb{E}_{q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)}\left(y^{*}\right) \approx \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat{y}^{*}\left(x^{*}, W_{1}^{(t)}, \ldots, W_{L}^{(t)}\right) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\mathbb{E}_{q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)}\left(y^{*}\right)$ is the expected prediction for the input $x^{*}$ under the predictive distribution $q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)$, representing the mean prediction, $T$ is the number of stochastic forward passes through the model, $\hat{y}^{*}\left(x^{*}, \mathbf{W}_{1}^{(t)}, \ldots, \mathbf{W}_{L}^{(t)}\right)$ is the model's predicted output during the $t$-th pass, given input $x^{*}$ and a specific set of sampled weights.

The standard deviation of predictions across forward passes served as a measure of uncertainty: \begin{equation*} \operatorname{Var}\left(y^{*}\right) \approx \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T}\left(\hat{y}_{t}^{*}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat{y}_{t}^{*}\right)^{2} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\operatorname{Var}(y)$ represents the predictive variance of the model for a new input $x$, quantifying the uncertainty in its predictions, and $\hat{y}_{t}^{*}$ corresponds to the scalar prediction generated at each pass.

 Interpretability Using Grad-Cam

Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GradCAM), a post-hoc visualization method widely used for explaining the spatial reasoning of CNNs, was deployed for generating interpretations of the model's predictions [12]. Grad-CAM identifies the spatial regions of an input image that contribute most significantly to a model's decision, offering visual explanations without requiring architectural modifications or retraining. The method operates by computing the gradients of the predicted class score $y^{c}$ with respect to the feature maps $A^{k}$ from a chosen network layer. These gradients are then globally averaged to produce importance weights $\alpha_{k}^{c}$ for class $c$ and channel $k$: \begin{equation*} \alpha_{k}^{c}=\frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \frac{\partial y^{c}}{\partial A_{i j}^{k}} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $Z$ is the number of spatial locations. The final GradCAM heatmap is computed by taking a weighted combination of the feature maps, followed by a ReLU activation: \begin{equation*} L_{\text{Grad}-\text{CAM}}^{c}=\operatorname{ReLU}\left(\sum_{k} \alpha_{k}^{c} A^{k}\right) \tag{1} \end{equation*}

This heatmap is then resized to the original image dimensions and overlaid on the input image to visualize which spatial regions most influenced the model's output.

In the present study, Grad-CAM allowed for qualitative validation of the CNN's focus areas in histopathology images, verifying whether the network based its decisions clinically relevant regions. It should be noted that Grad1M's effectiveness is influenced by the choice of nvolutional layer, as early layers capture lower-level itures (e.g., texture, structural) offering less semantic ormation, while deeper layers may sacrifice spatial olution but provide more semantic meaning. In this idy, the last convolutional layer was selected with the n of focusing on high-level semantic patterns associated th tissue-level abnormalities.

 Feature Analysis with Histomicstk

To further explore the morphological characteristics of age regions highlighted by Grad-CAM, the HistomicsTK rary [15] was utilized, which is an open-source toolkit - processing whole-slide images. After applying color rmalization (Reinhard) and hematoxylin extraction via in deconvolution, nuclear segmentation was performed ing a multi-scale Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) detector. r each segmented nucleus, morphometric features (area, entricity, perimeter), Fourier Shape Descriptors (FSD), dient-based features (edge sharpness), and Haralick ure features (contrast and correlation from the gray-level occurrence matrix-GLCM) were extracted.

Focusing on the interpretation of Grad-CAM results for; positive predictions, nuclei were categorized as vated (located within Grad-CAM activation regions) or -activated (located outside activation regions), and their acted feature distributions were compared. This analysis vided insight into whether the model's focus aligned with logically suspicious or diagnostically relevant regions, ngthening the biological interpretability of proposed del's decisions.

 Results and Discussion Evaluation Framework

To reliably assess the model's generalization ability, a tified 5-fold cross-validation scheme was employed, ntaining the original class distribution across all folds. In h iteration, four folds were used for training, and one; held out for testing. A further $75 \%-25 \%$ stratified split the training data created a validation set within each ation of the cross-validation scheme. All hyperameters were fixed across folds for consistency. After erparameters' optimization and evaluation through crossdation, a final evaluation was conducted using a 90-10 n-test split. Model performance was assessed using a prehensive set of metrics: Accuracy (Acc), Sensitivity ns), Specificity (Spec), Precision (Prec), F1-Score, Area der the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC), tthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) [16], and the ıden index.

 Hypeparameters ' Tuning

Following Grid Search, the identified optimal figuration included 64 filters, $3 \times 3$ kernels, 0.5 dropout, and 0.0001 learning rate, which provided the best le-off between training stability, generalization, and uracy. The use of smaller filters enabled finer feature action, while higher dropout and lower learning rate imized overfitting. Model training was conducted for up 5 epochs, with EarlyStopping based on validation loss. ige preprocessing was performed using ImageData terator, applying pixel rescaling ($1. / 255$) and generatored data loading for training and validation. A decision shold of 0.5397 was determined using Youden's J istic on the ROC curve, by selecting the cutoff that ximized the difference between true positive rate and e positive rate on the validation set.

Evaluation of the Model'S Performance

Table I summarizes the obtained performance of the proposed model using 5 -fold stratified cross-validation. The model achieved balanced and strong discrimination performance, with an average AUC of $98.08 \% \pm 3.02 \%$, Sensitivity of $89.15 \% \pm 3.30 \%$ and Specificity of $90.65 \% \pm 2.96 \%$. The observed MCC of $79.19 \% \pm 6.28 \%$ demonstrated a robust correlation between predicted and actual labels, while the F1-score of $87.25 \% \pm 3.80 \%$ reflected a good balance between precision and recall. These results suggest that the model generalized well across folds and maintained consistent behavior in distinguishing abnormal from normal tissue patches. Table II presents the final evaluation results obtained after training the model on 90 % of the dataset and testing on the remaining 10 %, using the optimal configuration identified through grid search. The model yielded high discrimination performance, with an AUC of 98.60 %, MCC of 0.8757, and F 1 -score of 92.33 %. These findings further confirmed the model's effectiveness when deployed on unseen data. Furthermore, when compared to state-of-the-art models trained and evaluated on the GasHisSDB dataset, including MCAM [6], a Gaussian-mixture-based Naïve Bayes framework [17], and a hybrid model combining DenseNet-201 deep features with a Random Forest classifier [18], which achieved accuracy scores of $99.60 \%, 98.47 \%$, and 91.93 %, respectively, the proposed CNN model demonstrated competitive performance, yielding a comparable accuracy of 94.12 % despite its simpler architecture. Additionally, when evaluated against the GasHis-Transformer [9], which was developed and assessed on a different gastric histopathological image dataset and achieved an accuracy of 97.97 %, the proposed CNN also exhibited comparable performance. Uncertainty Estimation

To visually represent the uncertainty of the model on the test set, a histogram of standard deviation values from MC Dropout predictions was generated (Figure 2). The distribution revealed that in the vast majority of cases, the model exhibited low uncertainty, which indicated high confidence in its predictions. As expected, predictions with high uncertainty were associated with output probabilities near the decision threshold of 0.5397, reflecting the model's lack of confidence in class assignment. Capturing these cases of high-uncertainty predictions could serve as an alert mechanism for human review, ensuring that critical or ambiguous cases are not overlooked.

 Interpretability

Grad-CAM Visualization: Within the context of the Grad-CAM analysis, two different visualisations were generated for each test image with the aim of shedding light on specific regions influencing the model's decisions: (i) the normalized Grad-CAM heatmap, representing the raw activations of the final convolutional layer and (ii) a colormapped overlay, where the heatmap was projected onto the original input image using a semi-transparent JET colormap.

Figure 3 depicts two examples of selected true positive (TP) cases alongside their corresponding heatmaps and color-mapped overlays. Visual inspection of the obtained heatmaps shows that Grad-CAM activations were systematically concentrated on biologically meaningful cellular structures, particularly dark-stained, morphologically atypical nuclei or densely packed nuclei.

In contrast, regions of background or healthy tissue exhibited minimal activation, demonstrating that the model effectively filtered out irrelevant regions. Overall, the obtained results indicated the model's ability to focus on morphological features commonly associated with abnormal tissue regions, such as nuclear atypia, increased cellular density, and nuclear hyperchromasia.

2) Nuclei-Level Interpretation with HistomicsTK: To quantitatively link Grad-CAM activation regions with biological entities and validate the model's decision-making process through measurable nuclear associations, activation maps were integrated with nuclear segmentation masks generated using HistomicsTK. This enabled not only visual comparison but also precise spatial correlation between activation maps and segmented nuclei, allowing for more detailed biological interpretation. Each Grad-CAM heatmap was converted into a binary activation mask using a threshold of 0.5 and each nucleus (uniquely labeled in the segmentation mask) was evaluated for overlap with Grad-

Original image samples (left), grad-cam heatmaps (middle), and color-mapped overlays (right) for the true positive samples 01902 and 12012.

CAM activated regions. Nuclei intersecting with activated regions were labeled as "activated."

Table III summarizes the total number of segmented nuclei, the number of activated nuclei, and the percentage of nuclei overlapping with Grad-CAM activations. The proportion of activated nuclei ranged from 30 % to 60 %, with the TP case "Abnormal-12012" exhibiting the highest overlap (60 %). This variation suggested heterogeneity in how consistently the model's attention aligned with nuclear structures across different cases.

Figure 4 depicts representative visualizations for the two examples of selected TP cases, including the original image, the segmented nuclei, and the Grad-CAM heatmap overlay. For both samples, Grad-CAM heatmaps showed concentrated activations in regions densely populated with morphologically atypical nuclei. The segmentation masks confirmed that these activated regions corresponded to areas of potential diagnostic relevance, such as irregularly shaped, hyperchromatic nuclei. The fact that for the two examined cases, nearly half or more of the nuclei, as shown in Table III, fell within activated regions, strengthened confidence in the interpretability and clinical relevance of the CNN's learned representations. Thus, the combination of heatmaps and nuclei masks demonstrated that the model's decisionmaking was not arbitrarily distributed across the image but rather focused on biologically meaningful regions. Feature Analysis with HistomicsTK: To further explore the influence of activated nuclei on the model's decisions, quantitative features extracted for each nucleus using HistomicsTK were combined with Grad-CAM activation data towards examining whether activated nuclei exhibited distinct characteristics compared to non-activated ones. For each segmented nucleus, features from the following categories were considered: (i) morphometric, (ii)

Original image samples (left), segmentation masks (middle), and grad-cam heatmaps (right) for the true positive samples 01902 and 12012.

Fourier Shape Descriptors (FSD), (iii) gradient-based, and (iv) Haralick texture. To compare feature distributions between activated and non-activated nuclei, the MannWhitney U test was applied independently to each feature [19] and the obtained p-values were recorded.

Table IV presents representative features from each category that exhibited statistically significant differences (p -value $<0.05$) between Grad-CAM activated and nonactivated nuclei. Among the morphometric features, both area and perimeter were significantly larger in activated nuclei (e.g., Abnormal-12012) suggesting that the model tended to focus on larger nuclei structures. FSD features (e.g., FSD4 and FSD5 in Abnormal-16643) also showed statistically significant separation, highlighting the model's sensitivity to subtle variations in nuclear boundary complexity. Regarding gradient-based features, standard deviation and kurtosis of the gradient magnitude (e.g., Abnormal-01902) both presented statistically significant differences between activated and non-activated nuclei, indicating the model's focus on sharper transitions and localized structural variation. Texture-based Haralick features, including contrast range and correlation mean, highlighted heterogeneity in chromatin texture and spatial relationships within the nucleus as discriminative characteristics. These findings provided evidence regarding the alignment between Grad-CAM activations and observed differences in nuclear morphology and texture.

 Limitations and Future Work

Potential limitations of the proposed approach include the patch-level data splitting inherent to the GasHisSDB dataset, which consists of $120 \times 120$ image patches extracted from whole-slide images. This design may have introduced data leakage, potentially inflating the reported performance metrics. Moreover, the dataset represents a limited subset of gastric histopathology collected under specific staining and imaging conditions, which may restrict generalizability to data from other institutions or protocols. Future work will focus on large-scale validation across more diverse gastric cancer subtypes and imaging conditions, as well as on independent, multi-institutional cohorts to assess real-world applicability. Extending the approach to other histopathological domains (e.g., colorectal, lung, or breast cancer) may further demonstrate its adaptability and generalizability. Conclusion

This study demonstrated the effectiveness of a relatively lightweight CNN model in the classification of gastric cancer histopathological images. Despite the absence of very deep or pretrained architectures, the model achieved performance levels comparable to state-of-the-art approaches and demonstrated its ability to provide predictions with high confidence. The combination of GradCAM and HistomicsTK enabled both visual localization of discriminative image regions and quantitative analysis of nuclear morphology, and provided evidence regarding the biological relevance of the model's decisions. This integrative approach not only improves transparency and trust in deep learning systems but also brings AI one step closer to clinical applicability in digital pathology.

Histopathological examination remains essential for GI cancer diagnosis, typically involving the microscopic analysis of tissue sections from suspicious lesions, conducted by experienced pathologists [5].

The successful integration of deep learning (DL) methods into histopathological workflows has demonstrated its potential to achieve expert-level diagnostic performance while improving time and cost efficiency and scalability [6]. In the domain of GI cancer, a wide range of DL techniques have been explored for the classification of histopathological images. Most studies adopt pretrained CNNs such as VGG16, ResNet50, and DenseNet121, which are fine-tuned on medical image datasets to extract complex morphological features. Beyond these, hybrid and ensemble models have also been developed to boost performance through methods such as attention mechanisms, including the Multi-Channel Attention Mechanism (MCAM) for gastric cancer [7] and stacking ensembles combining multiple CNNs [8]. In parallel, Vision Transformers (ViTs) and CNN-Transformer hybrids, such as GasHis-Transformer [9] and CCDNet, have emerged as promising alternatives, particularly due to their ability to capture global spatial relationships in complex tissue structures. However, despite their impressive accuracy, these approaches often come with substantial computational demands, high model complexity, lack of interpretability, and limited suitability for deployment in resource-constrained environments or realtime clinical settings.

To address these challenges, the present study proposes an interpretable CNN model, based on a lightweight architecture, for the classification of gastric cancer histopathology images into benign and malignant categories, using the GasHisSDB dataset [10]. To enhance model transparency and user trust, uncertainty quantification is performed using Monte Carlo Dropout [11], enabling assessment of the confidence of the model's predictions, while the Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) method [12] is employed to visualize the model's focus on specific spatial regions during inference. Furthermore, a nuclei-level interpretability analysis is conducted, combining Grad-CAM activation heatmaps with quantitative cellular features extracted via HistomicsTK [13], with the aim of providing deeper insights into the biological relevance of the model's decision-making process. An overview of the proposed approach is presented in Figure 1. By focusing on architectural simplicity, reliable performance, and transparency, this work aims to address some of the key challenges that remain open in the current landscape of DL-based histopathology [14].

Overview of the proposed pipeline for the classification of gastric histopathology images and the generation of interpretations.

 Methods Dataset

The development and evaluation of the proposed model was based on the use of the GasHisSDB dataset, which contains 245,196 histopathology image patches, categorized as Abnormal (97,076 patches) and Normal (148,120 patches) [10]. The dataset was constructed through a two-stage process. Initially, 600 high-resolution $(2048 \times 2048)$ gastric tissue images were annotated as Normal or Abnormal by four pathologists at Longhua Hospital. These images were then partitioned into sub-images of varying resolutions by biomedical researchers at Northeastern University and further verified by two expert pathologists from Liaoning Cancer Hospital, ensuring label consistency and diagnostic accuracy. Within the framework of the present study, the $120 \times 120$ pixel resolution was selected, corresponding to a subset of 65,261 image patches, with the aim to strike a balance between preserving sufficient morphological information and ensuring computational efficiency.

 Proposed CNN Model

Model Architecture: The proposed model's architecture was based on a custom 8-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), consisting of four convolutional blocks. Each block contained two Conv2D layers with ReLU activation and $3 \times 3$ kernels, followed by Batch Normalization, and a MaxPooling2D operation. The number of filters doubled with each block (from N to 8N), enabling progressive feature abstraction. During training, Dropout was applied after each block with progressively increasing rates (0.25 to 0.4) to prevent overfitting as the network deepened.

The resulting feature maps were flattened and passed through two fully connected (Dense) layers with 512 and 256 neurons, each followed by Batch Normalization and Dropout (applied during training). The final classification layer was a single neuron with sigmoid activation, producing a probability for classifying an image patch as abnormal. This architecture was lightweight yet expressive, leveraging hierarchical feature extraction to capture cancerrelevant patterns. To ensure convergence and generalization, the model integrated regularization techniques (Dropout, Batch Normalization) and was trained using the Adam optimizer.

Hyperparameter Tuning: Hyperparameter optimization was performed using Grid Search over a predefined parameter space. The grid featured the number of filters [32, 64], kernel size [(3,3), (5,5)], dropout rate [0.3, 0.5], and learning rate [$0.001,0.0001$]. Each configuration was evaluated on a fixed validation set.

 Uncertainty Estimation

The Monte Carlo Dropout (MC Dropout) method was applied for quantifying prediction uncertainty. After training, each test image was passed through the model 50 times with dropout enabled during inference, producing a distribution of stochastic outputs. The mean of the predicted probabilities from these forward passes was used as the final classification output: \begin{equation*} \mathbb{E}_{q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)}\left(y^{*}\right) \approx \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat{y}^{*}\left(x^{*}, W_{1}^{(t)}, \ldots, W_{L}^{(t)}\right) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\mathbb{E}_{q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)}\left(y^{*}\right)$ is the expected prediction for the input $x^{*}$ under the predictive distribution $q\left(y^{*} \mid x^{*}\right)$, representing the mean prediction, $T$ is the number of stochastic forward passes through the model, $\hat{y}^{*}\left(x^{*}, \mathbf{W}_{1}^{(t)}, \ldots, \mathbf{W}_{L}^{(t)}\right)$ is the model's predicted output during the $t$-th pass, given input $x^{*}$ and a specific set of sampled weights.

The standard deviation of predictions across forward passes served as a measure of uncertainty: \begin{equation*} \operatorname{Var}\left(y^{*}\right) \approx \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T}\left(\hat{y}_{t}^{*}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat{y}_{t}^{*}\right)^{2} \tag{2} \end{equation*} where $\operatorname{Var}(y)$ represents the predictive variance of the model for a new input $x$, quantifying the uncertainty in its predictions, and $\hat{y}_{t}^{*}$ corresponds to the scalar prediction generated at each pass.

 Interpretability Using Grad-CAM

Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), a post-hoc visualization method widely used for explaining the spatial reasoning of CNNs, was deployed for generating interpretations of the model's predictions [12]. Grad-CAM identifies the spatial regions of an input image that contribute most significantly to a model's decision, offering visual explanations without requiring architectural modifications or retraining. The method operates by computing the gradients of the predicted class score $y^{c}$ with respect to the feature maps $A^{k}$ from a chosen network layer. These gradients are then globally averaged to produce importance weights $\alpha_{k}^{c}$ for class $c$ and channel $k$: \begin{equation*} \alpha_{k}^{c}=\frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \frac{\partial y^{c}}{\partial A_{i j}^{k}} \tag{3} \end{equation*} where $Z$ is the number of spatial locations. The final GradCAM heatmap is computed by taking a weighted combination of the feature maps, followed by a ReLU activation: \begin{equation*} L_{\text{Grad}-\text{CAM}}^{c}=\operatorname{ReLU}\left(\sum_{k} \alpha_{k}^{c} A^{k}\right) \tag{4} \end{equation*}

This heatmap is then resized to the original image dimensions and overlaid on the input image to visualize which spatial regions most influenced the model's output.

In the present study, Grad-CAM allowed for qualitative validation of the CNN's focus areas in histopathology images, verifying whether the network based its decisions clinically relevant regions. It should be noted that Grad-CAM's effectiveness is influenced by the choice of nvolutional layer, as early layers capture lower-level itures (e.g., texture, structural) offering less semantic ormation, while deeper layers may sacrifice spatial olution but provide more semantic meaning. In this idy, the last convolutional layer was selected with the n of focusing on high-level semantic patterns associated th tissue-level abnormalities.

 Feature Analysis with HistomicsTK

To further explore the morphological characteristics of age regions highlighted by Grad-CAM, the HistomicsTK rary [15] was utilized, which is an open-source toolkit - processing whole-slide images. After applying color rmalization (Reinhard) and hematoxylin extraction via in deconvolution, nuclear segmentation was performed ing a multi-scale Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) detector. r each segmented nucleus, morphometric features (area, entricity, perimeter), Fourier Shape Descriptors (FSD), dient-based features (edge sharpness), and Haralick ure features (contrast and correlation from the gray-level occurrence matrix-GLCM) were extracted.

Focusing on the interpretation of Grad-CAM results for; positive predictions, nuclei were categorized as vated (located within Grad-CAM activation regions) or -activated (located outside activation regions), and their acted feature distributions were compared. This analysis vided insight into whether the model's focus aligned with logically suspicious or diagnostically relevant regions, ngthening the biological interpretability of proposed del's decisions.

 Results And Discussion Evaluation Framework

To reliably assess the model's generalization ability, a tified 5-fold cross-validation scheme was employed, ntaining the original class distribution across all folds. In h iteration, four folds were used for training, and one; held out for testing. A further $75 \%-25 \%$ stratified split the training data created a validation set within each ation of the cross-validation scheme. All hyperameters were fixed across folds for consistency. After hyper-parameters' optimization and evaluation through cross-validation, a final evaluation was conducted using a 90-10 n-test split. Model performance was assessed using a prehensive set of metrics: Accuracy (Acc), Sensitivity ns), Specificity (Spec), Precision (Prec), F1-Score, Area der the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC), tthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) [16], and the ıden index.

 Hypeparameters ' Tuning

Following Grid Search, the identified optimal figuration included 64 filters, $3 \times 3$ kernels, 0.5 dropout, and 0.0001 learning rate, which provided the best le-off between training stability, generalization, and uracy. The use of smaller filters enabled finer feature action, while higher dropout and lower learning rate imized overfitting. Model training was conducted for up 5 epochs, with EarlyStopping based on validation loss. ige preprocessing was performed using ImageData terator, applying pixel rescaling ($1. / 255$) and generatored data loading for training and validation. A decision shold of 0.5397 was determined using Youden's J istic on the ROC curve, by selecting the cutoff that ximized the difference between true positive rate and e positive rate on the validation set.

 Evaluation of the Model's Performance

Table I summarizes the obtained performance of the proposed model using 5 -fold stratified cross-validation. The model achieved balanced and strong discrimination performance, with an average AUC of $98.08 \% \pm 3.02 \%$, Sensitivity of $89.15 \% \pm 3.30 \%$ and Specificity of $90.65 \% \pm 2.96 \%$. The observed MCC of $79.19 \% \pm 6.28 \%$ demonstrated a robust correlation between predicted and actual labels, while the F1-score of $87.25 \% \pm 3.80 \%$ reflected a good balance between precision and recall. These results suggest that the model generalized well across folds and maintained consistent behavior in distinguishing abnormal from normal tissue patches. These findings further confirmed the model's effectiveness when deployed on unseen data. Furthermore, when compared to state-of-the-art models trained and evaluated on the GasHisSDB dataset, including MCAM [6], a Gaussian-mixture-based Naïve Bayes framework [17], and a hybrid model combining DenseNet-201 deep features with a Random Forest classifier [18], which achieved accuracy scores of $99.60 \%, 98.47 \%$, and 91.93 %, respectively, the proposed CNN model demonstrated competitive performance, yielding a comparable accuracy of 94.12 % despite its simpler architecture. Uncertainty Estimation

To visually represent the uncertainty of the model on the test set, a histogram of standard deviation values from MC Dropout predictions was generated (Figure 2). The distribution revealed that in the vast majority of cases, the model exhibited low uncertainty, which indicated high confidence in its predictions. As expected, predictions with high uncertainty were associated with output probabilities near the decision threshold of 0.5397, reflecting the model's lack of confidence in class assignment. Capturing these cases of high-uncertainty predictions could serve as an alert mechanism for human review, ensuring that critical or ambiguous cases are not overlooked.

 Interpretability Grad-CAM Visualization:

Within the context of the Grad-CAM analysis, two different visualisations were generated for each test image with the aim of shedding light on specific regions influencing the model's decisions: (i) the normalized Grad-CAM heatmap, representing the raw activations of the final convolutional layer and (ii) a colormapped overlay, where the heatmap was projected onto the original input image using a semi-transparent JET colormap.

Figure 3 depicts two examples of selected true positive (TP) cases alongside their corresponding heatmaps and color-mapped overlays. Visual inspection of the obtained heatmaps shows that Grad-CAM activations were systematically concentrated on biologically meaningful cellular structures, particularly dark-stained, morphologically atypical nuclei or densely packed nuclei.

In contrast, regions of background or healthy tissue exhibited minimal activation, demonstrating that the model effectively filtered out irrelevant regions. Overall, the obtained results indicated the model's ability to focus on morphological features commonly associated with abnormal tissue regions, such as nuclear atypia, increased cellular density, and nuclear hyperchromasia.

 Nuclei-Level Interpretation with HistomicsTK:

To quantitatively link Grad-CAM activation regions with biological entities and validate the model's decision-making process through measurable nuclear associations, activation maps were integrated with nuclear segmentation masks generated using HistomicsTK. This enabled not only visual comparison but also precise spatial correlation between activation maps and segmented nuclei, allowing for more detailed biological interpretation. Each Grad-CAM heatmap was converted into a binary activation mask using a threshold of 0.5 and each nucleus (uniquely labeled in the segmentation mask) was evaluated for overlap with Grad-CAM activated regions. Nuclei intersecting with activated regions were labeled as “activated.”

Original image samples (left), grad-cam heatmaps (middle), and color-mapped overlays (right) for the true positive samples 01902 and 12012.

Table III summarizes the total number of segmented nuclei, the number of activated nuclei, and the percentage of nuclei overlapping with Grad-CAM activations. The proportion of activated nuclei ranged from 30 % to 60 %, with the TP case “Abnormal-12012” exhibiting the highest overlap (60 %). This variation suggested heterogeneity in how consistently the model's attention aligned with nuclear structures across different cases.

Figure 4 depicts representative visualizations for the two examples of selected TP cases, including the original image, the segmented nuclei, and the Grad-CAM heatmap overlay. For both samples, Grad-CAM heatmaps showed concentrated activations in regions densely populated with morphologically atypical nuclei. The segmentation masks confirmed that these activated regions corresponded to areas of potential diagnostic relevance, such as irregularly shaped, hyperchromatic nuclei. The fact that for the two examined cases, nearly half or more of the nuclei, as shown in Table III, fell within activated regions, strengthened confidence in the interpretability and clinical relevance of the CNN's learned representations. Thus, the combination of heatmaps and nuclei masks demonstrated that the model's decision-making was not arbitrarily distributed across the image but rather focused on biologically meaningful regions. Feature Analysis with HistomicsTK:

To further explore the influence of activated nuclei on the model's decisions, quantitative features extracted for each nucleus using HistomicsTK were combined with Grad-CAM activation data towards examining whether activated nuclei exhibited distinct characteristics compared to non-activated ones. For each segmented nucleus, features from the following categories were considered: (i) morphometric, (ii) Fourier Shape Descriptors (FSD), (iii) gradient-based, and (iv) Haralick texture. To compare feature distributions between activated and non-activated nuclei, the Mann-Whitney U test was applied independently to each feature [19] and the obtained p-values were recorded.

Original image samples (left), segmentation masks (middle), and grad-cam heatmaps (right) for the true positive samples 01902 and 12012.

Table IV presents representative features from each category that exhibited statistically significant differences (p -value $<0.05$) between Grad-CAM activated and non-activated nuclei. Among the morphometric features, both area and perimeter were significantly larger in activated nuclei (e.g., Abnormal-12012) suggesting that the model tended to focus on larger nuclei structures. FSD features (e.g., FSD4 and FSD5 in Abnormal-16643) also showed statistically significant separation, highlighting the model's sensitivity to subtle variations in nuclear boundary complexity. Regarding gradient-based features, standard deviation and kurtosis of the gradient magnitude (e.g., Abnormal-01902) both presented statistically significant differences between activated and non-activated nuclei, indicating the model's focus on sharper transitions and localized structural variation. Texture-based Haralick features, including contrast range and correlation mean, highlighted heterogeneity in chromatin texture and spatial relationships within the nucleus as discriminative characteristics. These findings provided evidence regarding the alignment between Grad-CAM activations and observed differences in nuclear morphology and texture.

 Limitations and Future Work

Potential limitations of the proposed approach include the patch-level data splitting inherent to the GasHisSDB dataset, which consists of $120 \times 120$ image patches extracted from whole-slide images. This design may have introduced data leakage, potentially inflating the reported performance metrics. Moreover, the dataset represents a limited subset of gastric histopathology collected under specific staining and imaging conditions, which may restrict generalizability to data from other institutions or protocols. Future work will focus on large-scale validation across more diverse gastric cancer subtypes and imaging conditions, as well as on independent, multi-institutional cohorts to assess real-world applicability. Extending the approach to other histopathological domains (e.g., colorectal, lung, or breast cancer) may further demonstrate its adaptability and generalizability. Conclusion

This study demonstrated the effectiveness of a relatively lightweight CNN model in the classification of gastric cancer histopathological images. Despite the absence of very deep or pretrained architectures, the model achieved performance levels comparable to state-of-the-art approaches and demonstrated its ability to provide predictions with high confidence. The combination of Grad-CAM and HistomicsTK enabled both visual localization of discriminative image regions and quantitative analysis of nuclear morphology, and provided evidence regarding the biological relevance of the model's decisions. This integrative approach not only improves transparency and trust in deep learning systems but also brings AI one step closer to clinical applicability in digital pathology.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a cornerstone of medical diagnostics, yet its long acquisition times present significant clinical challenges. Compressed Sensing (CS) and Deep Learning (DL) have emerged as the two leading paradigms for accelerating MRI by reconstructing images from undersampled k -space data. While large-scale DL models are often considered state-of-the-art, the profound impact of the k-space sampling strategy on reconstruction quality is not always fully interrogated. This research directly investigates this interplay by evaluating a classical Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) against a custom-developed, baseline U-Net CNN across three diverse public MRI datasets. We test four distinct undersampling masks-from standard Cartesian to advanced variable-density and edge-enhanced patterns. Our findings reveal a compelling and nuanced outcome: the ISTA algorithm, when paired with a physics-informed sampling mask, consistently and significantly outperforms our CNN baseline, particularly in a "zero-shot" or data-limited training scenario. On a benchmark knee dataset, ISTA achieved a PSNR over 40 dB, a result the CNN failed to approach. This work concludes that the strategic design of the data acquisition process is a foundational, and in some cases dominant, factor in the success of accelerated MRI, demonstrating that a mathematically-grounded iterative method can surpass a baseline deep learning model when the underlying physics are respected.

 Compressed Sensing MRI Deep Learning Image Reconstruction K-Space Sampling Iterative Algorithms Introduction

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an indispensable tool in modern medicine, offering unparalleled soft-tissue contrast without ionizing radiation. Its primary limitation, however, is the slow data acquisition process, which can lead to patient discomfort, motion artifacts, and limited patient throughput [1]. To address this, researchers have focused on accelerating MRI by acquiring fewer data points (undersampling) in the MRI signal domain, known as k -space, and then computationally reconstructing a diagnostic-quality image.

Compressed Sensing (CS) marked a significant breakthrough in MRI reconstruction by demonstrating that images possessing sparse representations in certain transform domains can be accurately recovered from far fewer k -space samples than required by the Nyquist theorem [2], [3]. This insight allowed for substantial acceleration in MRI acquisition, with the added benefit of reducing scan times and motion artifacts. The fundamental principle of CS involves formulating an optimization problem that strikes a balance between two competing goals: ensuring consistency with the acquired undersampled k -space data, and promoting sparsity in the reconstructed image. To solve this inverse problem, iterative algorithms such as the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) became widely adopted [4]. These algorithms alternate between gradient descent steps that enforce data consistency and shrinkage (thresholding) steps that impose sparsity constraints. Despite their conceptual simplicity, ISTA and its variants have proven to be highly effective and remain a cornerstone in classical CS-based MRI reconstruction techniques.

In recent years, deep learning (DL) has emerged as a transformative force in the field of MRI reconstruction, particularly through the use of convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures such as U-Net [5]. These models are designed to learn an end-to-end mapping from undersampled k -space data to high-quality, fully-sampled images. By leveraging the ability of deep networks to model complex, nonlinear relationships, U-Net-based approaches have demonstrated exceptional capability in suppressing artifacts and restoring fine anatomical details lost during aggressive undersampling.

This rapid progress has been significantly driven by the availability of large-scale, high-quality datasets such as fastMRI [6], which provide thousands of paired undersampled and fully sampled MRI scans. These datasets have enabled researchers to train and validate deep learning models at scale, resulting in impressive improvements in reconstruction accuracy, speed, and generalizability across anatomies and imaging protocols.

However, the performance of any reconstruction algorithm is intrinsically tied to the quality of the undersampled data. The design of the k -space sampling mask, which dictates which data points are acquired, is a critical variable. Advanced, physics-informed strategies like variable-density sampling, which prioritize the information-rich center of k-space,

have been shown to be highly effective [7]. This raises a crucial question: how does the choice of sampling pattern influence the performance comparison between classical and DL methods? This study tackles this question by systematically evaluating both ISTA and a CNN across a spectrum of sampling masks, from naive to intelligent, to determine whether an optimized acquisition strategy can allow a classical algorithm to rival a modern deep learning approach.

This work explores the comparative performance of classical versus deep learning reconstruction algorithms under various k -space sampling patterns. We hypothesize that a physics-informed sampling strategy can enable a traditional iterative method to remain highly competitive with, and in datalimited contexts even surpass, a representative baseline deep learning model. To test this, we evaluate four undersampling masks-1D Cartesian, 2D random, variable-density polynomial, and edge-enhanced-using both ISTA with wavelet sparsity and a custom U-Net CNN. Our experiments span three diverse datasets (OASIS-1, UC Berkeley Knee Dataset, and fastMRI Brain Dataset) to ensure robust and generalizable insights into the crucial relationship between sampling physics and reconstruction algorithm design.

 Background and Related Work Classical Compressed Sensing Mri

The Compressed Sensing Magnetic Resonance Imaging (CS-MRI) framework addresses the problem of reconstructing a high-quality image $\mathbf{x}$ from a set of undersampled k -space measurements $\mathbf{y}$. In this context, the measurement model is given by: \begin{equation*} y=A x \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\mathbf{A}$ is a linear operator that encapsulates both the discrete Fourier transform (which models the k -space acquisition in MRI) and a binary sampling mask that selects only a subset of Fourier coefficients, effectively reducing the amount of data acquired. The goal is to recover $\mathbf{x}$ faithfully despite this data reduction.

Since direct inversion is ill-posed due to undersampling, CSMRI casts image reconstruction as an optimization problem that balances fidelity to the observed data with a regularization term that encodes prior knowledge about the image structure. This is typically formulated as the following unconstrained minimization: \begin{equation*} \hat{\mathbf{x}}=\arg \min _{\times} \frac{1}{2}\vert\mathbf{A x}-\mathbf{y}\vert_{2}^{2}+\lambda R(\mathbf{x}). \tag{1} \end{equation*}

The first term, $\frac{1}{2}\vert\mathbf{A x}-\mathbf{y}\vert Z$, is the data fidelity term, ensuring that the reconstructed image is consistent with the measured k -space data. The second term, $\lambda R(\mathbf{x})$, is the regularization term that imposes prior assumptions about the structure or sparsity of the image. The parameter $\lambda>0$ controls the tradeoff between these two objectives.

A common and effective choice of regularizer is the $\ell_{1}$ norm of the image coefficients in a transform domain: \begin{equation*} R(\mathbf{x})=\vert\boldmath{\Psi} \mathbf{x}\vert_{1} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\boldmath{\Psi}$ is a sparsifying transform, such as the Wavelet transform [1] or Total Variation (TV) operator [8]. This choice encourages sparsity, promoting solutions where many transform coefficients are zero or near-zero, which aligns with the compressibility of natural images in these domains.

To solve the optimization problem in (1), iterative algorithms are commonly used. One widely adopted method is the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA), which alternates between:

A gradient descent step on the data fidelity term to enforce consistency with measurements, and

A proximal (soft-thresholding) operation on the regularization term to enforce sparsity.

An accelerated version, known as FISTA (Fast ISTA), improves convergence speed by incorporating a momentum term. Both methods fall under the class of proximal gradient methods, which are well-suited for problems involving non-smooth regularizers like the $\ell_{1}$ norm.

Deep Learning for Mri Reconstruction

DL-based methods replace the handcrafted regularizer $R(\mathbf{x})$ with a powerful prior learned from a large training dataset. Many successful approaches utilize a U-Net architecture to learn a mapping from an aliased, zero-filled input image to a clean output [5]. More advanced techniques, known as physics-informed or model-based DL, unroll the iterations of a classical algorithm like ISTA into network layers. These "variational networks" or "unrolled networks" learn custom filter banks and activation functions in place of the fixed sparsity transform and thresholding, combining the robust structure of iterative methods with the representative power of deep learning [9].

 K-Space Sampling Strategies

The design of the sampling mask $\mathbf{M}$, an integral component of the sensing matrix $\mathbf{A}$ plays a pivotal role in the success of CS-MRI reconstruction. The sampling mask determines which k-space coefficients are acquired during the scan, and its structure directly influences the quality and fidelity of the reconstructed image.

A key principle in compressed sensing is the requirement of incoherent sampling, which ensures that aliasing artifacts introduced by undersampling are distributed in a noise-like fashion across the image domain. This incoherence allows the sparse regularization to effectively suppress such artifacts. Fully random 2D sampling patterns typically achieve high incoherence; however, they are often impractical to implement on clinical MRI scanners due to hardware constramts and acquisition inefficiencies.

To address these practical limitations, structured sampling masks are used. One of the most popular and effective strategies is variable-density sampling, wherein the central region of k -space (containing low-frequency components) is sampled densely, while the outer regions (containing high-frequency components) are sampled more sparsely [10]. This design aligns with the energy distribution in typical MR images,

where most of the image's energy is concentrated in the lowfrequency components.

In recent years, there has been growing interest in datadriven approaches to sampling pattern design. These methods aim to learn optimal sampling masks tailored to specific anatomies or reconstruction algorithms, using supervised or reinforcement learning paradigms. By adapting the sampling strategy to the characteristics of the target images and the downstream reconstruction pipeline, these learned masks can significantly enhance image quality, reduce artifacts, and shorten acquisition time [11].

 Methodology

Our experiments were implemented in Python, utilizing libraries such as PyTorch for model development, NumPy for numerical computations, Scikit-Image for image processing, and PyWavelets for wavelet-based transformations. The study followed a three-stage methodology: (i) data preprocessing and augmentation, (ii) formulation of experimental setups including sampling and reconstruction strategies, and (iii) quantitative and qualitative evaluation of reconstruction performance.

Datasets

We utilized three public datasets to ensure our conclusions were robust across different anatomies, contrasts, and data formats:

OASIS-1: T1-weighted brain MRI scans [12]. We extracted central axial 2D slices for analysis.

UC Berkeley Knee Dataset: High-resolution 3D knee scans with raw k-space data [13]. We used this to test performance on a high-SNR musculoskeletal case.

fastMRI Brain Dataset: Multi-coil raw brain k-space data [6], representing a state-of-the-art benchmark.

For all datasets, a single-channel ground truth image was derived (via RSS for multi-coil data) and normalized to [0, $1]$. The fully-sampled k -space was then simulated via a 2D FFT.

Undersampling Masks

We designed four k-space masks for a fixed acceleration factor of $\mathrm{R}=4$.

1D Cartesian: Uniformly skips $k$-space lines.

2D Random: Randomly samples individual points.

Variable-Density: Samples based on a polynomial probability distribution, heavily favoring the k -space center.

Edge-Enhanced: An anatomy-aware mask whose sampling probability is guided by the Fourier transform of a Sobel edge map of the image, prioritizing highfrequency detail.

Reconstruction Models

ISTA: Our classical baseline was an ISTA implementation solving (1) with a Daubechies 4 ('db4') wavelet prior. Following hyperparameter tuning (Experiment 1), we used 10 iterations and a regularization parameter $\lambda=0.0001$ for all comparative tests.

$C N N$: Our deep learning model was a lightweight U-Net architecture with an encoder-decoder structure, skip connections, and three downsampling levels. This architecture was chosen as a representative baseline for image-to-image translation tasks, rather than a state-of-the-art, heavily optimized model. It was trained using the Adam optimizer ($\mathrm{l}=5 \mathrm{e}-5$) and an MSE loss function. For the single-image UCB test, it was trained in a "zero-shot" fashion for 500 epochs [14]. For the fastMRI experiment, it was trained on 40 slices and tested on 10 unseen slices.

Evaluation

Reconstruction quality was quantitatively assessed using two widely adopted metrics: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), which measures the overall fidelity of the reconstructed image relative to the fully-sampled ground truth, and the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), which evaluates perceptual similarity by accounting for luminance, contrast, and structural information.

 Results

The series of experiments conducted provides a comprehensive evaluation of different sampling and reconstruction strategies across multiple datasets. In this section, we first present the results of hyperparameter tuning, followed by a detailed comparative analysis of the different reconstruction methods, supported by both quantitative metrics and qualitative visual assessments.

Hyperparameter Tuning for Ista

To establish a fair and robust baseline for the classical iterative method, we first optimized the key hyperparameters for the ISTA algorithm: the regularization parameter ($\lambda$) and the number of iterations. The initial tuning was performed using the OASIS-1 dataset, as detailed in Experiment 1.

The investigation into $\lambda$ consistently revealed that peak performance in both PSNR and SSIM was achieved with a very small regularization weight. As shown in the convergence analysis (Fig. 1), the algorithm's performance plateaued quickly, with significant gains observed within the first 10-15 iterations and minimal improvement thereafter.

To ensure the generalizability of these findings, the lambda tuning was replicated for both the UC Berkeley and fastMRI datasets, as shown in Fig. 2. Across all three distinct datasets, the trend remained the same: optimal reconstruction quality was consistently found at a low lambda value around $\lambda=0.0001$. This robust result across different anatomies and imaging contrasts gave us high confidence in our parameter selection. Therefore, for all subsequent comparative experiments, we used a fixed, near-optimal configuration of $\lambda=0.0001$ and a computationally efficient $\mathbf{1 0}$ iterations to ensure a fair and consistent baseline for the ISTA method.

 Comparative Analysis of Sampling Masks

A core objective of this study was to assess the impact of different k-space sampling masks on reconstruction quality.

 Ista Vs. Cnn: a Surprising Outcome

The final set of experiments (Experiments 9 and 10) compared the performance of the optimized ISTA framework against our custom-trained U-Net-based CNN. The analysis was conducted using the high-performing Variable Density mask.

Table III presents the results for the UCB knee dataset (Sec_128.mag). The findings are striking: the classical ISTA algorithm significantly outperformed the deep learning model. ISTA achieved a PSNR of 40.93 dB, which was not only superior to the CNN's 31.86 dB but was also very close to the zero-filled reconstruction quality, indicating the effectiveness of the sampling mask itself. The CNN, in this "zero-shot" training scenario, failed to improve upon the high-quality baseline and introduced smoothing artifacts, as seen in Fig. 4. Discussion

Our findings strongly suggest that the success of accelerated MRI is deeply rooted in the physics of data acquisition. The dramatic performance increase from using Variable Density and Edge-Enhanced masks indicates that capturing the most significant components of the k -space signal is the single most important step. When the sampling is intelligent, the subsequent reconstruction problem becomes far more well-posed, allowing even a simple, mathematically-defined algorithm like ISTA to excel.

Interpreting the Ista Vs. Cnn Performance

The superior performance of ISTA over our custom CNN in the single-image reconstruction task is particularly insightful and warrants careful contextualization. While large-scale, pretrained deep learning models have shown remarkable success [6], our experiment simulated a "zero-shot" or subject-specific scenario where the model must learn from scratch on a single instance. In this data-limited context, the CNN, despite its architectural complexity, appeared to learn a sub-optimal mapping that resulted in image smoothing and loss of fine texture.

This suggests that without the benefit of a large and diverse training set, the network's learned prior may be less effective than the explicit wavelet sparsity prior enforced by ISTA. The ISTA, grounded in a well-posed mathematical framework, leverages the intrinsic properties of the MR signal itself, which proves highly effective when the sampling is intelligent. This outcome aligns with an emerging area of research in physicsinformed deep learning, which seeks to integrate known

 Limitations and Clinical Implications

While this study provides valuable insights, it is crucial to acknowledge its limitations, as highlighted by our reviewers.

CNN Baseline and Training: The U-Net model used is a baseline and not a state-of-the-art architecture. Modern reconstruction networks, such as unrolled networks [9], are often significantly deeper and are trained on thousands of scans. The "zero-shot" training on the UCB dataset is a specific and challenging scenario; performance would almost certainly improve with a larger training corpus. Thus, our conclusion is not that ISTA is universally better than all deep learning, but that it can be superior to a baseline CNN in data-limited contexts.

Computational Time and Practicality: This study did not focus on a detailed analysis of computational tradeoffs. The CNN's training time (approx. 3 minutes) is substantial for a single slice, making it impractical for on-the-fly clinical use in a zero-shot setting. In contrast, ISTA requires no training and completed reconstruction in under a second. While a pre-trained CNN offers nearinstantaneous inference, the cost of training and the need for large, curated datasets are significant practical hurdles for clinical deployment that classical methods do not face.

Simulated Data and Coil Combination: Our k-space data was primarily simulated from reconstructed images or simplified via RSS coil combination. This idealized setup does not account for real-world factors like physiological noise, system imperfections, and complex coil sensitivities, which could affect the performance of both algorithms differently.

These limitations frame the practical implications of our work. For clinical settings where large, curated training datasets are unavailable or for novel imaging sequences, a classical CS approach with an optimized, physics-informed sampling pattern remains a highly effective and reliable strategy for MRI acceleration.

Conclusion

This research provides a comprehensive analysis of the factors influencing image reconstruction quality in accelerated Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Specifically, it reveals that the strategy used for sampling the k -space-the frequency domain where MRI data is acquired-plays a more crucial role in determining the final image quality than the reconstruction algorithm itself. In a direct comparison, a straightforward classical iterative method, the Iterative ShrinkageThresholding Algorithm (ISTA), when paired with a carefully designed physics-informed sampling mask, consistently and significantly outperformed a more complex deep learning model based on a custom U-Net convolutional neural network (CNN). This outcome highlights a key insight: optimizing the data acquisition process, particularly the k -space sampling pattern, can yield greater improvements in image quality than simply switching to advanced machine learning techniques.

These findings emphasize the enduring relevance and power of principled, model-based approaches in medical imaging. While deep learning has shown remarkable promise, this study suggests that even modest classical algorithms can excel when combined with thoughtfully engineered sampling strategies. Therefore, future research should prioritize the development of hybrid methods that integrate the interpretability and theoretical guarantees of classical reconstruction techniques with the adaptive capacity and representational power of deep neural networks [16]. Additionally, there is significant potential in leveraging artificial intelligence to automatically learn optimal, anatomy-specific sampling masks, which could further enhance image quality and acquisition efficiency [17].
