Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a cornerstone of medical diagnostics, yet its long acquisition times present significant clinical challenges. Compressed Sensing (CS) and Deep Learning (DL) have emerged as the two leading paradigms for accelerating MRI by reconstructing images from undersampled k -space data. While large-scale DL models are often considered state-of-the-art, the profound impact of the k-space sampling strategy on reconstruction quality is not always fully interrogated. This research directly investigates this interplay by evaluating a classical Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) against a custom-developed, baseline U-Net CNN across three diverse public MRI datasets. We test four distinct undersampling masks-from standard Cartesian to advanced variable-density and edge-enhanced patterns. Our findings reveal a compelling and nuanced outcome: the ISTA algorithm, when paired with a physics-informed sampling mask, consistently and significantly outperforms our CNN baseline, particularly in a “zero-shot” or data-limited training scenario. On a benchmark knee dataset, ISTA achieved a PSNR over 40 dB, a result the CNN failed to approach. This work concludes that the strategic design of the data acquisition process is a foundational, and in some cases dominant, factor in the success of accelerated MRI, demonstrating that a mathematically-grounded iterative method can surpass a baseline deep learning model when the underlying physics are respected.

 Compressed Sensing MRI Deep Learning Image Reconstruction K-Space Sampling Iterative Algorithms Introduction

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an indispensable tool in modern medicine, offering unparalleled soft-tissue contrast without ionizing radiation. Its primary limitation, however, is the slow data acquisition process, which can lead to patient discomfort, motion artifacts, and limited patient throughput [1]. To address this, researchers have focused on accelerating MRI by acquiring fewer data points (undersampling) in the MRI signal domain, known as k -space, and then computationally reconstructing a diagnostic-quality image.

Compressed Sensing (CS) marked a significant break-through in MRI reconstruction by demonstrating that images possessing sparse representations in certain transform domains can be accurately recovered from far fewer k -space samples than required by the Nyquist theorem [2], [3]. This insight allowed for substantial acceleration in MRI acquisition, with the added benefit of reducing scan times and motion artifacts. The fundamental principle of CS involves formulating an optimization problem that strikes a balance between two competing goals: ensuring consistency with the acquired undersampled k -space data, and promoting sparsity in the reconstructed image. To solve this inverse problem, iterative algorithms such as the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) became widely adopted [4]. These algorithms alternate between gradient descent steps that enforce data consistency and shrinkage (thresholding) steps that impose sparsity constraints. Despite their conceptual simplicity, ISTA and its variants have proven to be highly effective and remain a cornerstone in classical CS-based MRI reconstruction techniques.

In recent years, deep learning (DL) has emerged as a transformative force in the field of MRI reconstruction, particularly through the use of convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures such as U-Net [5]. These models are designed to learn an end-to-end mapping from undersampled k -space data to high-quality, fully-sampled images. By leveraging the ability of deep networks to model complex, nonlinear relationships, U-Net-based approaches have demonstrated exceptional capability in suppressing artifacts and restoring fine anatomical details lost during aggressive undersampling.

This rapid progress has been significantly driven by the availability of large-scale, high-quality datasets such as fastMRI [6], which provide thousands of paired undersampled and fully sampled MRI scans. These datasets have enabled researchers to train and validate deep learning models at scale, resulting in impressive improvements in reconstruction accuracy, speed, and generalizability across anatomies and imaging protocols.

However, the performance of any reconstruction algorithm is intrinsically tied to the quality of the undersampled data. The design of the k -space sampling mask, which dictates which data points are acquired, is a critical variable. Advanced, physics-informed strategies like variable-density sampling, which prioritize the information-rich center of k-space, have been shown to be highly effective [7]. This raises a crucial question: how does the choice of sampling pattern influence the performance comparison between classical and DL methods? This study tackles this question by systematically evaluating both ISTA and a CNN across a spectrum of sampling masks, from naive to intelligent, to determine whether an optimized acquisition strategy can allow a classical algorithm to rival a modern deep learning approach.

This work explores the comparative performance of classical versus deep learning reconstruction algorithms under various k -space sampling patterns. We hypothesize that a physics-informed sampling strategy can enable a traditional iterative method to remain highly competitive with, and in data-limited contexts even surpass, a representative baseline deep learning model. To test this, we evaluate four undersampling masks-1D Cartesian, 2D random, variable-density polynomial, and edge-enhanced-using both ISTA with wavelet sparsity and a custom U-Net CNN. Our experiments span three diverse datasets (OASIS-1, UC Berkeley Knee Dataset, and fastMRI Brain Dataset) to ensure robust and generalizable insights into the crucial relationship between sampling physics and reconstruction algorithm design.

 Background and Related Work Classical Compressed Sensing Mri

The Compressed Sensing Magnetic Resonance Imaging (CS-MRI) framework addresses the problem of reconstructing a high-quality image $\mathbf{x}$ from a set of undersampled k -space measurements $\mathbf{y}$. In this context, the measurement model is given by: \begin{equation*} y=A x \end{equation*} where $\mathbf{A}$ is a linear operator that encapsulates both the discrete Fourier transform (which models the k -space acquisition in MRI) and a binary sampling mask that selects only a subset of Fourier coefficients, effectively reducing the amount of data acquired. The goal is to recover $\mathbf{x}$ faithfully despite this data reduction.

Since direct inversion is ill-posed due to undersampling, CSMRI casts image reconstruction as an optimization problem that balances fidelity to the observed data with a regularization term that encodes prior knowledge about the image structure. This is typically formulated as the following unconstrained minimization: \begin{equation*} \hat{\mathbf{x}}=\arg \min _{\times} \frac{1}{2}\vert\mathbf{A x}-\mathbf{y}\vert_{2}^{2}+\lambda R(\mathbf{x}). \tag{1} \end{equation*}

The first term, $\frac{1}{2}\vert\mathbf{A x}-\mathbf{y}\vert Z$, is the data fidelity term, ensuring that the reconstructed image is consistent with the measured k -space data. The second term, $\lambda R(\mathbf{x})$, is the regularization term that imposes prior assumptions about the structure or sparsity of the image. The parameter $\lambda>0$ controls the tradeoff between these two objectives.

A common and effective choice of regularizer is the $\ell_{1}$ norm of the image coefficients in a transform domain: \begin{equation*} R(\mathbf{x})=\vert\boldmath{\Psi} \mathbf{x}\vert_{1} \end{equation*} where $\boldmath{\Psi}$ is a sparsifying transform, such as the Wavelet transform [1] or Total Variation (TV) operator [8]. This choice encourages sparsity, promoting solutions where many transform coefficients are zero or near-zero, which aligns with the compressibility of natural images in these domains.

To solve the optimization problem in (1), iterative algorithms are commonly used. One widely adopted method is the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA), which alternates between:

A gradient descent step on the data fidelity term to enforce consistency with measurements, and

A proximal (soft-thresholding) operation on the regularization term to enforce sparsity.

An accelerated version, known as FISTA (Fast ISTA), improves convergence speed by incorporating a momentum term. Both methods fall under the class of proximal gradient methods, which are well-suited for problems involving non-smooth regularizers like the $\ell_{1}$ norm.

 Deep Learning for Mri Reconstruction

DL-based methods replace the handcrafted regularizer $R(\mathbf{x})$ with a powerful prior learned from a large training dataset. Many successful approaches utilize a U-Net architecture to learn a mapping from an aliased, zero-filled input image to a clean output [5]. More advanced techniques, known as physics-informed or model-based DL, unroll the iterations of a classical algorithm like ISTA into network layers. These “variational networks” or “unrolled networks” learn custom filter banks and activation functions in place of the fixed sparsity transform and thresholding, combining the robust structure of iterative methods with the representative power of deep learning [9].

 K-Space Sampling Strategies

The design of the sampling mask $\mathbf{M}$, an integral component of the sensing matrix $\mathbf{A}$ plays a pivotal role in the success of CS-MRI reconstruction. The sampling mask determines which k-space coefficients are acquired during the scan, and its structure directly influences the quality and fidelity of the reconstructed image.

A key principle in compressed sensing is the requirement of incoherent sampling, which ensures that aliasing artifacts introduced by undersampling are distributed in a noise-like fashion across the image domain. This incoherence allows the sparse regularization to effectively suppress such artifacts. Fully random 2D sampling patterns typically achieve high incoherence; however, they are often impractical to implement on clinical MRI scanners due to hardware constramts and acquisition inefficiencies.

To address these practical limitations, structured sampling masks are used. One of the most popular and effective strategies is variable-density sampling, wherein the central region of k -space (containing low-frequency components) is sampled densely, while the outer regions (containing high-frequency components) are sampled more sparsely [10]. This design aligns with the energy distribution in typical MR images, where most of the image's energy is concentrated in the low-frequency components.

In recent years, there has been growing interest in data-driven approaches to sampling pattern design. These methods aim to learn optimal sampling masks tailored to specific anatomies or reconstruction algorithms, using supervised or reinforcement learning paradigms. By adapting the sampling strategy to the characteristics of the target images and the downstream reconstruction pipeline, these learned masks can significantly enhance image quality, reduce artifacts, and shorten acquisition time [11].

 Methodology

Our experiments were implemented in Python, utilizing libraries such as PyTorch for model development, NumPy for numerical computations, Scikit-Image for image processing, and PyWavelets for wavelet-based transformations. The study followed a three-stage methodology: (i) data preprocessing and augmentation, (ii) formulation of experimental setups including sampling and reconstruction strategies, and (iii) quantitative and qualitative evaluation of reconstruction performance.

Datasets

We utilized three public datasets to ensure our conclusions were robust across different anatomies, contrasts, and data formats:

OASIS-1: T1-weighted brain MRI scans [12]. We extracted central axial 2D slices for analysis.

UC Berkeley Knee Dataset: High-resolution 3D knee scans with raw k-space data [13]. We used this to test performance on a high-SNR musculoskeletal case.

fastMRI Brain Dataset: Multi-coil raw brain k-space data [6], representing a state-of-the-art benchmark.

For all datasets, a single-channel ground truth image was derived (via RSS for multi-coil data) and normalized to [0, 1]. The fully-sampled k -space was then simulated via a 2D FFT.

 Undersampling Masks

We designed four k-space masks for a fixed acceleration factor of $\mathrm{R}=4$.

1D Cartesian: Uniformly skips $k$-space lines.

2D Random: Randomly samples individual points.

Variable-Density: Samples based on a polynomial probability distribution, heavily favoring the k -space center.

Edge-Enhanced: An anatomy-aware mask whose sampling probability is guided by the Fourier transform of a Sobel edge map of the image, prioritizing highfrequency detail.

Reconstruction Models

ISTA: Our classical baseline was an ISTA implementation solving (1) with a Daubechies 4 ('db4') wavelet prior. Following hyperparameter tuning (Experiment 1), we used 10 iterations and a regularization parameter $\lambda=0.0001$ for all comparative tests.

CNN: Our deep learning model was a lightweight U-Net architecture with an encoder-decoder structure, skip connections, and three downsampling levels. This architecture was chosen as a representative baseline for image-to-image translation tasks, rather than a state-of-the-art, heavily optimized model. It was trained using the Adam optimizer ($\mathrm{l}=5 \mathrm{e}-5$) and an MSE loss function. For the single-image UCB test, it was trained in a “zero-shot” fashion for 500 epochs [14]. For the fastMRI experiment, it was trained on 40 slices and tested on 10 unseen slices.

 Evaluation

Reconstruction quality was quantitatively assessed using two widely adopted metrics: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), which measures the overall fidelity of the reconstructed image relative to the fully-sampled ground truth, and the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), which evaluates perceptual similarity by accounting for luminance, contrast, and structural information.

 Results

The series of experiments conducted provides a comprehensive evaluation of different sampling and reconstruction strategies across multiple datasets. In this section, we first present the results of hyperparameter tuning, followed by a detailed comparative analysis of the different reconstruction methods, supported by both quantitative metrics and qualitative visual assessments.

Hyperparameter Tuning for Ista

To establish a fair and robust baseline for the classical iterative method, we first optimized the key hyperparameters for the ISTA algorithm: the regularization parameter ($\lambda$) and the number of iterations. The initial tuning was performed using the OASIS-1 dataset, as detailed in Experiment 1.

The investigation into $\lambda$ consistently revealed that peak performance in both PSNR and SSIM was achieved with a very small regularization weight. As shown in the convergence analysis (Fig. 1), the algorithm's performance plateaued quickly, with significant gains observed within the first 10-15 iterations and minimal improvement thereafter.

To ensure the generalizability of these findings, the lambda tuning was replicated for both the UC Berkeley and fastMRI datasets, as shown in Fig. 2. Across all three distinct datasets, the trend remained the same: optimal reconstruction quality was consistently found at a low lambda value around $\lambda=0.0001$. This robust result across different anatomies and imaging contrasts gave us high confidence in our parameter selection. Therefore, for all subsequent comparative experiments, we used a fixed, near-optimal configuration of $\lambda=0.0001$ and a computationally efficient 10 iterations to ensure a fair and consistent baseline for the ISTA method.

 Comparative Analysis of Sampling Masks

A core objective of this study was to assess the impact of different k-space sampling masks on reconstruction quality. Experiments were conducted across all three datasets using a fixed acceleration factor of $\mathrm{R}=4$. The results consistently demonstrated the superiority of intelligent, non-uniform sampling strategies.

Convergence analysis on the oasis-1 dataset, showing that ista performance (psnr) plateaus after approximately 15 iterations. a choice of 10 iterations provides a strong balance of performance and computational efficiency.

Table I summarizes the findings from Experiment 7 on the OASIS-1 dataset, where multiple subjects and slices were processed. The Variable Density and Edge-Enhanced masks substantially outperformed the standard Cartesian and 2D Random masks for the ISTA reconstruction, yielding an average PSNR improvement of over 8 dB. Similar trends were observed on the UC Berkeley Knee Dataset (Experiment 6, Table II) and the fastMRI Brain Dataset (Experiment 8). In every case, the Variable Density and Edge-Enhanced masks delivered significantly higher PSNR and SSIM values for both the zero-filled and the final ISTA reconstructions. Qualitative results, as visualized in Fig. 3, corroborate these metrics. The images reconstructed from Cartesian and 2D Random masks exhibit significant aliasing and loss of detail, whereas those from Variable Density and Edge-Enhanced masks retain sharp anatomical features and high fidelity to the ground truth.

 Ista Vs. Cnn: a Surprising Outcome

The final set of experiments (Experiments 9 and 10) compared the performance of the optimized ISTA framework against our custom-trained U-Net-based CNN. The analysis was conducted using the high-performing Variable Density mask.

Table III presents the results for the UCB knee dataset (Sec_128.mag). The findings are striking: the classical ISTA algorithm significantly outperformed the deep learning model. ISTA achieved a PSNR of 40.93 dB, which was not only superior to the CNN's 31.86 dB but was also very close to the zero-filled reconstruction quality, indicating the effectiveness of the sampling mask itself. The CNN, in this “zero-shot” training scenario, failed to improve upon the high-quality baseline and introduced smoothing artifacts, as seen in Fig. 4. Discussion

Our findings strongly suggest that the success of accelerated MRI is deeply rooted in the physics of data acquisition. The dramatic performance increase from using Variable Density and Edge-Enhanced masks indicates that capturing the most significant components of the k -space signal is the single most important step. When the sampling is intelligent, the subsequent reconstruction problem becomes far more well-posed, allowing even a simple, mathematically-defined algorithm like ISTA to excel.

Interpreting the Ista Vs. Cnn Performance

The superior performance of ISTA over our custom CNN in the single-image reconstruction task is particularly insightful and warrants careful contextualization. While large-scale, pretrained deep learning models have shown remarkable success [6], our experiment simulated a “zero-shot” or subject-specific scenario where the model must learn from scratch on a single instance. In this data-limited context, the CNN, despite its architectural complexity, appeared to learn a sub-optimal mapping that resulted in image smoothing and loss of fine texture.

This suggests that without the benefit of a large and diverse training set, the network's learned prior may be less effective than the explicit wavelet sparsity prior enforced by ISTA. The ISTA, grounded in a well-posed mathematical framework, leverages the intrinsic properties of the MR signal itself, which proves highly effective when the sampling is intelligent. This outcome aligns with an emerging area of research in physics-informed deep learning, which seeks to integrate known physical models into neural networks to improve their data efficiency and robustness [15]. Our work empirically supports the idea that in the absence of big data, a good physical model can be more powerful than a generic learning architecture.

Validation of the regularization parameter $\lambda$ for ista was performed with an acceleration factor of $r=4$. results from both the uc berkeley dataset (a) and the fastmri dataset (b) demonstrate that the best reconstruction quality is obtained at relatively low values of $\lambda$. these findings corroborate our initial observations and support the selection of $\lambda=0.0001$ for all subsequent comparative experiments.

A visual comparison of reconstruction quality under different undersampling masks is presented for a representative brain slice from the oasis-1 dataset at an acceleration factor of $r=4$. the variable density and edge-enhanced masks yield visibly sharper images with improved structural detail preservation, outperforming both cartesian and fully random sampling schemes.

Reconstruction of the ucb knee image. (a) ground truth. (b) zero-filled. (c) ista reconstruction, visually identical to the ground truth. (d) cnn reconstruction, exhibiting noticeable blurring and loss of texture.

 Limitations and Clinical Implications

While this study provides valuable insights, it is crucial to acknowledge its limitations, as highlighted by our reviewers.

CNN Baseline and Training: The U-Net model used is a baseline and not a state-of-the-art architecture. Modern reconstruction networks, such as unrolled networks [9], are often significantly deeper and are trained on thousands of scans. The “zero-shot” training on the UCB dataset is a specific and challenging scenario; performance would almost certainly improve with a larger training corpus. Thus, our conclusion is not that ISTA is universally better than all deep learning, but that it can be superior to a baseline CNN in data-limited contexts.

Computational Time and Practicality: This study did not focus on a detailed analysis of computational tradeoffs. The CNN's training time (approx. 3 minutes) is substantial for a single slice, making it impractical for on-the-fly clinical use in a zero-shot setting. In contrast, ISTA requires no training and completed reconstruction in under a second. While a pre-trained CNN offers nearinstantaneous inference, the cost of training and the need for large, curated datasets are significant practical hurdles for clinical deployment that classical methods do not face.

Simulated Data and Coil Combination: Our k-space data was primarily simulated from reconstructed images or simplified via RSS coil combination. This idealized setup does not account for real-world factors like physiological noise, system imperfections, and complex coil sensitivities, which could affect the performance of both algorithms differently.

These limitations frame the practical implications of our work. For clinical settings where large, curated training datasets are unavailable or for novel imaging sequences, a classical CS approach with an optimized, physics-informed sampling pattern remains a highly effective and reliable strategy for MRI acceleration.

 Conclusion

This research provides a comprehensive analysis of the factors influencing image reconstruction quality in accelerated Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Specifically, it reveals that the strategy used for sampling the k -space-the frequency domain where MRI data is acquired-plays a more crucial role in determining the final image quality than the reconstruction algorithm itself. In a direct comparison, a straightforward classical iterative method, the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA), when paired with a carefully designed physics-informed sampling mask, consistently and significantly outperformed a more complex deep learning model based on a custom U-Net convolutional neural network (CNN). This outcome highlights a key insight: optimizing the data acquisition process, particularly the k -space sampling pattern, can yield greater improvements in image quality than simply switching to advanced machine learning techniques.

These findings emphasize the enduring relevance and power of principled, model-based approaches in medical imaging. While deep learning has shown remarkable promise, this study suggests that even modest classical algorithms can excel when combined with thoughtfully engineered sampling strategies. Therefore, future research should prioritize the development of hybrid methods that integrate the interpretability and theoretical guarantees of classical reconstruction techniques with the adaptive capacity and representational power of deep neural networks [16]. Additionally, there is significant potential in leveraging artificial intelligence to automatically learn optimal, anatomy-specific sampling masks, which could further enhance image quality and acquisition efficiency [17].

This paper presents a novel approach to inventory management by applying compartmental models based on differential equations across all three stages of the inventory lifecycle: raw materials, work in progress (WIP), and finished goods. Unlike previous studies that focus primarily on raw materials, our research provides a comprehensive framework for managing the entire inventory system. The proposed model effectively predicts inventory dynamics and optimizes costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. This model enables more robust decision making in inventory management by unifying control theory and machine learning. It holds potential for realtime implementation in supply chains and can be extended to stochastic environments, perishable goods, and multi-echelon systems.

 Inventory Management Compartmental Models Optimal Control Artificial Neural Networks Introduction

Inventory management is a critical component of supply chain operations, significantly influencing organizational performance and profitability [2], [19]. Traditional inventory management methods often emphasize individual stages in isolation, rather than adopting a holistic view of the entire system [20]. This research proposes a comprehensive framework that models all three key stages of inventory, raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods, using compartmental models formulated through differential equations, combined with artificial intelligence techniques.

Originally developed in the field of epidemiology [1], compartmental modeling has demonstrated considerable potential in supply chain applications as well [18]. Recent advancements in neural networks and optimal control theory have enabled the development of more sophisticated approaches for inventory optimization [16]. Building upon these developments, our study addresses several gaps in the existing literature by integrating control-theoretic and machine learning perspectives.

Specifically, our approach leverages Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [14] in conjunction with neural networks to derive optimal inventory control strategies. While prior research has primarily applied Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to the management of raw material inventory [8], our work extends the application of PINNs to all stages of the inventory lifecycle. This broader scope offers a more unified and effective method for managing inventory across the supply chain.

The key contributions of this work include:

A novel compartmental model framework that captures the dynamics of all three inventory stages simultaneously.

Integration of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle with neural networks for inventory optimization.

Comprehensive numerical validation demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the mathematical formulation of our compartmental inventory model. Section 3 details the optimization framework and Pontryagin's approach. Section 4 describes our numerical methods and presents results. Finally, Section 5 concludes with implications and future research directions.

Model Formulation

Our research introduces an Artificial Neural Network (ANN)-based Decision Support System for optimizing inventory management through compartmental modeling. This approach treats inventory as a system of interconnected stocks whose dynamics are governed by differential equations, similar to models used in epidemiological studies [4] but adapted for supply chain applications [6].

Inventory model.

Figure 1 illustrates a three-stage inventory flow system commonly found in manufacturing and supply chain operations.

Raw Materials: These are the basic inputs required for production. Procurement decisions (governed by control variable $\lambda_{r}(t)$ determines how much raw material is added to the system.

Work in Progress (WIP) Raw materials are processed through manufacturing operations to become intermediate products. Transformation rates govern this stage, $\mu_{R}$\\

and $\mu_{W}$. Depicted by interconnected gears, symbolizing machinery and production complexity.

Finished Goods The final products are ready for customer delivery or sale. The sales or dispatch rate is controlled by $\lambda_{r}(t)$.

Inventory of Raw Materials

Raw materials (R) are modeled as a stock that decreases through consumption in production and increases through procurement. The dynamics are governed by: \begin{equation*} \frac{d R(t)}{d t}=\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{r}(t) R(t) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\lambda_{r}(t)$ represents the procurement rate of raw materials, $\mu_{r}(t)$ denotes the rate at which raw materials are consumed and transferred to WIP. This formulation extends traditional economic order quantity models [7] by incorporating timevarying rates.

 Inventory of Work-in-Progress (Wip)

Work-in-Progress (W) represents items undergoing transformation but not yet completed. Its dynamics are modeled by: \begin{equation*} \frac{d W(t)}{d t}=\mu_{r}(t) R(t)-\mu_{w}(t) W(t) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\mu_{r}(t) R(t)$ is the rate at which raw materials are converted into WIP, $\mu_{w}(t) W(t)$ represents the rate at which WIP is processed into finished goods. This approach aligns with recent work on production-inventory systems [9].

 Inventory of Finished Goods

Finished Goods (F) represent completed products ready for distribution. Their dynamics follow: \begin{equation*} \frac{d F(t)}{d t}=\mu_{w}(t) W(t)-\lambda_{f}(t) F(t) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\mu_{w}(t) W(t)$ is the rate at which WIP is converted into finished goods, $\lambda_{f}(t)$ represents the rate at which finished goods are sold or dispatched. This formulation extends traditional inventory models [13] by incorporating time-dependent demand patterns.

 Optimization Problem

Our optimization framework is inspired from Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [11] to minimize total inventory costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. The model incorporates three primary cost components.

Holding cost: The cost of storing inventory over time is defined as: \begin{equation*} C_{u}=\int_{0}^{T}\left(C_{R} R(t)+C_{W} W(t)+C_{F} F(t)\right) d t \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $C_{R}, C_{W}$, and $C_{F}$ represent the holding costs per unit of raw materials, WIP, and finished goods, respectively.

Procurement Cost: The expense of acquiring raw materials is given by: \begin{equation*} C_{p}=\int_{0}^{T} K_{R} \lambda_{r}(t) d t \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $K_{R}$ represents the procurement cost per unit of raw materials

Production Cost: The expenses incurred during manufacturing are defined as: \begin{equation*} C_{s}=\int_{0}^{T}\left(K_{W} \mu_{w}(t) W(t)\right) d t \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $K_{W}$ is the production cost per unit of WIP.

The following differential equations govern the system dynamics: \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} \frac{d R(t)}{d t}=\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{r}(t) R(t) \\ \frac{d W(t)}{d t}=\mu_{r}(t) R(t)-\mu_{w}(t) W(t) \\ \frac{d F(t)}{d t}=\mu_{w}(t) W(t)-\lambda_{f}(t) F(t) \end{gathered} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Non-Negativity Constraints All variables must be nonnegative: \begin{equation*} R(t), W(t), F(t), \lambda_{r}(t), \mu_{r}(t), \mu_{w}(t), \lambda_{f}(t) \geq 0 \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Objective Function The goal is to minimize total cost: \begin{equation*} C_{t o t a l}=H_{t o t a l}+P_{1}+P_{2} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $H_{\text {total }}$ is total holding cost, $P_{1}$ is procurement cost, and $P_{2}$ is production cost.

Expression for Total Cost The total cost is expressed as: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} C_{\text {total }}= & \int_{0}^{T}\left(h_{R} \cdot R(t)+h_{W} \cdot W(t)+h_{F} \cdot F(t)\right. \\ & \left.+c_{R} \cdot \lambda_{r}(t)+c_{P} \cdot \mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)\right) d t \end{aligned} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

We apply the Pontryagin's Maximum Principle [3] assuming constant rates for $\mu_{R}, \mu_{W}$, and $\lambda_{F}$ to simplify the control problem while maintaining the integrity of the model. Here, the control variables are $\lambda_{r}(t), \mu_{r}(t), \mu_{w}(t), \lambda_{f}(t)$, and we assume that $h_{R}, h_{W}, h_{F}$ are constant.

Hamiltonian Construction: The Hamiltonian incorporates the total cost and state dynamics: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} H= & h_{R} \cdot R(t)+h_{W} \cdot W(t)+h_{F} \cdot F(t)+c_{R} \cdot \lambda_{r}(t) \\ & +c_{P} \cdot \mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)+\psi_{R}(t) \cdot\left(\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{R} \cdot R(t)\right) \\ & +\psi_{W}(t) \cdot\left(\mu_{R} \cdot R(t)-\mu_{W} \cdot W(t)\right) \\ & +\psi_{F}(t) \cdot\left(\mu_{W} \cdot W(t)-\lambda_{F} \cdot F(t)\right) \end{aligned} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Optimality Conditions: In the context of this inventory management problem, the Hamiltonian function H represents the total instantaneous cost of the system, combining both the direct costs (such as inventory holding and procurement) and the impact of the system's dynamics through the co-state variables. It is a central component of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle used to derive the optimal control policy. This function encapsulates the system's objectives and constraints, and it is used to derive the necessary conditions for optimality, including the co-state equations and optimal control laws. The optimal control $\lambda_{r}^{*}(t), \mu_{r}^{*}(t), \mu_{w}^{*}(t), \lambda_{f}^{*}(t)$ minimizes the Hamiltonian: \begin{equation*} u^{*}(t)=\arg \min _{u(t)} H(x(t), u(t), \psi(t), t) \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Co-state Equations The co-state variables evolve according to: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} \frac{d \psi_{R}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial R(t)} \\ \frac{d \psi_{W}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial W(t)} \\ \frac{d \psi_{F}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial F(t)} \end{aligned} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

In the framework of optimal control theory, particularly under Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, the co-state equations govern the evolution of the adjoint variables, also known as Lagrange multipliers or co-state variables. These variables are denoted as $\psi_{R}(t), \psi_{W}(t)$, and $\psi_{F}(t)$ are introduced to account for the influence of the state variables (e.g., inventory levels of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods) on the total cost function. In practical terms, a co-state variable represents the shadow price or sensitivity of the system, it quantifies how much the total cost would change in response to a small variation in the corresponding state variable.

Boundary Conditions The final boundary conditions for co-state variables are: \begin{equation*} \psi_{R}(T)=0, \psi_{W}(T)=0, \psi_{F}(T)=0 \tag{1} \end{equation*}

 Parameter Estimation

We employed a range of methods to analyze and validate our inventory management system, drawing on recent advances in scientific machine learning [12]. Specifically, we implemented the Runge-Kutta method [5] with the following parameters:

Initial time: $t_{0}=0.0$

Initial raw materials: $R_{0}=100$

Initial work-in-progress (WIP): $W_{0}=40$

Initial finished goods: $F_{0}=10$

Time step: $h=0.1$

Number of steps: $n=100$

Figure 2 presents the numerical simulation results of the system dynamics using the Runge-Kutta method. It illustrates the evolution over time of the three main inventory components:

$R(t)$ - Raw Materials (Blue Curve): Initially starts at a high level and decreases over time due to consumption in the production process.

$W(t)$ - Work-in-Progress (Orange Curve): Increases initially as raw materials are converted into intermediate

Solution of the system of inventory management equations using the runge-kutta method.

products. It eventually stabilizes, indicating a balance between incoming and outgoing flow.

$F(t)$ - Finished Goods (Green Curve): Grows gradually as WIP is processed into final products ready for sale.

This simulation validates the system's dynamic behavior governed by the differential equations.

We also employed a neural network architecture consisting of three hidden layers, each with 20 neurons and tanh activation functions, following the design proposed in [10]. The parameters used in the model were set as follows:

$\lambda_{r}(t)=1.0$

$\mu_{r}(t)=0.1$

$\mu_{w}(t)=0.05$

$\lambda_{f}(t)=0.02$

Solution of the inventory management system using physics-informed neural networks (pinns)

The system reaches steady state behavior after a short time, where all inventory levels become stable as shown in Figure 3. PINNs successfully capture the dynamics without requiring large amounts of labeled data. The inventory of raw materials starts at zero and steadily increases, eventually saturating. This suggests the model learns to procure raw materials at a growing but stabilizing rate, as shown in Figure 4. The WIP

Temporal dynamics of inventory levels in the raw material, work-inprogress, and finished goods compartments.

inventory grows gradually over time, fed by the increasing raw materials and processed through internal production. Finished goods begin with negative values (due to model initialization) and increase slowly, representing the final output stage of the inventory system. The smooth and converging trends indicate that the PINN has successfully captured the dynamic inventory flow. Unlike classical methods (e.g. Runge-Kutta), PINNs do not need labeled solutions, they learn directly from the governing differential equations and constraints. These results show stability and learning of long-term behavior, making PINNs a reliable tool for complex inventory optimization systems.

 Computing Costs:

The system was simulated over a total time horizon of $T=10$ units, divided into 100 equally spaced time points. The initial conditions were defined as: \begin{equation*} R(0)=100, \quad W(0)=0, \quad F(0)=0. \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Several cost components were analyzed following the framework presented in [15]:

Holding Cost: Calculated as the cumulative cost of holding inventory across all three components (raw materials, WIP, and finished goods).

Procurement Cost: Proportional to the rate of raw material procurement.

Production Cost: Dependent on the production rate and the level of work-in-progress.

Total Cost: Computed as the sum of all individual cost components.

The results presented in Figure 5 illustrate how inventory transitions through the various supply chain compartments over a 10 -unit time horizon. Raw materials, which begin at a high level of 100 units, decrease rapidly as a result of consumption in the production process. This sharp decline reflects an intensive procurement-to-processing phase with no replenishment.

Starting from zero, the work-in-progress (WIP) inventory increases rapidly as raw materials are converted into intermediate products, reaching a peak around $t=2.5$. Thereafter, it declines steadily as the WIP is transformed into finished

Inventory dynamics across various supply chain compartments are observed over a 10 -unit time horizon.

Temporal dynamics of major cost components in the inventory management system under optimal control strategies.

goods. Finished goods inventory also starts at zero and gradually accumulates as production output increases. The red curve, which represents finished goods, peaks near $t=4.5$ and subsequently decreases as the rate of sales or depletion exceeds the rate of production.

Figure 6 illustrates the time evolution of key cost components in the inventory management system governed by optimal control strategies.

Holding Costs: Represent the cost of storing raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. These costs peak around $t=2$, indicating maximum inventory levels, and then gradually decline as inventory decreases over time.

Procurement Costs: Associated with the rate of acquisition of raw materials, $\lambda_{r}(t)$. These costs start high, then drop, and stabilize, reflecting an efficient procurement strategy implemented early in the time horizon.

Production Costs: Dependent on the rate of WIP transformation, modeled as $\mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)$. These costs increase rapidly, peaking around $t=1.5$, and then slowly decline. This captures the dynamics of the production effort over time.

Total Costs: Computed as the sum of all three components:

Total Cost = Holding Cost + Procurement Cost + Production Cost.

Initially, the system experiences high total costs due to rapid procurement and production ramp-up. Over time, costs decrease as inventory is optimally depleted and production activities taper off. This trend validates the effectiveness of the applied optimal control strategies, demonstrating successful cost minimization over the time horizon.

Evolution of cost components over successive replenishment cycles in a periodic inventory policy framework.

Periodic Inventory Replenishment Function We implemented a periodic replenishment function: \begin{equation*} \operatorname{Replenishment}(t)= \begin{cases}\text { rate }, & \text { if }(t \bmod \text { period })=0 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Inventory Trajectories

We modeled the three-compartment inventory system with periodic raw material replenishment (pulses) following the approach in [17]: \begin{equation*} \lambda_{r}(t)= \begin{cases}\lambda_{r}, & \text { if }(t \text { mod cycle time })<\text { pulse duration } \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Parameters included: Cycle time: 5 units, Pulse duration: 0.1 units, Raw material input rate $\lambda_{r}$: 100 units per cycle, Conversion rates $\mu_{r}, \mu_{w}, \mu_{x}: 0.8,0.8$, and 1.2, respectively Cyclic Restocking: Every 5 units of time, the system restocks raw materials, visible as sharp jumps in the blue line as shown in the Figure 8, while this periodic replenishment triggers a cascade of production across compartments.

Exponential Depletion: After each restock, raw materials are consumed first, decreasing exponentially. This drives work-in-progress (WIP), which also depletes gradually. Finished goods accumulate last, then reduce as sales happen. The three compartments follow first-order decay curves between restocks. Inventory flows maintain synchrony, showing a stable conversion process:

Raw Materials $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{r}} \quad$ Work in Progress (WIP) $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{w}}$ Finished Goods.

This system mimics batch manufacturing or periodic procurement, useful for: seasonal industries, production with scheduled delivery, and optimized warehouse space usage. The results highlight the effectiveness of using optimal control $\lambda_{r}$, $\mu_{r}, m u_{w}$, and $\lambda_{f}$ to minimize cost, maintain service levels, and prevent overstock or stockout.

The Dispatch Rate is constrained by actual market demand as shown in Figure 9. The Raw Materials and WIP inventories

adapt to maintain production continuity. Inventory accumulation or depletion is influenced by the time-varying demand. This simulation demonstrates a realistic production-dispatch scenario, where the inventory levels are regulated dynamically to match fluctuating demand while minimizing holding costs and avoiding overproduction.

 Conclusion

This study introduced a novel AI-augmented optimal control framework for inventory management, addressing the dynamic interactions among raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. Simulation results revealed that our proposed method effectively stabilizes inventory trajectories, even under constraints like periodic replenishment and variable demand. The approach achieved superior cost efficiency compared to traditional methods, demonstrating strong generalization in approximating optimal state and control functions.

This work bridges the gap between classical control theory and modern AI techniques, offering a scalable framework for real-time inventory optimization. Future extensions may include incorporating uncertainty, multi-echelon networks, perishability, and stochastic demand to enhance applicability in complex supply chain environments. Future research directions include:

Extension to multi-echelon supply chain networks

Incorporation of stochastic demand patterns

Application to perishable inventory systems

Real-world validation with industrial partners

This paper presents a novel approach to inventory management by applying compartmental models based on differential equations across all three stages of the inventory lifecycle: raw materials, work in progress (WIP), and finished goods. Unlike previous studies that focus primarily on raw materials, our research provides a comprehensive framework for managing the entire inventory system. The proposed model effectively predicts inventory dynamics and optimizes costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. This model enables more robust decision making in inventory management by unifying control theory and machine learning. It holds potential for realtime implementation in supply chains and can be extended to stochastic environments, perishable goods, and multi-echelon systems.

 Inventory Management Compartmental Models Optimal Control Artificial Neural Networks Introduction

Inventory management is a critical component of supply chain operations, significantly influencing organizational performance and profitability [2], [19]. Traditional inventory management methods often emphasize individual stages in isolation, rather than adopting a holistic view of the entire system [20]. This research proposes a comprehensive framework that models all three key stages of inventory, raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods, using compartmental models formulated through differential equations, combined with artificial intelligence techniques.

Originally developed in the field of epidemiology [1], compartmental modeling has demonstrated considerable potential in supply chain applications as well [18]. Recent advancements in neural networks and optimal control theory have enabled the development of more sophisticated approaches for inventory optimization [16]. Building upon these developments, our study addresses several gaps in the existing literature by integrating control-theoretic and machine learning perspectives.

Specifically, our approach leverages Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [14] in conjunction with neural networks to derive optimal inventory control strategies. While prior research has primarily applied Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to the management of raw material inventory [8], our work extends the application of PINNs to all stages of the inventory lifecycle. This broader scope offers a more unified and effective method for managing inventory across the supply chain.

The key contributions of this work include:

A novel compartmental model framework that captures the dynamics of all three inventory stages simultaneously.

Integration of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle with neural networks for inventory optimization.

Comprehensive numerical validation demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the mathematical formulation of our compartmental inventory model. Section 3 details the optimization framework and Pontryagin's approach. Section 4 describes our numerical methods and presents results. Finally, Section 5 concludes with implications and future research directions.

 Model Formulation

Our research introduces an Artificial Neural Network (ANN)-based Decision Support System for optimizing inventory management through compartmental modeling. This approach treats inventory as a system of interconnected stocks whose dynamics are governed by differential equations, similar to models used in epidemiological studies [4] but adapted for supply chain applications [6].

Inventory model.

Figure 1 illustrates a three-stage inventory flow system commonly found in manufacturing and supply chain operations.

Raw Materials: These are the basic inputs required for production. Procurement decisions (governed by control variable $\lambda_{r}(t)$ determines how much raw material is added to the system.

Work in Progress (WIP) Raw materials are processed through manufacturing operations to become intermediate products. Transformation rates govern this stage, $\mu_{R}$ and $\mu_{W}$. Depicted by interconnected gears, symbolizing machinery and production complexity.

Finished Goods The final products are ready for customer delivery or sale. The sales or dispatch rate is controlled by $\lambda_{r}(t)$.

Inventory of Raw Materials

Raw materials (R) are modeled as a stock that decreases through consumption in production and increases through procurement. The dynamics are governed by: \begin{equation*} \frac{d R(t)}{d t}=\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{r}(t) R(t) \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $\lambda_{r}(t)$ represents the procurement rate of raw materials, $\mu_{r}(t)$ denotes the rate at which raw materials are consumed and transferred to WIP. This formulation extends traditional economic order quantity models [7] by incorporating time-varying rates.

 Inventory of Work-in-Progress (Wip)

Work-in-Progress (W) represents items undergoing transformation but not yet completed. Its dynamics are modeled by: \begin{equation*} \frac{d W(t)}{d t}=\mu_{r}(t) R(t)-\mu_{w}(t) W(t) \tag{2} \end{equation*} where $\mu_{r}(t) R(t)$ is the rate at which raw materials are converted into WIP, $\mu_{w}(t) W(t)$ represents the rate at which WIP is processed into finished goods. This approach aligns with recent work on production-inventory systems [9].

 Inventory of Finished Goods

Finished Goods (F) represent completed products ready for distribution. Their dynamics follow: \begin{equation*} \frac{d F(t)}{d t}=\mu_{w}(t) W(t)-\lambda_{f}(t) F(t) \tag{3} \end{equation*} where $\mu_{w}(t) W(t)$ is the rate at which WIP is converted into finished goods, $\lambda_{f}(t)$ represents the rate at which finished goods are sold or dispatched. This formulation extends traditional inventory models [13] by incorporating time-dependent demand patterns.

 Optimization Problem

Our optimization framework is inspired from Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [11] to minimize total inventory costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. The model incorporates three primary cost components.

Holding cost: The cost of storing inventory over time is defined as: \begin{equation*} C_{u}=\int_{0}^{T}\left(C_{R} R(t)+C_{W} W(t)+C_{F} F(t)\right) d t \tag{4} \end{equation*} where $C_{R}, C_{W}$, and $C_{F}$ represent the holding costs per unit of raw materials, WIP, and finished goods, respectively.

Procurement Cost: The expense of acquiring raw materials is given by: \begin{equation*} C_{p}=\int_{0}^{T} K_{R} \lambda_{r}(t) d t \tag{5} \end{equation*} where $K_{R}$ represents the procurement cost per unit of raw materials

Production Cost: The expenses incurred during manufacturing are defined as: \begin{equation*} C_{s}=\int_{0}^{T}\left(K_{W} \mu_{w}(t) W(t)\right) d t \tag{6} \end{equation*} where $K_{W}$ is the production cost per unit of WIP.

The following differential equations govern the system dynamics: \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} \frac{d R(t)}{d t}=\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{r}(t) R(t) \tag{7}\\ \frac{d W(t)}{d t}=\mu_{r}(t) R(t)-\mu_{w}(t) W(t) \tag{8}\\ \frac{d F(t)}{d t}=\mu_{w}(t) W(t)-\lambda_{f}(t) F(t) \end{gathered} \tag{9} \end{equation*}

Non-Negativity Constraints All variables must be non-negative: \begin{equation*} R(t), W(t), F(t), \lambda_{r}(t), \mu_{r}(t), \mu_{w}(t), \lambda_{f}(t) \geq 0 \tag{10} \end{equation*}

Objective Function The goal is to minimize total cost: \begin{equation*} C_{t o t a l}=H_{t o t a l}+P_{1}+P_{2} \tag{11} \end{equation*} where $H_{\text {total }}$ is total holding cost, $P_{1}$ is procurement cost, and $P_{2}$ is production cost.

Expression for Total Cost The total cost is expressed as: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} C_{\text {total }}= & \int_{0}^{T}\left(h_{R} \cdot R(t)+h_{W} \cdot W(t)+h_{F} \cdot F(t)\right. \\ & \left.+c_{R} \cdot \lambda_{r}(t)+c_{P} \cdot \mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)\right) d t \end{aligned} \tag{12} \end{equation*}

We apply the Pontryagin's Maximum Principle [3] assuming constant rates for $\mu_{R}, \mu_{W}$, and $\lambda_{F}$ to simplify the control problem while maintaining the integrity of the model. Here, the control variables are $\lambda_{r}(t), \mu_{r}(t), \mu_{w}(t), \lambda_{f}(t)$, and we assume that $h_{R}, h_{W}, h_{F}$ are constant.

Hamiltonian Construction: The Hamiltonian incorporates the total cost and state dynamics: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} H= & h_{R} \cdot R(t)+h_{W} \cdot W(t)+h_{F} \cdot F(t)+c_{R} \cdot \lambda_{r}(t) \\ & +c_{P} \cdot \mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)+\psi_{R}(t) \cdot\left(\lambda_{r}(t)-\mu_{R} \cdot R(t)\right) \\ & +\psi_{W}(t) \cdot\left(\mu_{R} \cdot R(t)-\mu_{W} \cdot W(t)\right) \\ & +\psi_{F}(t) \cdot\left(\mu_{W} \cdot W(t)-\lambda_{F} \cdot F(t)\right) \end{aligned} \tag{13} \end{equation*}

Optimality Conditions: In the context of this inventory management problem, the Hamiltonian function H represents the total instantaneous cost of the system, combining both the direct costs (such as inventory holding and procurement) and the impact of the system's dynamics through the co-state variables. It is a central component of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle used to derive the optimal control policy. This function encapsulates the system's objectives and constraints, and it is used to derive the necessary conditions for optimality, including the co-state equations and optimal control laws. The optimal control $\lambda_{r}^{*}(t), \mu_{r}^{*}(t), \mu_{w}^{*}(t), \lambda_{f}^{*}(t)$ minimizes the Hamiltonian: \begin{equation*} u^{*}(t)=\arg \min _{u(t)} H(x(t), u(t), \psi(t), t) \tag{14} \end{equation*}

Co-state Equations The co-state variables evolve according to: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} \frac{d \psi_{R}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial R(t)} \tag{15}\\ \frac{d \psi_{W}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial W(t)} \tag{16}\\ \frac{d \psi_{F}(t)}{d t} & =-\frac{\partial H}{\partial F(t)} \end{aligned} \tag{17} \end{equation*}

In the framework of optimal control theory, particularly under Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, the co-state equations govern the evolution of the adjoint variables, also known as Lagrange multipliers or co-state variables. These variables are denoted as $\psi_{R}(t), \psi_{W}(t)$, and $\psi_{F}(t)$ are introduced to account for the influence of the state variables (e.g., inventory levels of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods) on the total cost function. In practical terms, a co-state variable represents the shadow price or sensitivity of the system, it quantifies how much the total cost would change in response to a small variation in the corresponding state variable.

Boundary Conditions The final boundary conditions for co-state variables are: \begin{equation*} \psi_{R}(T)=0, \psi_{W}(T)=0, \psi_{F}(T)=0 \tag{18} \end{equation*}

 Parameter Estimation

We employed a range of methods to analyze and validate our inventory management system, drawing on recent advances in scientific machine learning [12]. Specifically, we implemented the Runge-Kutta method [5] with the following parameters:

Initial time: $t_{0}=0.0$

Initial raw materials: $R_{0}=100$

Initial work-in-progress (WIP): $W_{0}=40$

Initial finished goods: $F_{0}=10$

Time step: $h=0.1$

Number of steps: $n=100$

Figure 2 presents the numerical simulation results of the system dynamics using the Runge-Kutta method. It illustrates the evolution over time of the three main inventory components:

$R(t)$ - Raw Materials (Blue Curve): Initially starts at a high level and decreases over time due to consumption in the production process.

$W(t)$ - Work-in-Progress (Orange Curve): Increases initially as raw materials are converted into intermediate products. It eventually stabilizes, indicating a balance between incoming and outgoing flow.

$F(t)$ - Finished Goods (Green Curve): Grows gradually as WIP is processed into final products ready for sale.

Solution of the system of inventory management equations using the runge-kutta method.

This simulation validates the system's dynamic behavior governed by the differential equations.

We also employed a neural network architecture consisting of three hidden layers, each with 20 neurons and tanh activation functions, following the design proposed in [10]. The parameters used in the model were set as follows:

$\lambda_{r}(t)=1.0$

$\mu_{r}(t)=0.1$

$\mu_{w}(t)=0.05$

$\lambda_{f}(t)=0.02$

Solution of the inventory management system using physics-informed neural networks (pinns)

The system reaches steady state behavior after a short time, where all inventory levels become stable as shown in Figure 3. PINNs successfully capture the dynamics without requiring large amounts of labeled data. The inventory of raw materials starts at zero and steadily increases, eventually saturating. This suggests the model learns to procure raw materials at a growing but stabilizing rate, as shown in Figure 4. The WIP inventory grows gradually over time, fed by the increasing raw materials and processed through internal production. Finished goods begin with negative values (due to model initialization) and increase slowly, representing the final output stage of the inventory system. The smooth and converging trends indicate that the PINN has successfully captured the dynamic inventory flow. Unlike classical methods (e.g. Runge-Kutta), PINNs do not need labeled solutions, they learn directly from the governing differential equations and constraints. These results show stability and learning of long-term behavior, making PINNs a reliable tool for complex inventory optimization systems.

Temporal dynamics of inventory levels in the raw material, work-in-progress, and finished goods compartments.

Computing Costs:

The system was simulated over a total time horizon of $T=10$ units, divided into 100 equally spaced time points. The initial conditions were defined as: \begin{equation*} R(0)=100, \quad W(0)=0, \quad F(0)=0. \end{equation*}

Several cost components were analyzed following the framework presented in [15]:

Holding Cost: Calculated as the cumulative cost of holding inventory across all three components (raw materials, WIP, and finished goods).

Procurement Cost: Proportional to the rate of raw material procurement.

Production Cost: Dependent on the production rate and the level of work-in-progress.

Total Cost: Computed as the sum of all individual cost components.

The results presented in Figure 5 illustrate how inventory transitions through the various supply chain compartments over a 10 -unit time horizon. Raw materials, which begin at a high level of 100 units, decrease rapidly as a result of consumption in the production process. This sharp decline reflects an intensive procurement-to-processing phase with no replenishment.

Starting from zero, the work-in-progress (WIP) inventory increases rapidly as raw materials are converted into intermediate products, reaching a peak around $t=2.5$. Thereafter, it declines steadily as the WIP is transformed into finished goods. Finished goods inventory also starts at zero and gradually accumulates as production output increases. The red curve, which represents finished goods, peaks near $t=4.5$ and subsequently decreases as the rate of sales or depletion exceeds the rate of production.

Inventory dynamics across various supply chain compartments are observed over a 10 -unit time horizon.

Temporal dynamics of major cost components in the inventory management system under optimal control strategies.

Figure 6 illustrates the time evolution of key cost components in the inventory management system governed by optimal control strategies.

Holding Costs: Represent the cost of storing raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. These costs peak around $t=2$, indicating maximum inventory levels, and then gradually decline as inventory decreases over time.

Procurement Costs: Associated with the rate of acquisition of raw materials, $\lambda_{r}(t)$. These costs start high, then drop, and stabilize, reflecting an efficient procurement strategy implemented early in the time horizon.

Production Costs: Dependent on the rate of WIP transformation, modeled as $\mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)$. These costs increase rapidly, peaking around $t=1.5$, and then slowly decline. This captures the dynamics of the production effort over time.

Total Costs: Computed as the sum of all three components:

Total Cost = Holding Cost + Procurement Cost + Production Cost.

Initially, the system experiences high total costs due to rapid procurement and production ramp-up. Over time, costs decrease as inventory is optimally depleted and production activities taper off. This trend validates the effectiveness of the applied optimal control strategies, demonstrating successful cost minimization over the time horizon.

Evolution of cost components over successive replenishment cycles in a periodic inventory policy framework.

Periodic Inventory Replenishment Function We implemented a periodic replenishment function: \begin{equation*} \operatorname{Replenishment}(t)= \begin{cases}\text { rate }, & \text { if }(t \bmod \text { period })=0 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \tag{19} \end{equation*}

Inventory Trajectories

We modeled the three-compartment inventory system with periodic raw material replenishment (pulses) following the approach in [17]: \begin{equation*} \lambda_{r}(t)= \begin{cases}\lambda_{r}, & \text { if }(t \text { mod cycle time })<\text { pulse duration } \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \end{equation*}

Parameters included: Cycle time: 5 units, Pulse duration: 0.1 units, Raw material input rate $\lambda_{r}$: 100 units per cycle, Conversion rates $\mu_{r}, \mu_{w}, \mu_{x}: 0.8,0.8$, and 1.2, respectively Cyclic Restocking: Every 5 units of time, the system restocks raw materials, visible as sharp jumps in the blue line as shown in the Figure 8, while this periodic replenishment triggers a cascade of production across compartments.

Exponential Depletion: After each restock, raw materials are consumed first, decreasing exponentially. This drives work-in-progress (WIP), which also depletes gradually. Finished goods accumulate last, then reduce as sales happen. The three compartments follow first-order decay curves between restocks. Inventory flows maintain synchrony, showing a stable conversion process:

Raw Materials $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{r}} \quad$ Work in Progress (WIP) $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{w}}$ Finished Goods.

This system mimics batch manufacturing or periodic procurement, useful for: seasonal industries, production with scheduled delivery, and optimized warehouse space usage. The results highlight the effectiveness of using optimal control $\lambda_{r}$, $\mu_{r}, m u_{w}$, and $\lambda_{f}$ to minimize cost, maintain service levels, and prevent overstock or stockout.

The Dispatch Rate is constrained by actual market demand as shown in Figure 9. The Raw Materials and WIP inventories adapt to maintain production continuity. Inventory accumulation or depletion is influenced by the time-varying demand. This simulation demonstrates a realistic production-dispatch scenario, where the inventory levels are regulated dynamically to match fluctuating demand while minimizing holding costs and avoiding overproduction.

Inventory dynamics with demand constraint: time evolution of raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods, and customer demand. the plot demonstrates the interaction between inventory levels and periodic demand fluctuations over time.

 Conclusion

This study introduced a novel AI-augmented optimal control framework for inventory management, addressing the dynamic interactions among raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. Simulation results revealed that our proposed method effectively stabilizes inventory trajectories, even under constraints like periodic replenishment and variable demand. The approach achieved superior cost efficiency compared to traditional methods, demonstrating strong generalization in approximating optimal state and control functions.

This work bridges the gap between classical control theory and modern AI techniques, offering a scalable framework for real-time inventory optimization. Future extensions may include incorporating uncertainty, multi-echelon networks, perishability, and stochastic demand to enhance applicability in complex supply chain environments. This paper presents a scalable framework for virtual soil moisture sensing based on time series data collected from environmental sensors installed at the Rabat site. The proposed architecture leverages a Hadoop-based data pipeline that employs MapReduce for the distributed preprocessing of high-volume sensor data. We investigate the predictive performance of two models-Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Random Forest (RF)-at different soil depths. Experimental results show that the LSTM model efficiently captures temporal patterns in the data, while the RF model demonstrates strong performance with reduced computational cost. These results confirm the effectiveness of combining deep learning and ensemble learning approaches for accurate and scalable soil moisture forecasting in smart farming applications.

 Soil Moisture Forecasting Smart Farming Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Random Forest Hadoop MapReduce Time Series Prediction Virtual Sensors \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{2299}{\ifmmode\odot\else{$\odot$}\fi} Introduction

Soil moisture plays a critical role in agriculture and hydrology, directly affecting crop development, irrigation planning, and sustainable water resource management [1]. Accurate monitoring of soil moisture is fundamental to the success of precision farming practices, where data-driven approaches enable optimized irrigation scheduling, reduced water waste, and improved crop yields. Traditional measurement techniques such as Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR) and Frequency Domain Reflectometry (FDR) provide reliable results but are costly, energy-demanding, and impractical for widespread deployment across large agricultural areas [2], [3].

In response to these limitations, virtual soil moisture sensors have emerged as a promising solution. By utilizing historical environmental data and machine learning techniques, virtual sensors estimate physical parameters without requiring extensive sensor installations [4]. Among the machine learning techniques, ensemble methods such as Random Forest (RF) have gained popularity for their robustness and generalization capabilities, particularly in handling nonlinear relationships and noisy datasets [5]–[7]. In parallel, deep learning models-especially Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) net-works-have demonstrated a strong capacity for capturing temporal dependencies in multivariate time series, achieving high accuracy in soil moisture forecasting tasks [8]–[11].

At the same time, the growing volume and velocity of sensor-generated data necessitate scalable processing infrastructures. Big data frameworks such as Hadoop provide distributed storage and computing capabilities suitable for handling large-scale environmental datasets. The MapReduce programming paradigm enables efficient and parallelizable preprocessing, which is essential for extracting features from sensor time series at scale [12]–[14]. Recent smart farming platforms increasingly incorporate such technologies to support real-time and batch analytics in Internet of Things (IoT) environments [15], [16].

Although LSTM and RF are widely used in the machine learning literature, their combined application within a scalable big data framework for soil moisture forecasting in smart farming remains underexplored. Our study contributes novel insights by operationalizing the concept of virtual sensors and evaluating model performance across multiple soil depths using real-world data from a large experimental site in Rabat, equipped with over 40 environmental sensors. The integration of Hadoop-based preprocessing further distinguishes this work by addressing the scalability challenges inherent in environmental IoT systems. By doing so, this paper bridges the gap between theoretical modeling and practical deployment in data-intensive agricultural contexts.

The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows:

We design a scalable data processing pipeline based on Hadoop and MapReduce, tailored to the challenges of soil moisture time series data.

We implement and evaluate LSTM and RF models for multi-depth soil moisture forecasting using real-world data from a dense sensor network.

We analyze the strengths and limitations of each model under varying spatial and temporal conditions to assess their suitability as virtual sensors in smart farming applications.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section II reviews relevant literature. Section III presents the dataset and preprocessing steps. Section IV details the modeling approach and experimental setup. Section V discusses the results. Section VI concludes the paper and outlines future work.

 Related Work

Soil moisture estimation has been a critical area of research in environmental monitoring and precision agriculture. Traditional measurement techniques such as Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR) and Frequency Domain Reflectometry (FDR) offer accurate readings but are constrained by their high cost, power consumption, and limited scalability in field conditions [2]. As a result, virtual sensor approaches have emerged, using machine learning models trained on historical environmental data to estimate soil moisture across various spatial and temporal resolutions [3], [4].

Among machine learning techniques, Random Forest (RF) has gained wide adoption due to its robustness to noise, low risk of overfitting, and capacity to handle high-dimensional, nonlinear datasets [5]. Several studies have demonstrated the efficacy of RF in soil moisture estimation. For example, Shokati et al. [6] employed RF in conjunction with Sentinel2, Landsat-8/9, and UAV-based hyperspectral data to predict surface soil moisture with high accuracy. Similarly, Adab et al. [7] utilized ensemble learning techniques, including RF, to estimate surface moisture from remote sensing observations. Tyralis et al. [17] provided a focused review on the use of RF in hydrological and water-related applications, highlighting its adaptability to heterogeneous environmental datasets.

In parallel, deep learning techniques-particularly Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks-have shown significant promise in time series forecasting for environmental data. Initially introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber [8], LSTM networks are capable of learning long-range temporal dependencies, which is critical for accurately modeling the dynamics of soil moisture influenced by rainfall, temperature, and evapotranspiration. Several recent studies have validated the strength of LSTM in this domain: [10] applied multivariate LSTM for forecasting soil moisture under changing climatic conditions, while [11] demonstrated improved accuracy over traditional models using meteorological features. Xu et al. [18] proposed a hybrid PSO-LSTM model in which Particle Swarm Optimization was employed to fine-tune critical LSTM hyperparameters-such as batch size, time steps, and the number of hidden units-resulting in improved forecasting accuracy and extended lead times in rainfall-runoff prediction.

In terms of data infrastructure, the increasing availability of sensor-generated time series has led to the adoption of big data platforms for storage and processing. Hadoop, with its distributed file system (HDFS) and the MapReduce programming model, has been widely used for scalable preprocessing of large environmental datasets [12]. Applications in smart farming now frequently include such platforms to enable robust data pipelines for batch processing and feature extraction [13], [14]. Lambda architectures have also been proposed to support both real-time and batch analytics in agricultural monitoring systems [15]. The integration of these technologies with Internet of Things (IoT) devices further enhances scalability and responsiveness, as demonstrated in [19].

Despite these advancements, few studies have explored the combined use of distributed big data platforms like Hadoop with both deep learning and ensemble models in a unified pipeline for multi-depth soil moisture forecasting. This research aims to address this gap by proposing a scalable framework that integrates Hadoop-based preprocessing with LSTM and RF models to enable accurate virtual soil moisture sensing using real-world sensor data.

 Dataset and Preprocessing

This study utilizes time series data collected from a network of environmental sensors deployed at the Rabat experimental site in Morocco. The dataset includes both hourly and daily measurements of key agro-environmental variables such as soil moisture, temperature, and humidity, recorded at multiple depths-specifically at 30 cm and 90 cm. These sensors are part of an IoT-based infrastructure designed for continuous, high-frequency monitoring to support smart farming.

The raw sensor data is organized in structured text files (.txt) stored within two subdirectories: Hourly/ and Daily/, representing different temporal resolutions. Each file includes tab-separated values with attributes such as timestamp, location, sensor depth, and environmental readings. While the current dataset size remains moderate (hundreds of megabytes), future deployments and long-term monitoring are expected to produce large volumes of data. Therefore, scalability and efficiency in data preprocessing are key considerations.

Each record in the dataset contains: Location, Date, Time, Volumetric Water Content (VW), and Temperature (T) at depths of $30,60,90,120$, and 150 cm. This vertical profiling offers insights into subsurface soil conditions, which are critical for effective moisture prediction.

To prepare the data for machine learning models, we developed a scalable data preprocessing pipeline based on Apache Hadoop and the MapReduce programming model. The dataset was first uploaded to the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). A series of MapReduce jobs were then applied to perform parsing, cleaning, normalization, and feature extraction.

Preprocessing Pipeline

The distributed pipeline includes the following stages:

Parsing and Cleaning: Invalid records and outliers were removed based on domain-specific thresholds and statistical rules.

Time Synchronization: Readings from multiple depths were aligned to construct temporally consistent multivariate sequences.

Feature Engineering: Lagged variables, rolling statistics, and temporal gradients were computed to enrich the input space.

Normalization: Continuous variables were scaled to the [ 0], [1 ] range using Min-Max normalization to enhance model convergence.

Each stage is implemented as an independent MapReduce job, allowing distributed execution and efficient processing. This architecture ensures the system remains robust and scalable under increasing data loads.

 Machine Learning-Ready Datasets

The final output consists of two structured datasets exported as CSV files:

daily_ml_input.csv: Aggregated daily data for traditional ML models like Random Forest.

hourly_ml_input.csv: High-resolution hourly sequences used for time-series models such as LSTM.

Both datasets contain harmonized columns including soil moisture (target variable), temperature, humidity, sensor depth, and derived features. These preprocessed inputs serve as the foundation for the modeling experiments presented in Section IV.

Modeling and Experimental Setup

This section describes the modeling strategies and experiments designed to predict volumetric water content (VW) from sensor time series data. The primary goal is to forecast soil moisture at 30 cm depth using LSTM and Random Forest (RF) models. We also evaluate their performance at 90 cm depth to examine robustness across soil layers.

Lstm-Based Prediction

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are well-suited for time-dependent soil moisture series, capable of modeling long-range dependencies and non-linear patterns. Each LSTM cell contains a set of gates that regulate the flow of information through the network over time. The architecture of a standard LSTM cell is illustrated in Figure 1.

Forget Gate: $f_{t}=\sigma\left(W_{f} \cdot\left[h_{t-1}, x_{t}\right]+b_{f}\right)$

Input Gate: $i_{t}=\sigma\left(W_{i} \cdot\left[h_{t-1}, x_{t}\right]+b_{i}\right), \tilde{c}_{t}=\tanh \left(W_{c}\right.$. $\left.\left[h_{t-1}, x_{t}\right]+b_{c}\right)$

Cell Update: $c_{t}=f_{t} \odot c_{t-1}+i_{t} \odot \tilde{c}_{t}$

Output Gate: $o_{t}=\sigma\left(W_{o} \cdot\left[h_{t-1}, x_{t}\right]+b_{o}\right), h_{t}=o_{t} \odot \tanh \left(c_{t}\right)$\\

Variables: $x_{t}$: input, $h_{t-1}$: previous hidden state, $\sigma$: sigmoid, ⊙: element-wise product.

Lstm cell structure.
