Early collaborative filtering approaches - especially memory-based methods [1] - were interpretable and simple but struggled under data sparsity or irregular interaction patterns [12].

Matrix factorization (MF) techniques [2], popularized by the Netflix Prize, shifted focus toward latent embeddings. By mapping users and items into a shared Euclidean space, MF captured hidden preference patterns but treated entities as isolated vectors - losing sight of the interaction network that connects them.

Between history and state of the art: evolution of recommendation system techniques.

To address this, a wave of shallow graph embedding methods such as DeepWalk [7] and Node2Vec [8] emerged. These techniques sampled paths or neighborhoods in the graph and trained skip-gram models to encode structural proximity. While useful, they were largely unsupervised and decoupled from downstream tasks, limiting their utility in personalized recommendation.

Graph-based recommender models took this further by enabling supervised learning directly on graph structures. Architectures like NGCF [13], PinSage [14], and GraphSAGE [15] introduced message passing to capture multi-hop connectivity and higher-order relational signals. These GNN-based models treat recommendation not merely as matrix completion, but as structured prediction over graphs - blending node features, edge semantics, and topology into the learning process.

Recent surveys [15] [16] [17] offer taxonomies of graphbased recommendation techniques, yet few works isolate the structural signal from other data modalities. Our contribution fills this gap: we design an experimental setup that holds everything constant - same dataset, same evaluation metric, same training loop - across classical (MF, CF) and graphbased models. We strip away all user features and only retain minimal movie features (genres + title embeddings). By doing so, we shine light on the core question: to what extent can graph connectivity, as an inductive bias, explain model performance?

 Methodology: From Classical Models to Graph Neural Networks

In order to understand how Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are capable of revolutionizing recommendation systems, we compare their performance to two classical methods: memorybased Collaborative Filtering (CF) and Matrix Factorization (MF). We test the three methods on the MovieLens 100k dataset. We seek to highlight in which way leveraging graph structure releases a richer modeling bias, unleashing deeper relational patterns in user-item interactions.

Problem Formulation: Graph-Based Reframing of Recommendation

The MovieLens 100k dataset contains 100,000 explicit ratings (from 1 to 5) given by 943 users to 1,682 movies [3]. Rather than treating these ratings as isolated entries in a matrix, we reframe the task as a learning problem on a bipartite graph: user nodes on one side, movie nodes on the other, and edges representing observed ratings.

This shift of perspective highlights what really matters: the relationships. Each rating is not just a data point, but part of a broader interaction network. Our objective is to predict missing ratings - turning the task into a regression problem that learns from structure.

We go beyond pure topology by incorporating initial features. Movie nodes are initialized using both their genres (encoded as one-hot vectors) and title embeddings obtained from a pretrained language model. For user nodes, we rely only on their identifiers, represented as one-hot vectors - no additional metadata was considered for them in this study.

Figure 2 visualizes this graph, which forms the structural backbone of our GNN-based model

Illustration of the bipartite user-item graph. nodes represent users and movies; edges represent observed ratings.

 Baseline 1: Memory-Based Collaborative Filtering (CF)

Memory-based CF assumes that similar users will rate items similarly. It calculates similarity between users (or items) using metrics like cosine similarity or Pearson correlation.

The predicted rating $\hat{r}_{u, i}$ for user $u$ on item $i$ is computed as [1]: \begin{equation*} \hat{r}_{u, i}=\frac{\sum_{v \in N_{u}} s(u, v) \cdot r_{v, i}}{\sum_{v \in N_{u}}\verts(u, v)\vert} \end{equation*} where $s(u, v)$ is the similarity between users $u$ and $v$, and $N_{u}$ is the set of users similar to $u$.

Although intuitive and easy to implement, this method struggles with data sparsity and fails to capture deeper interaction structures.

 Baseline 2: Matrix Factorization (MF)

MF learns latent vectors for users and items. The rating matrix $R$ is approximated as the product of two low-rank matrices [2]: \begin{equation*} R \approx P Q^{\top} \end{equation*} where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{\vertU\vert \times k}$ and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{\vertI\vert \times k}$. Each user $u$ and item $i$ has a latent vector $p_{u}$ and $q_{i}$, and the predicted rating is: \begin{equation*} \hat{r}_{u, i}=p_{u}^{\top} q_{i} \end{equation*}

MF captures more abstract interactions than CF, but it still treats users and items as independent entities in a latent space, limiting its ability to model relational context.

 Graph Neural Networks for Recommendation

GNNs approach the problem differently. Rather than compressing the data into independent vectors, they model users and items as nodes and propagate information via edges through message passing.

At layer $l$, the embedding of node $u$ is updated by aggregating information from its neighbors [4]: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} h_{u}^{(l)}=\sigma & \left(W ^ { (l) } \cdot \operatorname { A G G R E G A T E } \left(\left\{h_{v}^{(l-1)}\right.\right.\right. \\ & \left.\left.: v \in \mathcal{N}(u)\} \cup\left\{h_{u}^{(l-1)}\right\}\right)\right) \end{aligned} \end{equation*} where $W^{(l)}$ is a learnable weight matrix, $\sigma$ is a non-linear activation function, and AGGREGATE can be a mean, sum, or max function.

This approach enables GNNs to learn complex, higher-order dependencies that MF and CF typically overlook. It results in richer representations that are aware of graph topology and interaction patterns.

 Model Structure: GraphSAGE with MLP for Edge Prediction

We represent user-item interactions with a Graph Neural Network with the GraphSAGE architecture [5]. As illustrated in figure 3, the model initially learns user and item node embeddings solely from their ID. They are then updated by two stacked layers of GraphSAGE convolution, which propagate information from each node's local neighborhood.

Each node embedding is updated using the GraphSAGE update rule: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} h_{u}^{(l)}=\sigma & \left(W ^ { (l) } \cdot \operatorname { C O N C A T } \left(h_{u}^{(l-1)},\right.\right. \\ & \left.\left.\operatorname{AGGREGATE}\left(\left\{h_{v}^{(l-1)}: v \in \mathcal{N}(u)\right\}\right)\right)\right) \end{aligned} \end{equation*} where $\mathcal{N}(u)$ denotes the neighbors of node $u, W^{(l)}$ is a learnable weight matrix, and $\sigma$ is a non-linear activation function.

The user and item latent representations are then fed through a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that predicts the interaction score for each user-item pair-equivalent to the rating in our regression setting. The model is end-to-end trained with mean squared error (MSE) loss.

This architecture allows the network to learn expressive structure-aware representations while preserving the relational inductive bias of the user-item graph. It also allows for more fine-grained predictions than conventional approaches, owing to the inclusion of neighborhood context under message passing.

Illustration of the bipartite user-item graph. nodes represent users and movies; edges represent observed ratings.

 Experimental Setup: Bringing Models to Life

To understand the real-world performance and expressive capacity of our GNN-based recommender system, we design a rigorous experimental setup. This setup mirrors the structure defined in our methodology and allows us to benchmark the proposed model against two classical approaches: Matrix Factorization (MF) and memory-based Collaborative Filtering (CF). All experiments are conducted using the well-established MovieLens 100k dataset [3].

Dataset and Graph Construction

The MovieLens 100k dataset is 100,000 user-movie ratings given by 943 users on 1,682 movies in a scale from 1 to 5. We represent this data as a bipartite graph: the set of nodes as the users, the set of nodes as the movies, and the edges as the observed ratings. This configuration automatically includes the relational viewpoint that our GNN model will utilize.

Node features. For movie nodes, we construct dense initial embeddings by concatenating genre encodings (onehot vectors) and semantic embeddings of movie titles. We extract the title embeddings using the pretrained all-distilroberta-v1 model from SentenceTransformers. For user nodes, one-hot vectors are utilized based on user IDs only, without side metadata.

This setup ensures that all of the structural cues are derived from the graph itself, rather than from engineered side features. This resulting graph serves as input to our models.

 Model Implementation and Architecture

Our GNN model is implemented using PyTorch Geometric [6]. The encoder is built using three stacked SAGEConv layers, each followed by ReLU activation. Node embeddings are updated through mean aggregation - the default aggregation function in PyG - enabling information to flow from neighboring nodes.

To adapt the encoder to our heterogeneous graph, we apply to_hetero, which maps the model to a type-aware version. The edge-level decoder combines the user and movie embeddings via concatenation and feeds them into a two-layer MLP that outputs predicted ratings.

The full architecture is trained end-to-end using mean squared error (MSE) loss. Ratings are treated as regression targets, and prediction outputs are later clamped and rounded to lie within the discrete range [1], [5].

 Training Protocol and Evaluation Metrics

Training for 200 epochs, using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01. A ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler is used to adjust the learning rate based on validation performance dynamically.

The data was divided into training, validation, and test sets. via random edge-level splitting (10 % for validation and test). No negative sampling is applied, as every rating is explicit.

Model performance is evaluated with Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the predicted ratings. This metric measures the model's capacity to effectively estimate real-valued ratings across all stages of the division.

Baselines:

Collaborative Filtering (CF): We apply item-based CF using both cosine and Pearson similarity metrics [1]. Predictions are computed by weighted averaging and evaluated using RMSE.

Matrix Factorization (MF): We use Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) with 20 latent components. Predicted ratings result from dot products between user and item vectors.

To ensure fairness, all models are trained and evaluated using the same data splits. This setup helps us fairly quantify the value added by the graph-based inductive bias of GNNs.

 Results and Discussion: What the Numbers Tell Us

Now that all models have been trained under consistent conditions, it's time to step back and interpret what we've learned. Our aim here isn't just to list results, but to reflect on the learning behaviors and the real value each approach brings to the recommendation task.

Reading the RMSE Curves

Across the 200 training cycles, we tracked the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) across the training, validation, and test datasets. The trajectory of the RMSE, plotted in Figure 5, traces the three stages of learning:

Early Learning (Epochs 0-25): The model learns very quickly, reducing error tremendously. The steep drop reminds the network of its capacity to understand moment-to-moment structure in the set of data.

Refinement and Stabilization (Epochs 25-175): RMS gradually flattening curves, indicating a period of stable refinement. Gradually, the model tends towards maximal representations.

Final Convergence (After Epoch 175): All three RMS curves - training, validation, and test - are kept proximally aligned with each other. Such concordance indicates good generalization and minimal chance of overfitting.

This learning path shows that the GNN architecture has been well-regularized and is helped by the structure that it acquires. The generalizability of the model lies in how it continually performs on unseen validation and test Samples.

Rmse progression across epochs for training, validation, and test sets.

 Comparing the Models: A Quantitative View

We compared three different models - a Graph Neural Network (GNN), Matrix Factorization (MF), and memorybased Collaborative Filtering (CF) - using RMSE as the evaluation metric. The results are summarized in Table ??.

The GNN model emerges as the clear winner. Its RMSE values remain under 1 across all data splits - an indication that it effectively captures the complex relationships between users and items. The model benefits not only from message passing but also from the node-level feature engineering we applied.

By contrast, MF performs moderately but lacks the expressive power to compete with graph-based learning. It still does better than CF, which appears particularly limited in sparse or noisy datasets. CF's reliance on similarity heuristics limits its effectiveness here.

Rmse progression across epochs for training, validation, and test sets.

 Looking Beyond the Scores: What Do These Results Tell Us?

Numbers alone don't tell the whole story-but they point us in the right direction. Here's what emerges from this comparative study, both technically and conceptually:

Graph structure changes the game. What truly differentiates GNNs is their capacity to move beyond flat data representations-what we often call Euclidean data, like matrices or grids-and embrace the complexity of non-Euclidean structures such as graphs. By modeling user-item interactions as a network, GNNs don't just predict-they understand. Connectivity becomes a source of inductive bias, allowing the model to detect subtle patterns and generalize more effectively.

Matrix Factorization still holds ground. While it operates in the Euclidean domain, MF can still uncover latent structures and offer solid predictions when data is dense or the graph structure is unavailable. It's efficient, interpretable, and sometimes, that's exactly what's needed in constrained environments.

Collaborative Filtering shows its limits. CF is based on intuitive similarity measures and works well in simpler scenarios. But as soon as the data becomes sparse or noisy-conditions often encountered in real-world sys-tems-its predictive power collapses. It lacks the expressive capacity to model high-order or indirect relationships.

In short, this work illustrates a shift from modeling with surface-level heuristics toward embracing structural awareness. GNNs don't simply memorize patterns-they learn how structure shapes behavior. And that shift, from flat to relational, from isolated to connected, is what makes them such a promising direction for the next generation of recommender systems.

 Conclusion and Future Work: Toward Structure-Aware Recommendations

This study has walked through a comparative journey, starting with classical baselines like Collaborative Filtering and Matrix Factorization, and moving toward a more structure-aware paradigm enabled by Graph Neural Networks. At each step, the message became clearer: when it comes to capturing the nuanced, interconnected nature of user-item interactions, graph-based models offer something qualitatively different.

The GNN architecture we explored doesn't just outperform its predecessors-it shifts the way we frame the recommendation problem. By embedding relational context directly into the learning process it allows us to move beyond flat, tabular representations and toward richer, non-Euclidean learning. The result? A model that generalizes better, adapts to sparse data more gracefully, and captures behavior patterns that other methods miss.

But this is just the beginning.

For future work, several promising directions open up:

Hybrid Feature Integration: While we intentionally minimized metadata usage in this first version, incorporating richer user and content features (e.g., temporal behavior, social signals) could further enhance prediction accuracy.

Hyperbolic and Topological Extensions: Given the hierarchical and latent geometry often found in user-item graphs, extending the model into hyperbolic or topologically-informed spaces may unlock deeper structure.

Explainability and Human-in-the-Loop: One key challenge that remains is interpretability. Future efforts could explore ways to make GNN-based recommendations more transparent and interactive-especially in sensitive domains like healthcare or education.

Scalability to Real-World Datasets: Testing this approach on larger, noisier datasets (such as Amazon, Goodreads, or streaming platforms) would further validate its robustness under real-world constraints.

In closing, this work is not just a performance comparison. It's a call to rethink how we design models that understand people, not as isolated vectors in a matrix, but as dynamic agents embedded in a web of interactions. Graph-based learning gives us the tools to model that web. And with it, perhaps, build recommender systems that are not only more accurate, but also more human-aware.

This paper examines Virtual Energy Communities (VECs) as a tool for sustainable energy transition and rural development. The main characteristics of VECs are presented, including the prosumer model, P2P energy trading, smart contracts, and flexible consumption. Three main architectures-centralized, decentralized, and hybrid-are analyzed together with their supporting ICT technologies such as IoT, edge computing, artificial intelligence, blockchain, and modern communication standards. The study summarizes international practices from the EU, North America, and Asia, identifying the social, technological, and regulatory factors driving the implementation of VECs. For Bulgaria, the opportunities are linked to European funding mechanisms and national strategies for digitalization and artificial intelligence. A mathematical optimization model is proposed to balance generation, storage, and consumption, with practical applications in rural and industrial regions. Challenges related to infrastructure, regulation, and cybersecurity are outlined. Recommendations are provided for future research and policies to support the development of VECs in Bulgaria within the framework of the European Green Deal.

 Virtual Energy Communities Information and Communication Technologies (ICT) Decentralized Energy Renewable Energy Sources (RES) Mathematical Models Introduction

Rural areas in Europe and around the world are increasingly facing new challenges related to energy independence, sustainability and access to developed energy infrastructure. These regions have an outdated energy grid, high energy costs and limited opportunities to participate in the energy transition - the process of moving towards a lowcarbon economy using renewable energy sources and modern energy solutions. Meanwhile, renewable energy sources (RES) such as solar, wind and biomass provide opportunities for decentralization and clean electricity generation, especially in agrarian areas. Maximizing the potential of these opportunities entails adopting organizational, coordination, and energy management models that surpass conventional centralized systems. The concept of Virtual Energy Communities has emerged as a progressive model for decentralized energy cooperation that, through information and communication technologies, allows the co-production, sharing, trading, and optimization of energy between individual consumers and producers without being geographically constrained.

VECs offer a real response to the global sustainable energy goals set out in the European Green Deal and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Through the integration of IoT, blockchain, artificial intelligence and smart grid technologies, VECs contribute to building more sustainable and intelligent energy systems that can meet the specific needs of rural areas.

This paper aims to present existing technological and organizational solutions related to VECs, to summarize best practices and models from international experience and to outline the potential for their implementation in rural Bulgaria. The role of ICT is highlighted in overcoming infrastructural and socio-economic barriers that limit the development of energy communities in the country.

 Virtual Energy Communities: Components and Architectures

VECs are collections of individuals and entities that are connected through digital infrastructure and interact with each other to produce, consume and manage energy more efficiently. Compared to conventional energy networks, VECs offer a model of decentralization where individual actors are not only consumers but active parties in the energy system. The main activities within VECs include production, sharing and trading and coordination and optimization. These communities are built on the principles of digitalization and smart governance, enabling the participation of diverse users-households, farmers, Small and Medium-sized Enterprises (SMEs), and municipalities. The main components of VECs include:

Prosumer model - each participant is both a producer and consumer of energy, which promotes autonomy and reduces dependence on centralized suppliers.

Peer-to-Peer (P2P) energy trading - participants can directly buy and sell energy with each other through decentralized platforms.

Smart Contracts - automation of energy transactions through blockchain technology, ensuring transparency and security of contractual relationships.

Demand Response - adapting consumption in real time according to production, market conditions and grid needs, through automated systems and smart devices.

Collective storage - the use of shared energy storage systems (batteries) that optimize the balance between production and consumption within a community.

Intelligent data management - systems for collecting, analyzing and predicting consumer energy behavior, supported by artificial intelligence and big data analytics.

The main components of virtual energy communities

The architecture of VECs defines how the interaction between participants, energy exchange and data processing is organized. Three main types of architecture are distinguished in the literature:

Centralized architecture - in this model, the community is managed through a central operator or platform that coordinates all energy flows, transactions and balancing between producers and consumers. The centralized structure facilitates connection to the national power system. It allows for tight control over security and compliance with regulatory requirements. This approach is particularly suitable for small communities with a limited number of participants or in regions where regulations and technical infrastructure do not allow for full decentralization [1].

Decentralized architecture - relies on blockchain technologies and P2P platforms where there is no central intermediary. Each participant can directly transact with other community members through smart contracts, ensuring security and automation. This model is suitable for communities with high digital literacy and infrastructure readiness to implement distributed ledger technologies (DLT). Despite the advantages, challenges are related to energy efficiency of blockchain networks and the need for reliable communication connectivity [2].

Hybrid architecture - this model combines the advantages of centralized and decentralized management. In this case, there is a central controlling authority or platform, but it allows decentralized transactions between participants based on blockchain technologies. Hybrid architecture is preferred when there is a need to ensure regulatory compliance and at the same time exploit the advantages of P2P markets and smart contracts [3].

The types of virtual energy community architecture are presented in Fig. 2:

Virtual energy community architecture

The development of VECs would be impossible without the use of advanced ICT components that support the construction and operation of VECs. These include:

Internet of Things (IoT) - provides a network of interconnected sensors and devices that monitor real-time energy production, consumption and storage. This enables efficient tracking of the energy contributions of each community actor.

Edge Computing - an approach to local data processing that allows decisions to be made close to where the information is generated, rather than sending the data to remote cloud servers. This significantly reduces latency and increases security. The application of edge computing in microgrids improves energy management and provides stability to local power systems [4].

Big Data Analytics - The large volumes of data generated within VECs require powerful analytical tools. Big Data analytics supports forecasting and the identification of energy patterns to enhance the allocation of energy resources. Big Data plays a crucial role in the transformation of smart grids by providing more accurate predictions and enabling strategic resource management [5].

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) - these technologies automate processes, offering intelligent strategies for renewable generation forecasts. There are models based on real data that use AI to manage energy communities and improve their efficiency [6].

Blockchain - technology can facilitate P2P trading in microgrids through smart contracts, increasing the security and efficiency of energy exchange processes [7].

Long Range Wide Area Network (LoRaWAN) communications - LoRaWAN is a wireless communication protocol designed specifically for long-range, low-power IoT networks. As a result, LoRaWAN allows building affordable and sustainable communication networks without the need for expensive mobile or wired infrastructure. This protocol proves to be effective in both rural and urban settings [8]. IoT systems often use communication protocols such as Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT), which provide realtime data transmission with minimal power consumption - an advantage for devices operating in remote areas with limited resources [9]. At the same time, 5 G networks provide the high speed and low latency needed for applications with critical time and capacity requirements [10].

These components form the technological basis of VECs and enable their sustainable integration into modern energy systems.

 Global Overview of Virtual Energy Communities

VECs are being widely developed and implemented across various regions of the world, adapting to local energy policies and regulatory frameworks. The European Union has led the way in creating a regulatory framework for the development of energy communities through the Renewable Energy Directive (RED II), which defines the concept of energy communities and encourages the participation of citizens, small businesses and local authorities in energy production, consumption and trade [11]. An example of a successful national policy is Energiewende in Germany, where energy cooperatives and communities are key to the transition to a low-carbon economy. Germany is also implementing decentralized P2P trading platforms and local markets [12].

In the US, the Brooklyn Microgrid in New York is one of the first real examples of a decentralized energy community based on a blockchain P2P trading platform. In this model, participants can directly buy and sell energy from and to their neighbors, and the process is fully automated through smart contracts [7].

In Asia, Japan is actively investing in building smart energy communities integrating IoT and AI to manage local microgrids and optimize consumption. Cities such as Fukuoka are implementing digital twins and AI for load balancing [13].

In South Korea, blockchain technologies are being used to build decentralized energy trading platforms, facilitating the integration of large numbers of prosumers into the national energy grid. There, the government is actively supporting pilot projects for virtual energy communities in smart cities [14].

 Challenges and Constraints

Despite the many advantages, the development of virtual energy communities is associated with several challenges and limitations that may hinder their widespread implementation and sustainability.

Technology challenges - include the complexity of integrating diverse renewable energy solutions. Another challenge is the coordination of energy storage and smart grid systems within a unified platform. The need for highly reliable communication infrastructures and the complexity of managing large volumes of data in real time make it difficult for VECs to operate effectively. Furthermore, the lack of standardized technology solutions and protocols can limit interoperability between different systems and devices.

Economic and social barriers - expressed in the need for significant upfront investment to build infrastructure, deploy IoT devices, storage systems and commerce platforms. Social challenges include lack of awareness and engagement among citizens, and reluctance to participate in energy communities due to lack of trust or understanding of the benefits.

Regulatory constraints - in many countries the regulatory framework is still insufficiently developed to fully support the development of VECs. The lack of clear rules on energy trading, sharing and taxation of prosumers creates uncertainty and hinders investment. There are also constraints related to access to energy markets and a lack of incentives for small producers, making participation in such communities less attractive.

The integration of modern digital technologies introduces new cybersecurity challenges. It leads to a higher risk of cyber-attacks and unauthorized access. Additionally, there is an increased threat of personal and energy data being misused. This involves ensuring that sensitive data remains protected from unauthorized access, remains unaltered during transmission or storage, and is only available to the appropriate users. The lack of standardized cybersecurity solutions in the energy community is a significant barrier to their wider adoption [3]. These challenges highlight the need for coordinated efforts between technology companies, policies, regulators, and the communities themselves to find sustainable solutions for the development of VECs.

 Opportunities and Prospects for Bulgaria

In Bulgaria, the development of Virtual Energy Communities faces challenges due to the lack of a legal definition and limited examples of energy cooperation. Nevertheless, the country possesses natural and technological conditions favorable for implementing VECs, particularly in regions with high solar radiation and renewable energy potential. Climate change is expected to impact biodiversity, water resources, agriculture, and forests, which will have negative effects on society, citizens, and the economy [15]. This necessitates the integration of sustainable energy solutions.

The development of virtual energy communities in Bulgaria combines significant potential with the need for targeted policies, technical infrastructure, and social engagement. Although the concept is not yet formally defined in Bulgarian legislation, several prerequisites provide a favorable foundation for their implementation.

Currently, there are only a few examples of energy cooperation in Bulgaria, mainly through associations for photovoltaic installations or energy efficiency initiatives. The lack of a clearly defined legal framework for "energy communities" and the absence of incentives for peer-to-peer energy trading limit the widespread adoption of VECs. Additionally, rural areas still experience insufficient digital connectivity and low levels of digital literacy.

The sustainable development of energy communities depends on the presence of supportive conditions established by both policy and infrastructure. Without a stable regulatory environment and mechanisms that ensure long-term viability, such initiatives face significant challenges. [1]. In this regard, Bulgaria should:

Adequately transpose RED II into national legislation with a focus on decentralized citizen participation.

Legally define the concept of a "virtual energy community".

Facilitate access to the energy grid for prosumers.

Introduce incentives for investments in digital platforms for managing VECs.

Bulgaria has access to a range of European and national programs that support the development of virtual energy communities. These include The LIFE Clean Energy Transition program, Horizon Europe - Cluster 5, the Recovery and Resilience Facility, and the Rural Development Program, which provide funding for the decentralization of energy systems, digitalization, renewable energy sources, and agroenergy communities. These mechanisms create favorable conditions for energy development and increased energy independence in rural areas. Leveraging all these instruments can accelerate the implementation of VECs in Bulgariaespecially considering the EU Green Deal, the need for energy independence, and the opportunities for digital transformation of the energy sector.

For the effective implementation of virtual energy communities, it is essential to:

Invest in communication infrastructure (LoRaWAN, 5G).

Develop open-source platforms for energy flow management and trading.

Promote training programs and digital skills development for local communities.

Introduce software solutions based on AI and blockchain to support management, forecasting, and data security.

The use of mathematical models and visualizations is an important element in the planning and management of VECs. These tools support the understanding and forecasting of the dynamics between energy production, consumption, storage, and trading, as well as informed decision-making for governance.

Applying mathematical models to optimize energy flows within VECs in Bulgaria can contribute to more efficient use of local resources and a reduction in energy dependence. A basic mathematical approach is illustrated by the following formula: \begin{equation*} \text { Minimize } C=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left(C_{\text {gen }}(t)+C_{\text {stor }}(t)+C_{\text {exch }}(t)\right) \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Where:

C - total system costs for energy management.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {gen }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost of energy generation at time t.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {stor }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost of energy storage and transfer at time t.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {exch }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost associated with energy exchange with the external grid and among participants at time t.

T - total number of time intervals.

This objective function is formulated to optimally balance energy production, storage, and trading within VECs, considering temporal dynamics.

The mathematical model presented in equation~(\ref{eqn1}) can be applied by collecting data on the costs of generation, storage, and exchange. Optimization algorithms then minimize the total system cost and determine whether it is more advantageous for the energy to be consumed, stored, or sold. In Bulgaria, this model is particularly suitable for agricultural regions such as Dobruja and the Thracian plain. It can also be applied in municipalities of the Rhodopes and the Northwestern region with outdated electrical grid, as well as in industrial zones and tourist areas. In these cases, balancing local generation, storage, and consumption improves energy autonomy and reduces dependence on the centralized grid. The model can assist municipalities and cooperatives in effective planning with limited resources by guiding investments in energy generation and trading. It also facilitates integration with the European energy market and supports the green transition by accounting for the carbon footprint of energy flows. Implementing this model in pilot projects for VECs and microgrids would promote long-term growth and enhance regional energy balance.

 Discussion

The future of Virtual Energy Communities in Bulgaria and globally requires a systematic scientific approach that encompasses both technological and economic aspects. To fully realize the potential of VECs, research should focus on the development of platform-based solutions that integrate IoT devices, edge computing, blockchain, and artificial intelligence into a unified environment for energy flow management. It is essential that these platforms offer userfriendly access to P2P energy trading.

The use of simulations and digital twins is a primary tool for modeling energy communities. These technologies allow for forecasting system behavior, managing loads, and assessing resilience under various scenarios. Cybersecurity is another major challenge in the digitalization of energy systems, necessitating the deployment of advanced protection protocols to prevent potential attacks.

In Bulgaria, the application of mathematical models for optimizing energy flows can support the strategic planning of VECs, enable optimal allocation of generated energy among consumption, storage, and trading, and improve forecasting based on seasonal fluctuations in renewable energy output. Bulgarian research institutes and universities have the capacity to adapt these methods to national conditions, including regional differences in consumption patterns and resource availability.

In the long term, implementing such models and technologies will contribute to the development of intelligent energy systems in Bulgaria, aligned with the goals of the European Green Deal and the evolving EU energy market.

 Conclusion

Virtual Energy Communities represent an effective organizational and technological approach to restructuring energy systems, particularly in rural areas. By combining renewable energy sources with modern digital technologies such as IoT, artificial intelligence, blockchain, and edge computing, VECs enable improved management of local generation, storage, and consumption. The analysis of architecture and ICT components highlights the potential of centralized, decentralized, and hybrid models, as well as the importance of secure and efficient data exchange through contemporary communication standards.

The application of mathematical models for energy flow optimization in Bulgaria can support agricultural regions, municipalities, industrial zones, and tourist areas in achieving greater energy autonomy. The proposed model demonstrates how general reduced storage, and exchange costs can be minimized to increase energy efficiency, reduce dependence on the centralized grid, and facilitate integration with the European energy market. By accounting for the carbon footprint of energy flows, the model also contributes to the objectives of the green transition.

The successful implementation of VECs requires not only technological infrastructure but also supportive policies, financial mechanisms, and initiatives for capacity building at the local level. Pilot projects in rural regions of Bulgaria can serve as experimental environments for validating the proposed models and strategies. In the long term, the integration of VECs into the national energy system has the potential to strengthen regional economies and align Bulgaria with the goals of the European Green Deal.

This paper examines Virtual Energy Communities (VECs) as a tool for sustainable energy transition and rural development. The main characteristics of VECs are presented, including the prosumer model, P2P energy trading, smart contracts, and flexible consumption. Three main architectures-centralized, decentralized, and hybrid-are analyzed together with their supporting ICT technologies such as IoT, edge computing, artificial intelligence, blockchain, and modern communication standards. The study summarizes international practices from the EU, North America, and Asia, identifying the social, technological, and regulatory factors driving the implementation of VECs. For Bulgaria, the opportunities are linked to European funding mechanisms and national strategies for digitalization and artificial intelligence. A mathematical optimization model is proposed to balance generation, storage, and consumption, with practical applications in rural and industrial regions. Challenges related to infrastructure, regulation, and cybersecurity are outlined. Recommendations are provided for future research and policies to support the development of VECs in Bulgaria within the framework of the European Green Deal.

 Virtual Energy Communities Information and Communication Technologies (ICT) Decentralized Energy Renewable Energy Sources (RES) Mathematical Models Konstantin Preslavsky University of Shumen RD-08109/05.02.2025 The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the project CoE UNITe BG16RFPR002-1.014-0004 funded by PRIDST, co-funded by the European Union. This study was supported by internal research project RD-08109/05.02.2025 funded by Konstantin Preslavsky University of Shumen. Introduction

Rural areas in Europe and around the world are increasingly facing new challenges related to energy independence, sustainability and access to developed energy infrastructure. These regions have an outdated energy grid, high energy costs and limited opportunities to participate in the energy transition - the process of moving towards a low-carbon economy using renewable energy sources and modern energy solutions. Meanwhile, renewable energy sources (RES) such as solar, wind and biomass provide opportunities for decentralization and clean electricity generation, especially in agrarian areas. Maximizing the potential of these opportunities entails adopting organizational, coordination, and energy management models that surpass conventional centralized systems. The concept of Virtual Energy Communities has emerged as a progressive model for decentralized energy cooperation that, through information and communication technologies, allows the co-production, sharing, trading, and optimization of energy between individual consumers and producers without being geographically constrained.

VECs offer a real response to the global sustainable energy goals set out in the European Green Deal and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Through the integration of IoT, blockchain, artificial intelligence and smart grid technologies, VECs contribute to building more sustainable and intelligent energy systems that can meet the specific needs of rural areas.

This paper aims to present existing technological and organizational solutions related to VECs, to summarize best practices and models from international experience and to outline the potential for their implementation in rural Bulgaria. The role of ICT is highlighted in overcoming infrastructural and socio-economic barriers that limit the development of energy communities in the country.

 Virtual Energy Communities: Components and Architectures

VECs are collections of individuals and entities that are connected through digital infrastructure and interact with each other to produce, consume and manage energy more efficiently. Compared to conventional energy networks, VECs offer a model of decentralization where individual actors are not only consumers but active parties in the energy system. The main activities within VECs include production, sharing and trading and coordination and optimization. These communities are built on the principles of digitalization and smart governance, enabling the participation of diverse users-households, farmers, Small and Medium-sized Enterprises (SMEs), and municipalities. The main components of VECs include:

Prosumer model - each participant is both a producer and consumer of energy, which promotes autonomy and reduces dependence on centralized suppliers.

Peer-to-Peer (P2P) energy trading - participants can directly buy and sell energy with each other through decentralized platforms.

Smart Contracts - automation of energy transactions through blockchain technology, ensuring transparency and security of contractual relationships.

Demand Response - adapting consumption in real time according to production, market conditions and grid needs, through automated systems and smart devices.

Collective storage - the use of shared energy storage systems (batteries) that optimize the balance between production and consumption within a community.

Intelligent data management - systems for collecting, analyzing and predicting consumer energy behavior, supported by artificial intelligence and big data analytics.

The main components of virtual energy communities

The architecture of VECs defines how the interaction between participants, energy exchange and data processing is organized. Three main types of architecture are distinguished in the literature:

Centralized architecture - in this model, the community is managed through a central operator or platform that coordinates all energy flows, transactions and balancing between producers and consumers. The centralized structure facilitates connection to the national power system. It allows for tight control over security and compliance with regulatory requirements. This approach is particularly suitable for small communities with a limited number of participants or in regions where regulations and technical infrastructure do not allow for full decentralization [1].

Decentralized architecture - relies on blockchain technologies and P2P platforms where there is no central intermediary. Each participant can directly transact with other community members through smart contracts, ensuring security and automation. This model is suitable for communities with high digital literacy and infrastructure readiness to implement distributed ledger technologies (DLT). Despite the advantages, challenges are related to energy efficiency of blockchain networks and the need for reliable communication connectivity [2].

Hybrid architecture - this model combines the advantages of centralized and decentralized management. In this case, there is a central controlling authority or platform, but it allows decentralized transactions between participants based on blockchain technologies. Hybrid architecture is preferred when there is a need to ensure regulatory compliance and at the same time exploit the advantages of P2P markets and smart contracts [3].

The types of virtual energy community architecture are presented in Fig. 2:

Virtual energy community architecture

The development of VECs would be impossible without the use of advanced ICT components that support the construction and operation of VECs. These include:

Internet of Things (IoT) - provides a network of interconnected sensors and devices that monitor real-time energy production, consumption and storage. This enables efficient tracking of the energy contributions of each community actor.

Edge Computing - an approach to local data processing that allows decisions to be made close to where the information is generated, rather than sending the data to remote cloud servers. This significantly reduces latency and increases security. The application of edge computing in microgrids improves energy management and provides stability to local power systems [4].

Big Data Analytics - The large volumes of data generated within VECs require powerful analytical tools. Big Data analytics supports forecasting and the identification of energy patterns to enhance the allocation of energy resources. Big Data plays a crucial role in the transformation of smart grids by providing more accurate predictions and enabling strategic resource management [5].

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) - these technologies automate processes, offering intelligent strategies for renewable generation forecasts. There are models based on real data that use AI to manage energy communities and improve their efficiency [6].

Blockchain - technology can facilitate P2P trading in microgrids through smart contracts, increasing the security and efficiency of energy exchange processes [7].

Long Range Wide Area Network (LoRaWAN) communications - LoRaWAN is a wireless communication protocol designed specifically for long-range, low-power IoT networks. As a result, LoRaWAN allows building affordable and sustainable communication networks without the need for expensive mobile or wired infrastructure. This protocol proves to be effective in both rural and urban settings [8]. IoT systems often use communication protocols such as Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT), which provide real-time data transmission with minimal power consumption - an advantage for devices operating in remote areas with limited resources [9]. At the same time, 5 G networks provide the high speed and low latency needed for applications with critical time and capacity requirements [10].

These components form the technological basis of VECs and enable their sustainable integration into modern energy systems.

 Global Overview of Virtual Energy Communities

VECs are being widely developed and implemented across various regions of the world, adapting to local energy policies and regulatory frameworks. The European Union has led the way in creating a regulatory framework for the development of energy communities through the Renewable Energy Directive (RED II), which defines the concept of energy communities and encourages the participation of citizens, small businesses and local authorities in energy production, consumption and trade [11]. An example of a successful national policy is Energiewende in Germany, where energy cooperatives and communities are key to the transition to a low-carbon economy. Germany is also implementing decentralized P2P trading platforms and local markets [12].

In the US, the Brooklyn Microgrid in New York is one of the first real examples of a decentralized energy community based on a blockchain P2P trading platform. In this model, participants can directly buy and sell energy from and to their neighbors, and the process is fully automated through smart contracts [7].

In Asia, Japan is actively investing in building smart energy communities integrating IoT and AI to manage local microgrids and optimize consumption. Cities such as Fukuoka are implementing digital twins and AI for load balancing [13].

In South Korea, blockchain technologies are being used to build decentralized energy trading platforms, facilitating the integration of large numbers of prosumers into the national energy grid. There, the government is actively supporting pilot projects for virtual energy communities in smart cities [14].

 Challenges and Constraints

Despite the many advantages, the development of virtual energy communities is associated with several challenges and limitations that may hinder their widespread implementation and sustainability.

Technology challenges - include the complexity of integrating diverse renewable energy solutions. Another challenge is the coordination of energy storage and smart grid systems within a unified platform. The need for highly reliable communication infrastructures and the complexity of managing large volumes of data in real time make it difficult for VECs to operate effectively. Furthermore, the lack of standardized technology solutions and protocols can limit interoperability between different systems and devices.

Economic and social barriers - expressed in the need for significant upfront investment to build infrastructure, deploy IoT devices, storage systems and commerce platforms. Social challenges include lack of awareness and engagement among citizens, and reluctance to participate in energy communities due to lack of trust or understanding of the benefits.

Regulatory constraints - in many countries the regulatory framework is still insufficiently developed to fully support the development of VECs. The lack of clear rules on energy trading, sharing and taxation of prosumers creates uncertainty and hinders investment. There are also constraints related to access to energy markets and a lack of incentives for small producers, making participation in such communities less attractive.

The integration of modern digital technologies introduces new cybersecurity challenges. It leads to a higher risk of cyber-attacks and unauthorized access. Additionally, there is an increased threat of personal and energy data being misused. This involves ensuring that sensitive data remains protected from unauthorized access, remains unaltered during transmission or storage, and is only available to the appropriate users. The lack of standardized cybersecurity solutions in the energy community is a significant barrier to their wider adoption [3]. These challenges highlight the need for coordinated efforts between technology companies, policies, regulators, and the communities themselves to find sustainable solutions for the development of VECs.

 Opportunities and Prospects for Bulgaria

In Bulgaria, the development of Virtual Energy Communities faces challenges due to the lack of a legal definition and limited examples of energy cooperation. Nevertheless, the country possesses natural and technological conditions favorable for implementing VECs, particularly in regions with high solar radiation and renewable energy potential. Climate change is expected to impact biodiversity, water resources, agriculture, and forests, which will have negative effects on society, citizens, and the economy [15]. This necessitates the integration of sustainable energy solutions.

The development of virtual energy communities in Bulgaria combines significant potential with the need for targeted policies, technical infrastructure, and social engagement. Although the concept is not yet formally defined in Bulgarian legislation, several prerequisites provide a favorable foundation for their implementation.

Currently, there are only a few examples of energy cooperation in Bulgaria, mainly through associations for photovoltaic installations or energy efficiency initiatives. The lack of a clearly defined legal framework for “energy communities” and the absence of incentives for peer-to-peer energy trading limit the widespread adoption of VECs. Additionally, rural areas still experience insufficient digital connectivity and low levels of digital literacy.

The sustainable development of energy communities depends on the presence of supportive conditions established by both policy and infrastructure. Without a stable regulatory environment and mechanisms that ensure long-term viability, such initiatives face significant challenges. [1]. In this regard, Bulgaria should:

Adequately transpose RED II into national legislation with a focus on decentralized citizen participation.

Legally define the concept of a “virtual energy community”.

Facilitate access to the energy grid for prosumers.

Introduce incentives for investments in digital platforms for managing VECs.

Bulgaria has access to a range of European and national programs that support the development of virtual energy communities. These include The LIFE Clean Energy Transition program, Horizon Europe - Cluster 5, the Recovery and Resilience Facility, and the Rural Development Program, which provide funding for the decentralization of energy systems, digitalization, renewable energy sources, and agro-energy communities. These mechanisms create favorable conditions for energy development and increased energy independence in rural areas. Leveraging all these instruments can accelerate the implementation of VECs in Bulgaria—especially considering the EU Green Deal, the need for energy independence, and the opportunities for digital transformation of the energy sector.

For the effective implementation of virtual energy communities, it is essential to:

Invest in communication infrastructure (LoRaWAN, 5G).

Develop open-source platforms for energy flow management and trading.

Promote training programs and digital skills development for local communities.

Introduce software solutions based on AI and blockchain to support management, forecasting, and data security.

The use of mathematical models and visualizations is an important element in the planning and management of VECs. These tools support the understanding and forecasting of the dynamics between energy production, consumption, storage, and trading, as well as informed decision-making for governance.

C - total system costs for energy management.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {gen }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost of energy generation at time t.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {stor }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost of energy storage and transfer at time t.

$\mathrm{C}_{\text {exch }}(\mathrm{t})$ - cost associated with energy exchange with the external grid and among participants at time t.

T - total number of time intervals.

This study uses advanced machine learning techniques to investigate consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India. Data were collected through an online survey distributed across various social media platforms, resulting in $\mathbf{1, 9 0 7}$ respondents from different Indian states. The research focused on key factors, including knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, health, price, availability, consumption behaviour, attitude, and behaviour control of participants. A comparative analysis was conducted using multiple machine learning algorithms: logistic regression, support vector machines (SVM), k-nearest neighbors, random forest, naive Bayes, neural networks, and decision trees. After using five evaluation metrics, the decision tree model demonstrated superior performance, achieving a mean rank of 1.8. Additionally, these models ranked the variables affecting behavioural intention. Price and Motivation emerged as the most significant predictors of organic food consumption behavior. The findings contribute to understanding organic food consumption patterns and can inform marketing strategies and policy decisions in the organic food industry. Future research aims to refine and deploy these models for practical implementation, potentially enhancing decision-making processes in the organic food market.

 Consumer Behavior Organic Food Consumption Machine Learning Predictive Modeling Introduction

The increasing global awareness of health, environmental sustainability, and food safety has led to a growing interest in organic food consumption [1]. In India, a country with deeprooted agricultural traditions and a rapidly evolving consumer base, the organic food market has experienced significant growth in recent years [2]. Despite this positive trend, organic food adoption rates remain uneven across different regions and demographic segments [3]. Understanding the behavioral factors that influence consumer intent to purchase and consume organic food is crucial for promoting sustainable consumption and informing policy, marketing, and production strategies [4].

Consumer behaviour towards organic food is a complex interplay of psychological, social, economic, and demographic factors [5]. Previous studies have identified various constructs, such as health consciousness, price sensitivity, knowledge, availability, motivation, and social influence that shape individual decisions in this domain [6]. However, traditional analytical methods often fail to capture such multidimensional data's nuanced patterns and non-linear relationships. Advanced machine learning (ML) techniques offer a promising alternative by enabling more accurate prediction, deeper insight, and the ability to model interactions between numerous variables [7].

This study uses a data-driven approach to explore the underlying factors influencing organic food consumption in India. By leveraging a robust dataset collected through a nationwide online survey, we apply a suite of ML algorithms, including Decision Trees, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Naive Bayes, and Neural Networks, to identify and evaluate the predictors of consumer intention. The study also ranks the relative importance of behavioral constructs such as knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, price, health, and availability, providing empirical insights that can inform stakeholders across the organic food supply chain.

Through rigorous data pre-processing, feature engineering, and model evaluation, this research seeks to answer two key questions: (1) Which factors most significantly influence the intention to consume organic food in the future? (2) Which ML model most accurately predicts this behavioral intention? The findings of this study will not only contribute to the academic discourse on sustainable consumption and serve as a practical guide for marketers, policymakers, and organic food producers aiming to align their strategies with consumer needs and expectations in India's diverse and dynamic market.

 Methodology Data Description and Collection

This study investigates consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India using advanced ML techniques. Data were meticulously collected through online surveys via Google Forms distributed across various Indian states. The dataset consists of 1,907 respondents, and each survey form consists of 31 statements that capture a wide array of variables relevant to understanding consumer behaviour towards organic food. The questionnaire was designed to gather comprehensive information about the participants, focusing on key factors such as knowledge, motivation, subjective norms, health, price, availability, consumption behaviour, attitude, and behaviour control which were used as different independent variables (Table I) [8]. A five-point Likert scale was used for all the behavioral variables (1: strongly disagree, 5: strongly agree). In this study, the Behavioral Intention towards the consumption of organic food products was considered as the dependent variable.

 Data Pre- Processing

After the initial data collection, the data were preprocessed by removing the null-valued rows and outlier removal using the Interquartile Range (IQR) Technique in XLSTAT. Further, the pre-processed data was cleaned to generate the final dataset, which was further divided into training and testing sets at a ratio of $80: 20$. For classification purposes, the output variable was binarized using a threshold criterion: values greater than 2.5 were coded as 1 (indicating intention to consume organic products), while values less than or equal to 2.5 were coded as 0 (indicating no intention to consume organic products). Construct Item Code Statement

A2 There is a lack of clear labelling indicating organic products.

M3 Better taste and superior quality of organic food motivated me to buy it.

SN2 Social media and online platforms influenced me to start buying organic food products.
SN3 I came to know more about organic food production from worldwide research.
SN4 Government initiatives on organic farming have influenced me.

C. Model Framework

Model framework

Seven ML models, namely Random Forest, SVM, Logistic Regression, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes and Neural Network, are implemented in the study, and different performance metrics were implemented to validate the models (Fig. 1). The models were selected to conduct a comprehensive comparison across a diverse range of ML techniques. This was achieved by choosing a representative algorithm from each major classification family, such as treebased, linear, probabilistic, and neural network methods.

Random Forest: It is an ensemble learning method that constructs multiple decision trees during training and outputs the mode of the classes for classification. It improves accuracy and controls overfitting by averaging multiple trees [9].

Support Vector Machine (SVM): SVM is a supervised learning model that finds the hyperplane that best separates the feature space classes. It is effective in high-dimensional spaces and uses kernel functions to handle non-linear relationships [10].

Logistic Regression: It is a linear model used for binary classification. It estimates the probability that a given input belongs to a particular class by applying a logistic function to a linear combination of input features [11].

K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): KNN is a simple, instancebased learning algorithm that classifies a data point based on the majority class among its k-nearest neighbours in the feature space. It is intuitive and effective for small datasets [12].

Decision Tree: A Decision Tree is a tree-structured model where each node represents a feature, each branch represents a decision rule, and each leaf represents an outcome. It is easy to interpret and can handle numerical and categorical data [9].

Naive Bayes: It is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem. It assumes independence between features and is particularly effective for high-dimensional data. It is simple, fast, and works well for text classification tasks [13].

Neural Network: A Neural Network consists of interconnected layers of neurons, with each connection having a weight that adjusts during training. It can model complex non-linear relationships and is particularly powerful for image and speech recognition tasks [14].

Further, the performance of the ML models was measured by employing five metrics. The performance metrics were selected on the basis of their relevance and suitability for binary classification problems. The performance metrics used in this study are Accuracy, F1 Score, Precision, Recall, Area under the receiver operating curve (AUC), and finally, the mean rank of the model was reported.

 Results and Discussion Comparisons of Ml Techniques

The out-of-sample performance of seven supervised classifiers on our binary organic food consumption behavioural intention was reported in Table II. We evaluate each model using five standard metrics: Accuracy, F1-Score, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity) and AUC-ROC. The Decision Tree achieves the highest overall discriminative power (AUC$\text{ROC}=0.556$). It leads in Accuracy (57.3%) and balanced F1-

Score (0.643), indicating its ability to classify both positive and negative intent at various thresholds correctly. The Random Forest yields the strongest F1-Score (0.713) and exceptional Recall (92.6%), demonstrating that it captures nearly all true positive cases, albeit at the expense of lower Precision (58.0%) and a sub-par AUC-ROC (0.496), suggesting poor probability calibration. The Neural Network offers a close second in F1-Score (0.648) with balanced Precision and Recall (59.9 % and 70.5 %, respectively), while Logistic Regression and SVM deliver moderate but consistent performance across all metrics. KNN and Naive Bayes perform least effectively, with Accuracy near random-guess levels (50.0 % and 49.3%) and lower F1-scores (0.521 and 0.538).

The ROC-curve plot (Fig. 2) overlays the true-positive vs. false-positive trade-off for seven classifiers against the 45° "chance" line ($\text{AUC}=0.50$). SVM achieves the highest area under the curve (AUC $=0.55$), indicating it best distinguishes between positive and negative purchase intentions among the tested models. KNN (AUC $=0.53$) and Random Forest (AUC $=0.52)$ follow closely, showing only modest gains over random guessing. Decision Tree ($\text{AUC}=0.50$) performs at the chance level, while Logistic Regression ($\text{AUC}=0.46$), Naive Bayes (AUC $=0.48$), and Neural Network (AUC $=0.43$) fall below or barely above the baseline, reflecting poor discrimination. These results imply that if the objective is maximal detection of potential organic-food consumers, Random Forest is preferable. The Decision Tree is the superior choice if overall classification quality and thresholdagnostic ranking matter most. Comparison of ml models

Roc curves for different ml models

 Comparisons of the Constructs in Different Models

The Built-in feature importance in scikit-learn implementation (Python) was used to identify and rank the independent variables in each model. Table III reports each model's ranked importance of the nine variables. Across treebased and linear classifiers in Random Forest, SVM, and Logistic Regression, Price consistently emerges as the top predictor (rank 1), followed by Motivation in Random Forest and Consumption Behaviour in both SVM and Logistic Regression. This indicates that the willingness to pay more for organic products plays a crucial role in purchase or consumption intention in these models [15]. Previous literature has also identified price as a major challenge that stops people from buying organic food products [16]. Therefore, reducing the price can further attract more consumers towards organic food and encourage farmers to move forward with green production. Motivation follows closely with the top four ranks in Random Forest (2), Logistic Regression (4), KNN (2), Decision tree (1), Naive Bayes (4) and Neural network (4), highlighting the role of motivation towards health and environmental concern of an individual in intention to buy it [17]. It corroborates a study by [18], where motivation played one of the most crucial roles in behavioral intention to purchase organic food products according to the Theory of Planned Behaviour and Protection Motivation Theory. Subjective Norms also rank among the top five for SVM (3), Logistic regression (5), KNN (5), Decision tree (5) and Naive Bayes (3), underscoring the role of social influence in purchase intent [19] [20]. However, a study by [21] found subjective norms to have barely a significant effect on the purchase intention of consumers. In contrast, Knowledge uniformly occupies the lowest ranks across almost all models, indicating that it contributes little to predictive performance [22]. It suggested that knowledge increases trust and improves attitudes towards organic food products, but several challenges, such as price, can limit the intention. However, the Neural Network uniquely elevates Availability to the highest rank (1), suggesting its sensitivity to market-access variables. In contrast, the KNN classifier highlights Behaviour Control (1) above all others, even though they showed lower ranks in all other models. These divergences reveal that different algorithms emphasize distinct secondary factors such as social norms, accessibility, or self-efficacy when modelling the consumer intention. Conclusion

This study utilized advanced ML techniques to analyze consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India, leveraging data collected from 1,907 participants across various states, which, upon cleaning, came up to 1704. Key factors such as Knowledge, Motivation, Subjective norms, Health, Price, Availability, Consumption Behaviour, Attitudes, and Behaviour Control were examined to understand their influence on organic food consumption.

The comparative analysis of ML models demonstrated that decision trees provided the highest accuracy in predicting future consumption intent, highlighting the potential of ML techniques in uncovering complex patterns in consumer behaviour. These insights offer practical implications for policymakers, marketers, and stakeholders in the organic food sector, enabling them to design targeted interventions, awareness campaigns, and distribution strategies that resonate with consumer values and concerns.

This research offers a predictive tool for industry and makes informed policy decisions. Understanding the nuanced factors affecting organic food consumption can guide efforts to promote sustainable food choices and address consumer concerns effectively. Future research directions include refining these predictive models for practical implementation and exploring additional variables or methodologies to enhance decision-making processes in the organic food market.

K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): KNN is a simple, instance-based learning algorithm that classifies a data point based on the majority class among its k-nearest neighbours in the feature space. It is intuitive and effective for small datasets [12].

Decision Tree: A Decision Tree is a tree-structured model where each node represents a feature, each branch represents a decision rule, and each leaf represents an outcome. It is easy to interpret and can handle numerical and categorical data [9].

Naive Bayes: It is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem. It assumes independence between features and is particularly effective for high-dimensional data. It is simple, fast, and works well for text classification tasks [13].

Neural Network: A Neural Network consists of interconnected layers of neurons, with each connection having a weight that adjusts during training. It can model complex non-linear relationships and is particularly powerful for image and speech recognition tasks [14].

Further, the performance of the ML models was measured by employing five metrics. The performance metrics were selected on the basis of their relevance and suitability for binary classification problems. The performance metrics used in this study are Accuracy, F1 Score, Precision, Recall, Area under the receiver operating curve (AUC), and finally, the mean rank of the model was reported.

 Results and Discussion Comparisons of ML Techniques

The out-of-sample performance of seven supervised classifiers on our binary organic food consumption behavioural intention was reported in Table II. We evaluate each model using five standard metrics: Accuracy, F1-Score, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity) and AUC-ROC. The Decision Tree achieves the highest overall discriminative power (AUC-ROC=0.556). It leads in Accuracy (57.3%) and balanced F1-Score (0.643), indicating its ability to classify both positive and negative intent at various thresholds correctly. The Random Forest yields the strongest F1-Score (0.713) and exceptional Recall (92.6%), demonstrating that it captures nearly all true positive cases, albeit at the expense of lower Precision (58.0%) and a sub-par AUC-ROC (0.496), suggesting poor probability calibration. The Neural Network offers a close second in F1-Score (0.648) with balanced Precision and Recall (59.9 % and 70.5 %, respectively), while Logistic Regression and SVM deliver moderate but consistent performance across all metrics. KNN and Naive Bayes perform least effectively, with Accuracy near random-guess levels (50.0 % and 49.3%) and lower F1-scores (0.521 and 0.538).

The ROC-curve plot (Fig. 2) overlays the true-positive vs. false-positive trade-off for seven classifiers against the 45° “chance” line ($\text{AUC}=0.50$). SVM achieves the highest area under the curve (AUC $=0.55$), indicating it best distinguishes between positive and negative purchase intentions among the tested models. KNN (AUC $=0.53$) and Random Forest (AUC $=0.52)$ follow closely, showing only modest gains over random guessing. Decision Tree ($\text{AUC}=0.50$) performs at the chance level, while Logistic Regression ($\text{AUC}=0.46$), Naive Bayes (AUC $=0.48$), and Neural Network (AUC $=0.43$) fall below or barely above the baseline, reflecting poor discrimination. These results imply that if the objective is maximal detection of potential organic-food consumers, Random Forest is preferable. The Decision Tree is the superior choice if overall classification quality and threshold-agnostic ranking matter most. Comparison of ml models

 Comparisons of the Constructs in Different Models

The Built-in feature importance in scikit-learn implementation (Python) was used to identify and rank the independent variables in each model. Table III reports each model's ranked importance of the nine variables. Across tree-based and linear classifiers in Random Forest, SVM, and Logistic Regression, Price consistently emerges as the top predictor (rank 1), followed by Motivation in Random Forest and Consumption Behaviour in both SVM and Logistic Regression. This indicates that the willingness to pay more for organic products plays a crucial role in purchase or consumption intention in these models [15]. Previous literature has also identified price as a major challenge that stops people from buying organic food products [16]. Therefore, reducing the price can further attract more consumers towards organic food and encourage farmers to move forward with green production. Motivation follows closely with the top four ranks in Random Forest (2), Logistic Regression (4), KNN (2), Decision tree (1), Naive Bayes (4) and Neural network (4), highlighting the role of motivation towards health and environmental concern of an individual in intention to buy it [17]. It corroborates a study by [18], where motivation played one of the most crucial roles in behavioral intention to purchase organic food products according to the Theory of Planned Behaviour and Protection Motivation Theory. Subjective Norms also rank among the top five for SVM (3), Logistic regression (5), KNN (5), Decision tree (5) and Naive Bayes (3), underscoring the role of social influence in purchase intent [19] [20]. However, a study by [21] found subjective norms to have barely a significant effect on the purchase intention of consumers. In contrast, Knowledge uniformly occupies the lowest ranks across almost all models, indicating that it contributes little to predictive performance [22]. It suggested that knowledge increases trust and improves attitudes towards organic food products, but several challenges, such as price, can limit the intention. However, the Neural Network uniquely elevates Availability to the highest rank (1), suggesting its sensitivity to market-access variables. In contrast, the KNN classifier highlights Behaviour Control (1) above all others, even though they showed lower ranks in all other models. These divergences reveal that different algorithms emphasize distinct secondary factors such as social norms, accessibility, or self-efficacy when modelling the consumer intention. Conclusion

This study utilized advanced ML techniques to analyze consumer behaviour towards organic food consumption in India, leveraging data collected from 1,907 participants across various states, which, upon cleaning, came up to 1704. Key factors such as Knowledge, Motivation, Subjective norms, Health, Price, Availability, Consumption Behaviour, Attitudes, and Behaviour Control were examined to understand their influence on organic food consumption.

The comparative analysis of ML models demonstrated that decision trees provided the highest accuracy in predicting future consumption intent, highlighting the potential of ML techniques in uncovering complex patterns in consumer behaviour. These insights offer practical implications for policymakers, marketers, and stakeholders in the organic food sector, enabling them to design targeted interventions, awareness campaigns, and distribution strategies that resonate with consumer values and concerns.

This research offers a predictive tool for industry and makes informed policy decisions. Understanding the nuanced factors affecting organic food consumption can guide efforts to promote sustainable food choices and address consumer concerns effectively. Future research directions include refining these predictive models for practical implementation and exploring additional variables or methodologies to enhance decision-making processes in the organic food market.
