Automatically identifying the biological processes implicated in cancer development remains one of the fundamental challenges in biomedical research. In this study, we address the task of multi-label classification (MLC) of biomedical abstracts according to the HoC (Hallmarks of Cancer) framework, which organizes key mechanisms involved in tumor progression. To tackle this challenge, we propose a hybrid PubMedBERT-CNN model that combines the contextual understanding of PubMedBERT, a transformer pre-trained on biomedical literature, with convolutional layers tailored for multi-label prediction. Our approach includes rigorous preprocessing steps, such as domainspecific tokenization, label vectorization, and class imbalance mitigation. Evaluated on the HoC dataset, the model achieves an F1-score of 0.89 on the test set, outperforming baseline methods including CNN, SVM, and GHS-NET. Beyond strong overall results, the model also performs consistently well across most individual Hallmarks, confirming its robustness. These findings highlight the potential of transformer-based models, when carefully adapted, to extract structured knowledge from unstructured biomedical texts in oncology research.

 \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{00D7}{\ifmmode\times\else{$\times$}\fi}

Index Terms: Multi-label classification, Natural language processing, PubMedBERT, Contextual representations, CNN.

Introduction

Over the past few years, the exponential growth in biomedical literature has posed a major challenge for researchers seeking to retrieve structured knowledge from unstructured text. Automated classification of text into relevant biomedical categories is one of the primary tasks in biomedical natural language processing (BioNLP) [1], [2]. The task becomes particularly challenging in a multi-label classification (MLC) context, where a document may refer to multiple interdependent biomedical concepts simultaneously.

For instance, consider the following abstract fragment: "Loss of p53 is considered to allow progression of colorectal tumors from the adenoma to the carcinoma stage. Using mice with an intestinal epithelial cell (IEC)-specific p53 deletion, we demonstrate that loss of p53 alone is insufficient to initiate intestinal tumorigenesis but markedly enhances carcinogeninduced tumor incidence and leads to invasive cancer and lymphnode metastasis. Whereas p53 controls DNA damage and IEC survival during the initiation stage, loss of p53 during tumor progression is associated with increased intestinal permeability, causing formation of an NF- $\chi \mathrm{B}$-dependent inflammatory microenvironment and the induction of epithelialmesenchymal transition. Thus, we propose a p53-controlled tumor-suppressive function that is independent of its wellestablished role in cell-cycle regulation, apoptosis, and senescence." This short text refers to five distinct hallmarks of cancer: "Activating invasion and metastasis", "Resisting cell death", "Enabling replicative immortality", "Tumor promoting inflammation", and "Evading growth suppressors". Such examples illustrate the complexity and overlapping nature of cancer-related mechanisms, which motivates the need for models capable of assigning multiple relevant labels to a single text. Several studies have investigated text classification in the context of biomedical texts, such as in the areas of adverse drug reaction (ADR) detection and patient safety report classification. For example, deep neural networks have been used to classify ADRs efficiently, leveraging word embeddings and domain-specific features to recognize fine-grained semantic cues in clinical text [3]. As a result, domain-adapted and deployable models have been created to facilitate real-time classification across various healthcare settings [4].

Contrastive and context-aware learning techniques [2] have also been proposed in recent work to encourage generalization to novel biomedical classes, highlighting the significance of representation learning under zero-shot or low-resource settings. These methods demonstrate that deep learning can support not only accurate classification, but also the discovery of valuable insights in biomedical areas where annotated examples remain scarce.Even with recent advances, the classification of biomedical abstracts based on the HoC, a conceptual structure central to oncology research, remains underexplored. The Hallmarks, or the biological traits that cells acquire during tumor progression, represent a key axis for structuring cancer-related knowledge. Automatically assigning one or more Hallmarks to a scientific abstract could accelerate literature curation, hypothesis generation, and translational insight [5].

In this work, we address the challenge of MLC of biomedical abstracts into the Cancer Hallmarks, with the goal of connecting high-level oncology concepts to textual evidence in the literature. Extending prior work in multilabel biomedical classification and drawing upon current best practices in deep learning, we propose and evaluate an approach specifically designed for this concept-driven classification challenge.

This paper has the following structure: Section II overviews related work on biomedical multi-label classification; Section III describes our proposed method, i.e., dataset description, preprocessing, experimental process, and text representation methods; Section IV explains the experimental setup, i.e., hyperparameter tuning, evaluation measures, and performance results on the HoC dataset; Section V contrasts our approach with the current state-of-the-art models; Section VI presents limitations and future work directions; and Section VII concludes the paper and outlines perspectives.

 Related Work

The biomedical text classification task has been of significant importance over the past few years because of the widespread explosion of biomedical literature. Conventionally, methods of text classification, especially in the biomedical domain, have historically been based on single-label classification models, which are inadequate when a paper pertains to more than one closely related biomedical concept. Consequently, the trend has shifted toward MLC, where it is possible to label one document with multiple relevant tags.

Earlier work from Baker et al. [6], (2016) presented a benchmark dataset for biomedical abstract classification according to the Cancer Hallmarks framework and illustrated the viability of this task on Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with rich, manually crafted linguistic features. They reasoned that SVMs might do well, but the dependency on advanced NLP pipelines introduced issues of scalability and generalizability.

Follow-up work further built upon this using deep learning architectures, i.e., Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), which facilitated end-to-end learning and automated feature representation. CNNs surpassed baseline SVMs by not requiring handcrafted features and learning semantic patterns from text itself [6]. Those models, however, mostly succeeded at modeling local dependencies but did not model contextual relations so well at times.

To address these shortcomings, hybrid architectures have been suggested. For instance, GHS-NET, introduced by Ibrahim et al. [7] is a framework that combines CNNs and Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks for simultaneous extraction of local discriminative features and long-range relationships. GHS-NET outperformed other deep learning methods on various biomedical benchmarks, including the HoC dataset, for both recall and F1-score. This hybridization is only one part of the larger trend in biomedical NLP towards utilizing several deep learning components to deliver excellent performance on multi-label tasks.

Transformer models like BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT have also transformed biomedical text classification. Such pre-trained language models fine-tuned on domainspecific corpora deliver excellent contextual representation, greatly boosting the ability to represent fine-grained biomedical semantics. Kumawat et al. [8] emphasized the superiority of PubMedBERT over MLC tasks, especially trained on domain-specialized data like the Chemical Exposure Information (CEI) and Hallmarks of Cancer (HoC), and outperforming conventional and embedding-based models in the process.

Meanwhile, MLC has been utilized in other biomedical subdomains like COVID-19 literature topic annotation. Chen et al. [9] introduced the BioCreative VII LitCovid Track, which addressed the challenges of automatically assigning multiple thematic labels to COVID-19-related publications. Their work emphasized the complexity of this task and the necessity for scalable, accurate solutions. Notably, most participating teams in the track employed transformer-based models, reflecting the growing reliance on deep contextualized language models for biomedical text classification.

Other studies have focused on improving classification via better text representation strategies. Mustafa et al. [10], proposed a Word2Vec-based approach to capture semantic similarity for MLC, showing that embedding-based methods outperform traditional TF-IDF or Bag-of-Words representations. This line of work emphasizes the importance of semanticaware representations, especially in domains like biomedical science where terminology is complex and nuanced.

Despite recent progress, the classification of biomedical abstracts according to the HoC remains a relatively underexplored yet crucial task for structuring knowledge in oncology. While several methods have been applied to this problem, the conceptual and biological specificity of the Hallmarks framework introduces challenges that are not fully addressed by generic MLC approaches. This research seeks to fill this gap by applying previous methods in this context, particularly as an attempt to better capture the semantic dimensions inherent in scientific texts on cancer.

 Proposed Work

This section describes the dataset used, the experimented task in the context of the research work, and provides a step-by-step description of the various stages of this task.

Dataset Description

The corpus of our study is the Hallmarks of Cancer (HoC) corpus. This human-annotated biomedical corpus was initially proposed by Baker et al. (2016) [11] and processed 1,580 PubMed abstracts annotated as per ten identified cancer hallmarks, which link PubMed scientific abstracts to the ten HoC defined in the seminal papers by Hanahan and Weinberg (2000, 2011). It is one of the first publicly available datasets to explicitly address MLC according to this oncological framework. Descriptive statistics of the hallmarks of cancer dataset
Dataset Abstracts Avg. Len. Unique Labels Lbls/Abs

The ten Hallmarks represented in the dataset are:

Sustaining proliferative signaling

Avoiding immune destruction

A quick inspection of these counts indicates that the classes are not fully balanced: for example, Sustaining proliferative signaling appears in 462 abstracts, whereas Cellular energetics and Enabling replicative immortality appear in only around 100 each. Such imbalance may affect model performance and motivates the use of techniques like class weighting.

 Experimental Workflow

The experimental workflow adopted in this study follows a sequence of key stages, starting with the leading dataset. This is followed by preprocessing, tokenization using a biomedical language model, model architecture design, and finally, MLC. Each stage in the pipeline contributes critically to the overall effectiveness and accuracy of the classification process. An overview of this workflow is clarified in Figure 1.

Overview of the experimental workflow.

Data cleaning and preprocessing: To ensure highquality input for the classification task, we applied a series of preprocessing steps to the textual data. First, all abstracts were converted to lowercase, a standard normalization step that helps eliminate inconsistencies due to case sensitivity and ensures that semantically identical words (e.g., 'Cancer' and 'cancer') are treated equivalently.

Next, we performed character filtering using regular expressions. This involved removing digits, punctuation, and special characters that are not relevant to biomedical semantics. By retaining only alphabetic characters and select symbols used in domain-specific terms, we reduced noise and improved the clarity of the textual content.

To further refine the text, common English stopwords were removed using the NLTK stopwords list. These highfrequency words (such as "the", "and", "of") typically do not carry significant semantic meaning and can obscure important signals in text classification tasks, especially when dealing with domain-specific terminology.

Finally, the remaining words were lemmatized using the WordNet lemmatizer. This step reduces inflected or derived words to their base forms (e.g., "regulating" to "regulate", "mutations" to "mutation"), which helps in capturing the core meaning of terms and improving generalization during model training.

In parallel, for each abstract, a binary vector representation of labels was constructed. Each vector indicates the presence (1) or absence (0) of each of the ten Cancer Hallmarks, thus enabling the MLC framework used in our experiments.

Tokenization and Dataset Preparation: Before text can be processed by a transformer-based language model, it must first be converted into a format the model can understand. This is the role of the tokenizer, which breaks down raw text into smaller units called tokens. In modern

NLP, especially with transformer architectures, tokenization is a critical step because models do not operate on raw words or characters, but rather on sequences of numerical token IDs. Effective tokenization ensures consistent representation of terms, including rare or domain-specific words, and directly influences model performance.

In our study, we employ the tokenizer associated with PubMedBERT, specifically the version "microsoft/BiomedNLP-PubMedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext", which has been pre-trained on a large corpus of PubMed abstracts and full-text articles. This tokenizer is based on the WordPiece algorithm and is optimized for biomedical terminology. It splits words into subword units, allowing the model to handle rare and complex medical terms more effectively by reducing out-of-vocabulary issues.

Using a tokenizer trained on biomedical data ensures that domain-specific language is segmented and represented in a semantically meaningful way. This not only improves the quality of the input embeddings but also enhances the model's ability to extract relevant information for the MLC of cancer-related abstracts.

Once tokenization is completed, the data must be structured appropriately for model input. To achieve this, we implemented a custom PyTorch Dataset class named CancerDataset. This class prepares each sample by applying the PubMedBERT tokenizer to generate two required inputs: input_ids, which represents the tokenized abstract, and attention_mask, which signals to the model which tokens should be attended to. In addition, the class formats the label vectors corresponding to each sample. These labels are represented as binary vectors indicating the presence or absence of each Cancer Hallmark, and are converted into floating-point tensors. This structure enables efficient batching, data loading, and training in a MLC setting.

 Model Architecture and Multi-Label Classification:

The classification model builds on the PubMedBERT transformer, pre-trained on a large biomedical corpus, and adds a lightweight CNN head for multi-label prediction. During the forward pass, tokenized inputs (input_ids and attention_mask) are first encoded by PubMedBERT, yielding a sequence of contextual embeddings of shape (batch_size, seq_len, hidden_size).

These embeddings are then transposed to (batch_size, hidden_size, seq_len) so they can be processed by 1D convolutional layers. Multiple convolutional filters of sizes 3, 4, and 5 scan these embeddings to capture local patterns of varying lengths. Each convolution's output is passed through a max-pooling operation along the sequence dimension, producing for each filter a tensor of shape (batch_size, num_filters) that retains the most salient features.

The pooled outputs from all filters are concatenated into a single vector of size num_filters × number_of_kernels. A dropout layer (rate $=0.3$) is applied to this vector to mitigate overfitting. Finally, a linear layer projects the resulting representation into a space whose dimensionality equals the number of classes (the ten Cancer Hallmarks).

A sigmoid activation is applied to the linear outputs to produce independent probabilities for each class, allowing the model to assign multiple labels to a single abstract. Training minimizes binary cross-entropy loss, computed separately for each label.

 Evaluation Protocol and Results

This section outlines the evaluation protocol used to assess our model's performance. We describe the execution environment, the metrics employed for multi-label evaluation, and the results obtained on the HoC dataset. These elements provide a comprehensive view of the model's effectiveness and reproducibility.

Execution Environment

The experiments were conducted in the environment detailed in Table III. Execution environment specifications
Component Specification
Platform Google Colab
GPU Tesla T4
RAM 16 GB
Disk Storage Approximately 100 GB
Programming Language Python
Libraries PyTorch, Transformers (HuggingFace)
Training Setup

The data were split into 80 % train and 20 % test. 10 % of the training set was also reserved as a validation set for tracking progress while training. The input sequences were padded or truncated to a maximum of 256 tokens. A dropout of 0.3 was also applied to the concatenated CNN feature vector in an attempt to avoid overfitting. It was then optimized with the AdamW optimizer and a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$ and weight decay of 0.01. Training was performed in mini-batches of 16 samples for 20 epochs, with early stopping based on the validation F1-score.

Table IV summarizes both the training parameters and key architectural choices of the proposed PubMedBERT-CNN model, including convolutional filter settings and the pooling mechanism. Training, architecture, and data-split hyperparameters of the pubmedbert-cnn model.
Hyperparameter Value
Train 80%
Test 20%
Validation (of training) 10%
Dropout rate 0.3
Optimizer AdamW ($\operatorname{lr}=2 \times 10^{-5}$, weight decay $=0.01$)
Max sequence length 256 tokens (with padding/truncation)
Batch size 16
Number of epochs 20
CNN filters per kernel 100
Kernel sizes [3], [4], [5]
Pooling strategy 1D max-pooling per filter
Evaluation Metrics

To evaluate the performance of our MLC model, we adopted micro-averaged metrics, which aggregate the contributions of all classes before computing the overall score. This strategy is particularly suitable for imbalanced datasets, as it treats each individual prediction equally, regardless of class frequency.

The micro-averaged Recall, Precision, and F1-Score can be computed using Equations 1, 2, and 3, respectively: \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} \text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}}{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}+F N_{k}} \\ \text { Precision }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}}{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}+F P_{k}} \\ F 1-\text { Score }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{2 \cdot \text { Precision }_{\text {micro }} \cdot \text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}}{\text { Precision }_{\text {micro }}+\text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}} \end{gathered} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Where:

$T P_{k}$ is the number of true positives for class $k$,

$F P_{k}$ is the number of false positives for class $k$,

$F N_{k}$ is the number of false negatives for class $k$,

and $L$ is the set of all labels (i.e., the 10 Cancer Hallmarks).

In addition to these, we also report the micro-AUC, which represents the area under the ROC curve, computed across all instances and classes collectively.

 Results

Table V shows the overall performance of the proposed PubMedBERT + CNN classifier, which achieves a precision of 0.85, a recall of 0.93, and an F1-score of 0.89, indicating strong predictive capacity across multiple cancer-related categories. Final performance of the proposed model on the test set

 Comparison with Existing Approaches

To evaluate the effectiveness of our model, we compared its performance against several existing state-of-the-art methods applied to the same HoC dataset. These models include CNN-based architectures, hybrid shallow networks like GHSNET, Word2Vec-based classifiers, and transformer models. Table VII summarizes the performance in terms of F1-score, precision, and recall. As shown, our proposed model achieves superior results, particularly in recall and F1-score, demonstrating its ability to generalize across diverse cancer-related labels. Discussion

The experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid PubMedBERT + CNN model delivers strong performance for MLC across the ten HoC. The high recall and F1-scores reflect its ability to correctly identify relevant labels, even when multiple hallmarks co-occur or are expressed implicitly in biomedical abstracts. However, a closer look at per-class metrics reveals variability in performance. For instance, the lower F1-score for Sustaining proliferative signaling may be due to ambiguous language in the abstracts or underrepresentation in the training data. Addressing this could involve targeted data augmentation or balancing the dataset.

While PubMedBERT's contextual embeddings capture rich biomedical semantics, further improvements may be achieved by incorporating external domain knowledge, such as biomedical ontologies or knowledge graphs. Embedding entity definitions or semantic relationships directly into the model inputs could help resolve subtle distinctions between overlapping labels. Another limitation is the model's generalizability. Although it performs well on the HoC dataset, its effectiveness on other cancer-related corpora or biomedical subdomains remains unexplored. Future research should therefore explore domain adaptation techniques, zero-shot learning, and semisupervised approaches, as well as testing the model on fulltext articles or clinical reports, to assess its robustness and practical utility across broader biomedical contexts.

 Conclusion and Perspectives

In this study, we addressed the task of MLC of biomedical abstracts according to the ten HoC by fine-tuning the PubMedBERT transformer. Our hybrid architecture, which combines PubMedBERT with a CNN classification head, achieved high precision, recall, and F 1 -scores, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods on the HoC dataset. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of contextualized language models in handling complex classification tasks in the biomedical domain and highlight their potential for extracting structured insights from unstructured biomedical text.

As part of the future work, we will first investigate dataset augmentation strategies (synonym replacement, backtranslation, and mixup) to enrich the HoC corpus and solve the class imbalance problem. We will then test our PubMedBERTCNN model on a range of biomedical datasets to see how well it generalizes to new domains. Lastly, we will incorporate zeroshot learning methods so that the model can identify new or future landmarks without extra annotating steps, evermore taking advantage of its usage in fast-paced biomedical research.

Automatically identifying the biological processes implicated in cancer development remains one of the fundamental challenges in biomedical research. In this study, we address the task of multi-label classification (MLC) of biomedical abstracts according to the HoC (Hallmarks of Cancer) framework, which organizes key mechanisms involved in tumor progression. To tackle this challenge, we propose a hybrid PubMedBERT-CNN model that combines the contextual understanding of PubMedBERT, a transformer pre-trained on biomedical literature, with convolutional layers tailored for multi-label prediction. Our approach includes rigorous preprocessing steps, such as domain-specific tokenization, label vectorization, and class imbalance mitigation. Evaluated on the HoC dataset, the model achieves an F1-score of 0.89 on the test set, outperforming baseline methods including CNN, SVM, and GHS-NET. Beyond strong overall results, the model also performs consistently well across most individual Hallmarks, confirming its robustness. These findings highlight the potential of transformer-based models, when carefully adapted, to extract structured knowledge from unstructured biomedical texts in oncology research.

 Multi-label classification Natural language processing PubMedBERT Contextual representations CNN Introduction

Over the past few years, the exponential growth in biomedical literature has posed a major challenge for researchers seeking to retrieve structured knowledge from unstructured text. Automated classification of text into relevant biomedical categories is one of the primary tasks in biomedical natural language processing (BioNLP) [1], [2]. The task becomes particularly challenging in a multi-label classification (MLC) context, where a document may refer to multiple interdependent biomedical concepts simultaneously.

For instance, consider the following abstract fragment: “Loss of p53 is considered to allow progression of colorectal tumors from the adenoma to the carcinoma stage. Using mice with an intestinal epithelial cell (IEC)-specific p53 deletion, we demonstrate that loss of p53 alone is insufficient to initiate intestinal tumorigenesis but markedly enhances carcinogeninduced tumor incidence and leads to invasive cancer and lymphnode metastasis. Whereas p53 controls DNA damage and IEC survival during the initiation stage, loss of p53 during tumor progression is associated with increased intestinal permeability, causing formation of an NF- $\chi \mathrm{B}$-dependent inflammatory microenvironment and the induction of epithelialmesenchymal transition. Thus, we propose a p53-controlled tumor-suppressive function that is independent of its well-established role in cell-cycle regulation, apoptosis, and senescence.” This short text refers to five distinct hallmarks of cancer: “Activating invasion and metastasis”, “Resisting cell death”, “Enabling replicative immortality”, “Tumor promoting inflammation”, and “Evading growth suppressors”. Such examples illustrate the complexity and overlapping nature of cancer-related mechanisms, which motivates the need for models capable of assigning multiple relevant labels to a single text. Several studies have investigated text classification in the context of biomedical texts, such as in the areas of adverse drug reaction (ADR) detection and patient safety report classification. For example, deep neural networks have been used to classify ADRs efficiently, leveraging word embeddings and domain-specific features to recognize fine-grained semantic cues in clinical text [3]. As a result, domain-adapted and deployable models have been created to facilitate real-time classification across various healthcare settings [4].

Contrastive and context-aware learning techniques [2] have also been proposed in recent work to encourage generalization to novel biomedical classes, highlighting the significance of representation learning under zero-shot or low-resource settings. These methods demonstrate that deep learning can support not only accurate classification, but also the discovery of valuable insights in biomedical areas where annotated examples remain scarce.Even with recent advances, the classification of biomedical abstracts based on the HoC, a conceptual structure central to oncology research, remains underexplored. The Hallmarks, or the biological traits that cells acquire during tumor progression, represent a key axis for structuring cancer-related knowledge. Automatically assigning one or more Hallmarks to a scientific abstract could accelerate literature curation, hypothesis generation, and translational insight [5].

In this work, we address the challenge of MLC of biomedical abstracts into the Cancer Hallmarks, with the goal of connecting high-level oncology concepts to textual evidence in the literature. Extending prior work in multi-label biomedical classification and drawing upon current best practices in deep learning, we propose and evaluate an approach specifically designed for this concept-driven classification challenge.

This paper has the following structure: Section II overviews related work on biomedical multi-label classification; Section III describes our proposed method, i.e., dataset description, preprocessing, experimental process, and text representation methods; Section IV explains the experimental setup, i.e., hyperparameter tuning, evaluation measures, and performance results on the HoC dataset; Section V contrasts our approach with the current state-of-the-art models; Section VI presents limitations and future work directions; and Section VII concludes the paper and outlines perspectives.

 Related Work

The biomedical text classification task has been of significant importance over the past few years because of the widespread explosion of biomedical literature. Conventionally, methods of text classification, especially in the biomedical domain, have historically been based on single-label classification models, which are inadequate when a paper pertains to more than one closely related biomedical concept. Consequently, the trend has shifted toward MLC, where it is possible to label one document with multiple relevant tags.

Earlier work from Baker et al. [6], (2016) presented a benchmark dataset for biomedical abstract classification according to the Cancer Hallmarks framework and illustrated the viability of this task on Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with rich, manually crafted linguistic features. They reasoned that SVMs might do well, but the dependency on advanced NLP pipelines introduced issues of scalability and generalizability.

Follow-up work further built upon this using deep learning architectures, i.e., Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), which facilitated end-to-end learning and automated feature representation. CNNs surpassed baseline SVMs by not requiring handcrafted features and learning semantic patterns from text itself [6]. Those models, however, mostly succeeded at modeling local dependencies but did not model contextual relations so well at times.

To address these shortcomings, hybrid architectures have been suggested. For instance, GHS-NET, introduced by Ibrahim et al. [7] is a framework that combines CNNs and Bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks for simultaneous extraction of local discriminative features and long-range relationships. GHS-NET outperformed other deep learning methods on various biomedical benchmarks, including the HoC dataset, for both recall and F1-score. This hybridization is only one part of the larger trend in biomedical NLP towards utilizing several deep learning components to deliver excellent performance on multi-label tasks.

Transformer models like BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT have also transformed biomedical text classification. Such pre-trained language models fine-tuned on domain-specific corpora deliver excellent contextual representation, greatly boosting the ability to represent fine-grained biomedical semantics. Kumawat et al. [8] emphasized the superiority of PubMedBERT over MLC tasks, especially trained on domain-specialized data like the Chemical Exposure Information (CEI) and Hallmarks of Cancer (HoC), and outperforming conventional and embedding-based models in the process.

Meanwhile, MLC has been utilized in other biomedical subdomains like COVID-19 literature topic annotation. Chen et al. [9] introduced the BioCreative VII LitCovid Track, which addressed the challenges of automatically assigning multiple thematic labels to COVID-19-related publications. Their work emphasized the complexity of this task and the necessity for scalable, accurate solutions. Notably, most participating teams in the track employed transformer-based models, reflecting the growing reliance on deep contextualized language models for biomedical text classification.

Other studies have focused on improving classification via better text representation strategies. Mustafa et al. [10], proposed a Word2Vec-based approach to capture semantic similarity for MLC, showing that embedding-based methods outperform traditional TF-IDF or Bag-of-Words representations. This line of work emphasizes the importance of semanticaware representations, especially in domains like biomedical science where terminology is complex and nuanced.

Despite recent progress, the classification of biomedical abstracts according to the HoC remains a relatively underexplored yet crucial task for structuring knowledge in oncology. While several methods have been applied to this problem, the conceptual and biological specificity of the Hallmarks framework introduces challenges that are not fully addressed by generic MLC approaches. This research seeks to fill this gap by applying previous methods in this context, particularly as an attempt to better capture the semantic dimensions inherent in scientific texts on cancer.

 Proposed Work

This section describes the dataset used, the experimented task in the context of the research work, and provides a step-by-step description of the various stages of this task.

Dataset Description

The corpus of our study is the Hallmarks of Cancer (HoC) corpus. This human-annotated biomedical corpus was initially proposed by Baker et al. (2016) [11] and processed 1,580 PubMed abstracts annotated as per ten identified cancer hallmarks, which link PubMed scientific abstracts to the ten HoC defined in the seminal papers by Hanahan and Weinberg (2000, 2011). It is one of the first publicly available datasets to explicitly address MLC according to this oncological framework. Descriptive statistics of the hallmarks of cancer dataset
Dataset Abstracts Avg. Len. Unique Labels Lbls/Abs
Hallmarks of Cancer [11] 1582 242 10 1.56

The ten Hallmarks represented in the dataset are:

Inducing angiogenesis

Sustaining proliferative signaling

Resisting cell death

Evading growth suppressors

Genomic instability and mutation

Enabling replicative immortality

Activating invasion and metastasis

Tumor promoting inflammation

Cellular energetics

Avoiding immune destruction

Table II shows the number of documents annotated with each hallmark. Number of documents per hallmark class in the hoc corpus
Class Documents
Inducing angiogenesis 143
Sustaining proliferative signaling 462
Resisting cell death 429
Evading growth suppressors 240
Genomic instability and mutation 333
Enabling replicative immortality 115
Activating invasion and metastasis 291
Tumor promoting inflammation 240
Cellular energetics 105
Avoiding immune destruction 108

A quick inspection of these counts indicates that the classes are not fully balanced: for example, Sustaining proliferative signaling appears in 462 abstracts, whereas Cellular energetics and Enabling replicative immortality appear in only around 100 each. Such imbalance may affect model performance and motivates the use of techniques like class weighting.

 Experimental Workflow

The experimental workflow adopted in this study follows a sequence of key stages, starting with the leading dataset. This is followed by preprocessing, tokenization using a biomedical language model, model architecture design, and finally, MLC. Each stage in the pipeline contributes critically to the overall effectiveness and accuracy of the classification process. An overview of this workflow is clarified in Figure 1.

Overview of the experimental workflow.

Data cleaning and preprocessing:

To ensure highquality input for the classification task, we applied a series of preprocessing steps to the textual data. First, all abstracts were converted to lowercase, a standard normalization step that helps eliminate inconsistencies due to case sensitivity and ensures that semantically identical words (e.g., 'Cancer' and 'cancer') are treated equivalently.

Next, we performed character filtering using regular expressions. This involved removing digits, punctuation, and special characters that are not relevant to biomedical semantics. By retaining only alphabetic characters and select symbols used in domain-specific terms, we reduced noise and improved the clarity of the textual content.

To further refine the text, common English stopwords were removed using the NLTK stopwords list. These high-frequency words (such as “the”, “and”, “of”) typically do not carry significant semantic meaning and can obscure important signals in text classification tasks, especially when dealing with domain-specific terminology.

Finally, the remaining words were lemmatized using the WordNet lemmatizer. This step reduces inflected or derived words to their base forms (e.g., “regulating” to “regulate”, “mutations” to “mutation”), which helps in capturing the core meaning of terms and improving generalization during model training.

In parallel, for each abstract, a binary vector representation of labels was constructed. Each vector indicates the presence (1) or absence (0) of each of the ten Cancer Hallmarks, thus enabling the MLC framework used in our experiments.

 Tokenization and Dataset Preparation:

Before text can be processed by a transformer-based language model, it must first be converted into a format the model can understand. This is the role of the tokenizer, which breaks down raw text into smaller units called tokens. In modern NLP, especially with transformer architectures, tokenization is a critical step because models do not operate on raw words or characters, but rather on sequences of numerical token IDs. Effective tokenization ensures consistent representation of terms, including rare or domain-specific words, and directly influences model performance.

In our study, we employ the tokenizer associated with PubMedBERT, specifically the version “microsoft/BiomedNLP-PubMedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext”, which has been pre-trained on a large corpus of PubMed abstracts and full-text articles. This tokenizer is based on the WordPiece algorithm and is optimized for biomedical terminology. It splits words into subword units, allowing the model to handle rare and complex medical terms more effectively by reducing out-of-vocabulary issues.

Using a tokenizer trained on biomedical data ensures that domain-specific language is segmented and represented in a semantically meaningful way. This not only improves the quality of the input embeddings but also enhances the model's ability to extract relevant information for the MLC of cancer-related abstracts.

Once tokenization is completed, the data must be structured appropriately for model input. To achieve this, we implemented a custom PyTorch Dataset class named CancerDataset. This class prepares each sample by applying the PubMedBERT tokenizer to generate two required inputs: input_ids, which represents the tokenized abstract, and attention_mask, which signals to the model which tokens should be attended to. In addition, the class formats the label vectors corresponding to each sample. These labels are represented as binary vectors indicating the presence or absence of each Cancer Hallmark, and are converted into floating-point tensors. This structure enables efficient batching, data loading, and training in a MLC setting.

 Model Architecture and Multi-Label Classification:

The classification model builds on the PubMedBERT transformer, pre-trained on a large biomedical corpus, and adds a lightweight CNN head for multi-label prediction. During the forward pass, tokenized inputs (input_ids and attention_mask) are first encoded by PubMedBERT, yielding a sequence of contextual embeddings of shape (batch_size, seq_len, hidden_size).

These embeddings are then transposed to (batch_size, hidden_size, seq_len) so they can be processed by 1D convolutional layers. Multiple convolutional filters of sizes 3, 4, and 5 scan these embeddings to capture local patterns of varying lengths. Each convolution's output is passed through a max-pooling operation along the sequence dimension, producing for each filter a tensor of shape (batch_size, num_filters) that retains the most salient features.

The pooled outputs from all filters are concatenated into a single vector of size num_filters × number_of_kernels. A dropout layer (rate $=0.3$) is applied to this vector to mitigate overfitting. Finally, a linear layer projects the resulting representation into a space whose dimensionality equals the number of classes (the ten Cancer Hallmarks).

A sigmoid activation is applied to the linear outputs to produce independent probabilities for each class, allowing the model to assign multiple labels to a single abstract. Training minimizes binary cross-entropy loss, computed separately for each label.

 Evaluation Protocol and Results

This section outlines the evaluation protocol used to assess our model's performance. We describe the execution environment, the metrics employed for multi-label evaluation, and the results obtained on the HoC dataset. These elements provide a comprehensive view of the model's effectiveness and reproducibility.

Execution Environment

The experiments were conducted in the environment detailed in Table III. Execution environment specifications
Component Specification
Platform Google Colab
GPU Tesla T4
RAM 16 GB
Disk Storage Approximately 100 GB
Programming Language Python
Libraries PyTorch, Transformers (HuggingFace)

Training Setup

The data were split into 80 % train and 20 % test. 10 % of the training set was also reserved as a validation set for tracking progress while training. The input sequences were padded or truncated to a maximum of 256 tokens. A dropout of 0.3 was also applied to the concatenated CNN feature vector in an attempt to avoid overfitting. It was then optimized with the AdamW optimizer and a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$ and weight decay of 0.01. Training was performed in mini-batches of 16 samples for 20 epochs, with early stopping based on the validation F1-score.

Table IV summarizes both the training parameters and key architectural choices of the proposed PubMedBERT-CNN model, including convolutional filter settings and the pooling mechanism. Training, architecture, and data-split hyperparameters of the pubmedbert-cnn model.

Kernel sizes [3], [4], [5]

Evaluation Metrics

To evaluate the performance of our MLC model, we adopted micro-averaged metrics, which aggregate the contributions of all classes before computing the overall score. This strategy is particularly suitable for imbalanced datasets, as it treats each individual prediction equally, regardless of class frequency.

The micro-averaged Recall, Precision, and F1-Score can be computed using Equations 1, 2, and 3, respectively: \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} \text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}}{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}+F N_{k}} \tag{1}\\ \text { Precision }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}}{\sum_{k \in L} T P_{k}+F P_{k}} \tag{2}\\ F 1-\text { Score }_{\text {micro }}=\frac{2 \cdot \text { Precision }_{\text {micro }} \cdot \text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}}{\text { Precision }_{\text {micro }}+\text { Recall }_{\text {micro }}} \end{gathered} \tag{3} \end{equation*} Where:

$T P_{k}$ is the number of true positives for class $k$,

$F P_{k}$ is the number of false positives for class $k$,

$F N_{k}$ is the number of false negatives for class $k$,

and $L$ is the set of all labels (i.e., the 10 Cancer Hallmarks).

In addition to these, we also report the micro-AUC, which represents the area under the ROC curve, computed across all instances and classes collectively.

To evaluate the effectiveness of our model, we compared its performance against several existing state-of-the-art methods applied to the same HoC dataset. Discussion

The experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid PubMedBERT + CNN model delivers strong performance for MLC across the ten HoC. The high recall and F1-scores reflect its ability to correctly identify relevant labels, even when multiple hallmarks co-occur or are expressed implicitly in biomedical abstracts. However, a closer look at per-class metrics reveals variability in performance. For instance, the lower F1-score for Sustaining proliferative signaling may be due to ambiguous language in the abstracts or underrepresentation in the training data. Addressing this could involve targeted data augmentation or balancing the dataset.

While PubMedBERT's contextual embeddings capture rich biomedical semantics, further improvements may be achieved by incorporating external domain knowledge, such as biomedical ontologies or knowledge graphs. Embedding entity definitions or semantic relationships directly into the model inputs could help resolve subtle distinctions between overlapping labels. Another limitation is the model's generalizability. Although it performs well on the HoC dataset, its effectiveness on other cancer-related corpora or biomedical subdomains remains unexplored. Future research should therefore explore domain adaptation techniques, zero-shot learning, and semisupervised approaches, as well as testing the model on fulltext articles or clinical reports, to assess its robustness and practical utility across broader biomedical contexts.

 Conclusion and Perspectives

In this study, we addressed the task of MLC of biomedical abstracts according to the ten HoC by fine-tuning the PubMedBERT transformer. Our hybrid architecture, which combines PubMedBERT with a CNN classification head, achieved high precision, recall, and F 1 -scores, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods on the HoC dataset. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of contextualized language models in handling complex classification tasks in the biomedical domain and highlight their potential for extracting structured insights from unstructured biomedical text.

As part of the future work, we will first investigate dataset augmentation strategies (synonym replacement, backtranslation, and mixup) to enrich the HoC corpus and solve the class imbalance problem. We will then test our PubMedBERTCNN model on a range of biomedical datasets to see how well it generalizes to new domains. Lastly, we will incorporate zero-shot learning methods so that the model can identify new or future landmarks without extra annotating steps, evermore taking advantage of its usage in fast-paced biomedical research.

The article is devoted to the development of a methodology for predicting the sustainability and efficiency of organizations based on an innodiversification approach. The authors offer a comprehensive model integrating multifactorial analysis, linear regression and machine learning, which allows taking into account both internal and external factors affecting the activities of organizations. The article presents the stages of model construction, including data collection, preprocessing, analysis, and forecasting. Special attention is paid to the advantages of an innovative diversification approach that ensures the sustainable development of organizations in the context of global economic instability, which is directly relevant to the conference's focus on energy efficiency and sustainable development in agro-industrial and related sectors.

 innodiversification organizational sustainability efficiency multifactor analysis linear regression machine learning forecasting management solutions Introduction (Heading 1)

The relevance of the research is determined by the need to increase the sustainability and efficiency of organizations in the context of global economic instability, growing competition, and the increasing importance of energy and resource efficiency [1]. Modern organizations, particularly in sectors related to agriculture and engineering, face multifaceted challenges related to volatile market conditions, rapid technological innovations, and the imperative to integrate social and environmental responsibilities into their core strategies. The search for robust methodological tools to forecast organizational performance under such conditions remains a pressing issue in economic science [ 2], [3 ].

Analysis of the current state of research reveals significant contributions in the fields of sustainability modeling and performance forecasting. Traditional methods, such as financial analysis [7], cost-benefit analysis [3], [4], and SWOTanalysis [8], provide valuable insights but often lack a holistic view, overlooking the synergistic effects between innovation, diversification, and long-term resilience. Furthermore, the application of advanced analytical techniques, including machine learning, for forecasting in the context of regional economic systems is an area of growing interest [9], [10]. This study aims to contribute to this discourse by justifying and integrating an innodiversification approach with modern quantitative methods.

The purpose of this research is to justify and refine a methodology for constructing a forecasting model for the sustainability and efficiency of organizations. This involves a critical comparative analysis of existing methods, the synthesis of an innodiversification-based framework, and the integration of multifactorial analysis, linear regression, and machine learning techniques to enhance predictive accuracy and practical applicability for managerial decision-making.

 Research Methodology

The proposed methodology for constructing a model for predicting the sustainability and efficiency of organizations is presented in Figure 1. It synthesizes elements from established approaches [3]–[8] into a new, integrated framework centered on the innodiversification paradigm.

In the context of global economic instability and growing competition, organizations of organizations face the need to increase their sustainability and efficiency. This requires the development of a reliable methodology for forecasting key indicators, which will make it possible to make informed management decisions. In this paper, we propose a hypothesis of a methodology for building a model for predicting the sustainability and effectiveness of organizations.

The hypothesis of the methodology is based on the integration of multifactorial analysis, linear regression, machine learning, data clustering and comparative analysis of techniques.

The algorithm of the technique includes five fundamental steps, detailed below.

Comparison of Methods and Justification of the Innodiversification Approach

This initial stage involves a systematic comparison of various methods for selecting and justifying indicators. Based on a comparative analysis (see Table I), the innodiversification approach was selected due to its comprehensive nature, which integrates innovativeness, diversification, and a multi-faceted assessment of sustainability and efficiency.

 Data Collection and Preprocessing

The second stage entails collecting data on key indicators of sustainability and efficiency of organizations. These data may include financial, industrial, social, and environmental metrics. The collected data subsequently undergo preprocessing, including clustering, to ensure quality and consistency for subsequent analysis.

 Multifactorial Analysis

This stage focuses on identifying key internal and external factors affecting the sustainability and efficiency of organizations. The analysis of correlations between these factors and target indicators allows for the determination of the most significant influences and their dynamics over time.

 Linear Regression and Machine Learning

At this stage, linear regression models are constructed for key indicators. The models are evaluated for accuracy using metrics such as the coefficient of determination ($\mathrm{R}^{2}$) and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE). The methodology also incorporates machine learning techniques to account for possible non-linear dependencies and complex interactions between factors, thereby enhancing the robustness of the forecasting model.

 Integration of Results

The final stage involves the integration of results from regression and machine learning models to produce a comprehensive forecast. The forecasts are interpreted considering economic theory and the practical context of the organizations' activities, enabling the formulation of informed management decisions and development scenarios.

Methodology for constructing a forecasting model for the sustainability and efficiency of organizations

 Justification of the Methodology

Integrated approach: the methodology integrates multifactorial analysis, linear regression and machine learning, which allows you to take into account a variety of factors and dependencies. This ensures more accurate and reliable forecasting. Taking into account non-linear dependencies makes it possible to identify complex and nonlinear relationships between factors and targets. This increases the accuracy of forecasts and their adequacy to real conditions. Cross-validation of models ensures their reliability and stability. This helps to increase confidence in forecasts. The forecasting results can be used to make informed management decisions. This helps to increase the sustainability and effectiveness of organizations. The proposed methodology for constructing a forecasting model for the sustainability and effectiveness of organizations is based on the integration of multifactorial analysis, linear and nonlinear regression. This allows you to take into account a variety of factors and dependencies, ensuring the accuracy and reliability of forecasts. The implementation of this methodology helps to increase the competitiveness and sustainability of organizations in the context of global economic instability.

The selection and justification of indicators of sustainability and effectiveness of organizations are key tasks to ensure their long-term development and competitiveness. The selection and justification of indicators of sustainability and efficiency of organizations require the use of comprehensive analysis methods that take into account the specifics of the industry and modern economic conditions. There are many techniques that can be used for this purpose, including financial analysis, cost-benefit analysis, value chain analysis, SWOT analysis, and innodiversification approach.

The selection and justification of indicators for organizational sustainability and efficiency are critical for ensuring long-term development and competitiveness. This requires comprehensive analysis methods that account for industry specifics and contemporary economic conditions. Table I presents a comparison of various methodologies. Comparison of methods for selecting and justifying indicators of sustainability and effectiveness of organizations
Methodology Description Advantages Disadvantages
Cost-benefit analysis [3], [4] Involves estimating the costs and benefits of projects or strategies. Evaluates economic feasibility of solutions. Limited to economic aspects; ignores long-term social/environmen tal consequences.
Value chain analysis [5] Identifies key stages of value creation for optimization. End-product orientation, focuses on process optimization. Lacks consideration of innovative changes and the external environment.
Innodiversificati on Approach [6], [9] Assesses innovative and diversificatio n strategies for sustainable development Comprehensi ve; covers financial, social, environmental, and innovative aspects. Requires significant resources; can be complex to implement.
Financial analysis [7], [10] Assesses financial indicators Simple, accessible, provides Limited perspective on development
(profitability, liquidity, stability). quantitative assessment. prospects; insufficient attention to intangibles and innovation.
SWOTanalysis [8] Assesses internal (Strengths, Weaknesses) and external (Opportuniti es, Threats) factors. Provides a comprehensiv e qualitative understanding Subjective; not always suitable for quantitative assessment.

As a result of a comparative analysis of the five most wellknown methods that can be used to select and justify indicators of sustainability and effectiveness of organizations, an innodiversification approach was preferred. The choice of this approach was based on the advantages of this approach shown in Figure 2 [9]–[10].

The innodiversification approach was preferred due to its integrated nature, which allows organizations to adapt to changing market conditions, enhance competitiveness, and ensure long-term sustainability. Its core advantages are innovativeness, diversification, an integrated approach, social and environmental responsibility, and a focus on sustainable development.

Applying this approach to the consumer cooperation system in the Novosibirsk region, indicators were grouped into three blocks, resulting in the selection of 36 sustainability indicators and 8 efficiency indicators for the period from 2014 to 2023. The analysis revealed a significant improvement in both sustainability and effectiveness over this decade.

As a result of the innodiversification analysis, using the example of the consumer cooperation of the Novosibirsk region, the indicators were grouped into three blocks characterizing the results of the industry, including a period of time, 36 indicators of sustainability and 8 indicators of efficiency.

Over the period from 2014 to 2023, there has been a significant improvement in the sustainability and effectiveness of consumer cooperation.

Sustainability Indicators

The analysis of key sustainability indicators demonstrated positive dynamics:

Growth in Asset Value: the average annual cost of fixed assets (buildings, machinery, vehicles) and working capital showed a substantial increase, indicating a strengthening material and technical base. For instance, the value of buildings and structures (Y1) grew by 2.67 times.

Improved Financial Stability: key financial metrics improved significantly. The average annual amount of funds (Y5) increased markedly. The financial independence coefficient (Y14) rose to 0.94, indicating higher resilience. Liquidity ratios (Y19, Y20, Y21) also showed positive dynamics.

Human Resources Development: the average age of staff (Y6) decreased, while the average salary (Y8) increased from 14,783 to 35,797 rubles, confirming improved working conditions and incentives.

Advantages of the innodiversification approach

 Efficiency Indicators

The analysis of efficiency revealed a mixed but generally positive trend:

Increased Operational Efficiency: labor productivity (E3) and the volume of activity per shareholder (E2) increased substantially, indicating more effective use of resources.

Profitability Dynamics: return on assets (E5) and return on equity (E8) showed significant growth, demonstrating enhanced profitability. However, the return on capital (E1) decreased, signaling a need for optimized asset utilization despite their overall growth.

Financial Indicators:

The average annual amount of funds (Y5) increased from 7,360.5 thousand rubles to $64,324.5$ thousand rubles.

Average salary (Y8): increases from 14,783 rubles in 2014 to 35,797 rubles in 2023, which confirms the improvement in working conditions.

Financial independence coefficient (Y14): increases to 0.94, which indicates a higher financial stability of organizations.

The liquidity ratios (Y19, Y20, Y21) also showed positive dynamics, which indicates an increase in financial stability.

D. Human resources:

The average age of staff (Y6) decreased from 52.5 years to 46.4 years, which may indicate a restructuring of the staff.

The level of education (Y7) remained stable, which indicates the maintenance of staff qualifications.

The average salary (Y8) increased from 14,783 rubles to 35,797 rubles, indicating an improvement in working conditions.

Performance Indicators

Return on capital (E1): decreases from 1.94 in 2014 to 1.63 in 2023, which may be due to an increase in the cost of fixed assets, which indicates the need to pay attention to the efficiency of asset use.

The volume of activity per shareholder (E2) increased from 2.86 to 59.48, which indicates an increase in the activity of shareholders.

Labor productivity (E3) increased from 9.57 to 27.56, which indicates an increase in the efficiency of using labor resources and indicates dynamic development.

Return on assets (E5): increases by 98.74 % in 2023, demonstrating an increase in profitability.

Return on equity (E8) has increased significantly, indicating an increase in profitability.

Trends and Prospects A. Identified Trends

Based on the analysis, three key trends were identified:

Growth of Sustainability: A significant increase in the value of fixed assets and cash reserves, creating a foundation for further expansion.

Improving Working Conditions: A rise in average wages and stable educational levels, contributing to higher productivity.

Need for Optimization: A decrease in return on capital necessitates more efficient management of financial and material resources.

Five-Year Forecast

Based on the analysis of current trends, the following forecasts are proposed for the next 5 years:

The value of fixed and working capital will continue to grow, further strengthening the material base.

Financial stability will improve, with the financial independence coefficient potentially exceeding 1.

The average age of staff will decrease further, and the average salary is projected to rise to 42,000 rubles, improving qualifications and attracting talent.

Efficiency metrics are expected to improve, with return on capital (E1) projected to grow to 2.0, and returns on assets and equity to show steady increases.

Conclusion

The practical significance lies in the possibility of applying the proposed methodology to make informed management decisions. The model allows you to predict the key performance indicators of organizations, which helps to increase their competitiveness and sustainability in the long term.

The proposed methodology based on the innodiversification approach makes it possible to effectively predict the sustainability and effectiveness of organizations. The main advantages of the technique include:

Consideration of a wide range of factors, including financial, social, environmental and innovative aspects.

The ability to adapt to changing market conditions.

Improve forecast accuracy by integrating multifactor analysis and machine learning.

The study justifies a methodology for forecasting organizational sustainability and efficiency based on the innodiversification approach. The main advantages of the proposed methodology include its ability to consider a wide range of factors, adapt to changing market conditions, and improve forecast accuracy through the integration of multifactor analysis and machine learning.

The practical significance lies in the model's applicability for making informed management decisions aimed at enhancing the competitiveness and long-term sustainability of organizations, particularly in sectors critical to regional development, such as consumer cooperation.

Further development of the methodology will be aimed at incorporating additional factors, such as the impact of digitalization and global economic trends. It is recommended to test the methodology on real data from various organizations and to conduct a comparative analysis of forecasting results against other methods to quantitatively assess its advantages.

The proposed innodiversification approach has high practical value and can be recommended for use in management practice.

Consideration of a wide range of factors, including financial, social, environmental and innovative aspects.

The ability to adapt to changing market conditions.

Improve forecast accuracy by integrating multifactor analysis and machine learning.

The study justifies a methodology for forecasting organizational sustainability and efficiency based on the innodiversification approach. The main advantages of the proposed methodology include its ability to consider a wide range of factors, adapt to changing market conditions, and improve forecast accuracy through the integration of multifactor analysis and machine learning.

The practical significance lies in the model's applicability for making informed management decisions aimed at enhancing the competitiveness and long-term sustainability of organizations, particularly in sectors critical to regional development, such as consumer cooperation.

Further development of the methodology will be aimed at incorporating additional factors, such as the impact of digitalization and global economic trends. It is recommended to test the methodology on real data from various organizations and to conduct a comparative analysis of forecasting results against other methods to quantitatively assess its advantages.

The proposed innodiversification approach has high practical value and can be recommended for use in management practice.

The paper provides a comprehensive examination of the operational challenges associated with the $\mathrm{U} 140 / \mathrm{U} 240 / \mathrm{U} 241$ automatic transmissions. Identified are the primary defects that may arise during their use and analysed, further, are the causes of these issues, as well as the methods for their elimination. The performance characteristics of the pressure regulator solenoids are tested on a special bench that simulates the operational conditions within the transmission. Following repairs, these solenoids undergo further testing on the same bench to confirm their successful restoration and operational reliability.

 automatic transmission valve body electronic pressure regulator Introduction

Globally, the number of vehicles with automatic transmissions is steadily increasing. These transmissions are undergoing continuous development and improvements in control systems, and the number of gears has increased from three in the past to eight or more nowadays [1], [2]. After a certain mileage, especially in cases of improper maintenance, automatic transmissions begin to develop faults, making driving uncomfortable or even impossible [3].

The U140E/U240E/U241E automatic transmissions were commonly used in Toyota and Lexus vehicles from 1998 to 2013. These vehicles are powered by engines ranging from 1,8 to 3,0 liters in displacement, and can be found in models such as RAV4, MATRIX, HIGHLANDER, SOLARA, CELICA GTS, CAMRY, ES 300, RX 300, Scion, and Pontiac. The designation "E" indicates front-wheel drive configurations, while "F" denotes all-wheel drive ($4 \times 4$) systems. The analysed automatic transmission is fully electronic, with control managed by an electronic control module (PCM) that synchronises the gearbox's operation with other vehicle systems. The mechanical design of this transmission features three planetary gear sets, two one-way clutches, and six multiplate clutches, three of which serve as brakes by connecting various components to the transmission housing. The main transmission components and the differential are typically positioned at the output section of the gearbox.

This transmission system comprises four forward gears and one reverse gear, along with a torque converter featuring a built-in "Lock up clutch" designed to enhance the vehicle's fuel efficiency.

To test the control unit of automatic transmissions, it is necessary to construct test benches [4]. There are studies focused on fuel economy in comparison with other types of transmissions [5].

Toyota vehicles are sold in the millions, with the majority equipped with automatic transmissions. However, there are very few publications addressing the operational issues of automatic transmissions and their diagnostics, which defines the focus of the current study.

Research objective: The primary objective of the current study is twofold:

To identify mechanical defects that may arise during the operation of the gearbox.

To assess the operational condition of the valve body and recommend effective repair methods. First, confirm that you have the correct template for your paper size.

Components of the gearbox

 Materials and Methods

The surveyed gearbox is widely utilised in vehicles throughout the Republic of Bulgaria, particularly in models such as RAV4 and LEXUS. Despite the advanced age of these vehicles, many owners continued to rely on them due to their exceptional durability and reliability.

Extensive practical experience has been accumulated regarding the potential defects that can occur during the operation of these gearboxes, as well as the reliable methods for resolving such issues.

Figure 1 illustrates a cross-section of the automatic transmission, highlighting its constituent components. Table I details the implementation of the gears and lists the solenoids related to each gear.

Defects observed during operation can be classified into two primary groups, mechanical and electro-hydraulic failures.

Mechanical Defects:

2.1.1. Low oil level, or a gearbox oil leak. Insufficient oil levels or leaks can result in clutch slippage and accelerated wear, ultimately necessitating gearbox repairs. Conversely, an excessively high oil level may also lead to defects due to overheating and oil foaming.

2.1.2. Improper filter selection during oil changes. There are two types of filters available for this transmission: one featuring a bag-type cleaning element and the other a meshtype cleaning element. In addition to the original equipment manufacturer filters, a variety of aftermarket filters are available at lower prices.

Nonetheless, incorporating a bag-type cleaning filter may cause some fine threads to dislodge from certain gearbox components within designated transmission situations. This detachment is often undetectable, as the threads are extremely fine and microscopic.

On the left - bag-type filter, on the right - mesh-type filter

2.1.3. Wear of the „Lock-up clutch” in the torque converter. The deterioration of the lock-up clutch in the torque converter can be attributed to several factors, including excessive vehicle mileage, antifreeze leaking into the transmission, inappropriate oil usage for this specific type of transmission, and not adhering to recommended oil change schedules.

2.1.4. Mechanical defects within the transmission may also include damage to the planetary mechanisms.

This issue often arises from neglecting to change the oil, which leads to a loss of its lubricating properties. Additionally, wear on the bearing bushings located on the shafts can diminish or eliminate the oil pressure necessary for lubricating the planetary mechanisms.

 Wear of Clutch "C2".

Clutch C2 may exhibit defects for two primary reasons. First, wear may develop within the two channels located in the rear casing of the transmission, where the segments facilitating the provision of oil to engage the clutch are housed. Components involved in gear implementation.

The primary defect identified involves the infiltration of particulate matter into the solenoids and valve body of the transmission system. Following just a modest amount of mileage, the solenoids' control components, namely the pressure regulators, start to fail, causing subsequently improper pressure regulation within the transmission.

Throughout actual road-testing scenarios, this malfunction manifests as jarring gear shifts or an unanticipated surge in engine speed during gear transitions. Additionally, drivers may experience a loss of specific gears, which can lead to transmission entering an emergency mode.

Figure 2 exemplifies the two types of filters involved in this process.

Second, the piston that applies pressure to the clutch plates may also experience wear. Replacing this piston with a new part is crucial whenever the transmission undergoes repairs [11].

2.1.6. All of the aforementioned mechanical issues might stem from an inadequate operation of the valve body- [7], [13].

2. Electrohydraulic issues:

2.2.1. Given that gear shifting is electronically controlled, it is essential to utilize an electronic tester to retrieve any error codes stored in the memory of the electronic control module. This diagnostic step must be completed prior to initiating any repair work. Should any electrical components be found defective, they must be replaced immediately to ensure optimal functionality. - [8].

2.2.2. Evidence suggests that the transmission control module "TCM" is susceptible to frequent faults. These issues typically manifest as delays and harsh shifting when engaging the reverse gear, as well as during transitions from second to third gear. Continuing to operate the vehicle under these conditions can lead to mechanical failures within the gearbox, necessitating a comprehensive overhaul. It is imperative to either repair or replace the TCM module without delay; failure to do so may result in recurring defects within one to two weeks post-repair.

In older vehicle models, the TCM can be disassembled, allowing for the replacement of its electronic components. However, in newer models, the TCM is encapsulated in resin, rendering it non-disassemblable and thus impossible to repair.

2.2.3. Wear in specific holes within the valve body can lead to altered pressures in certain control circuits, resulting in erratic gearbox behaviour. To address this issue, the valve body must be disassembled and thoroughly cleaned. The two blocks are then reassembled separately, with every opening undergoing evaluation via a vacuum wear tester. Any worn holes are reamed using specialised reamers and fixtures from "Sonnax", and repair pistons and springs from the same manufacturer are installed [9].

It should be emphasized that the control components normally exhibit minimal wear. The most susceptible part is the opening of the "solenoid modulator" valve, which regulates the pressure supplied to the solenoids.

For reference purposes, the two types of valve bodies are illustrated in Figure 3.

At the left side - model years 1998-2005, at the right -2006-2013

2.2.4. Defects in solenoids. Solenoids can experience two primary types of defects: electrical and mechanical.

2.2.4.1. Electrical defects are typically identified using an electronic tester, which can pinpoint the specific solenoid that is malfunctioning.

2.2.4.2. Mechanical issues are detected when solenoids are removed from the valve body for inspection. The S4 and DSL solenoids are classified as "ON - OFF" types and are "normally closed". They can be tested using a 12 -volt power supply. In contrast, the SLT, SL1 and SL2 solenoids function as pressure regulators and operate on a "Duty cycle" system. Duty cycle is the fraction of a time period a system or signal is active, or "on," compared to its total period, typically expressed as a percentage. Testing for these solenoids must be conducted on a specialised bench that simulates the operational conditions within the gearbox, with a frequency of 300 Hz.

To that effect, two regulators were selected from a total of six solenoids acquired from two different gearboxes. Both gearboxes exhibited mechanical defects, including worn clutches that required repair. It should be pointed out that the valve body of the first gearbox showed no unacceptable wear, while the bore of the "solenoid modulator" valve in the second gearbox was significantly worn. The control and improvement of designs depend on our experience in maintenance, failures and repairs of previous generations of automatic transmissions [12], [14].

 Experimental Results:

The solenoids were tested on the bench- [6] under two conditions: with cold oil and with oil at operating temperature. The results of pressure [bar] tests are summarised in Tables II and III, and the corresponding graphs are illustrated in Figures 4 and 5.

In addition, solenoid L2 functions within certain percentage limits, which adversely affects the transmission overall performance. Practical experience indicates that the transmission performs relatively well with cold oil.  Conclusion

The failure of these gearboxes may be attributed to several factors, including the selection of an unsuitable filter, the use of oil that does not meet the specifications for this transmission, or an incorrectly set oil level - either insufficiently low or excessively high.

When replacing the valve body, it is crucial to install a valve body that is specifically compatible with the specifications of this particular transmission model.

The valve body should be thoroughly cleaned and inspected for wear using a vacuum test. Any worn components should be repaired to ensure optimal performance.

Should there be delays or challenges in shifting into reverse and the second or third gears, it is advisable to consider repairing the transmission control module if feasible, or replacing it with a new unit. This proactive measure can prevent potential mechanical failures within the transmission.

Pressure regulator solenoids must be bench-tested to verify their proper functionality. Any malfunctioning solenoid ought to be mended if possible or replaced with new ones to ensure system reliability.

Adhering to proper maintenance protocols and executing appropriate repairs on these transmissions will ensure a longlasting and trouble-free service for the vehicle owner.
