2.2.2. Evidence suggests that the transmission control module “TCM” is susceptible to frequent faults. These issues typically manifest as delays and harsh shifting when engaging the reverse gear, as well as during transitions from second to third gear. Continuing to operate the vehicle under these conditions can lead to mechanical failures within the gearbox, necessitating a comprehensive overhaul. It is imperative to either repair or replace the TCM module without delay; failure to do so may result in recurring defects within one to two weeks post-repair.

In older vehicle models, the TCM can be disassembled, allowing for the replacement of its electronic components. However, in newer models, the TCM is encapsulated in resin, rendering it non-disassemblable and thus impossible to repair.

2.2.3. Wear in specific holes within the valve body can lead to altered pressures in certain control circuits, resulting in erratic gearbox behaviour. To address this issue, the valve body must be disassembled and thoroughly cleaned. The two blocks are then reassembled separately, with every opening undergoing evaluation via a vacuum wear tester. Any worn holes are reamed using specialised reamers and fixtures from “Sonnax”, and repair pistons and springs from the same manufacturer are installed [9].

It should be emphasized that the control components normally exhibit minimal wear. The most susceptible part is the opening of the “solenoid modulator” valve, which regulates the pressure supplied to the solenoids.

For reference purposes, the two types of valve bodies are illustrated in Figure 3.

At the left side - model years 1998-2005, at the right -2006-2013

2.2.4. Defects in solenoids. Solenoids can experience two primary types of defects: electrical and mechanical.

2.2.4.1. Electrical defects are typically identified using an electronic tester, which can pinpoint the specific solenoid that is malfunctioning.

2.2.4.2. Mechanical issues are detected when solenoids are removed from the valve body for inspection. The S4 and DSL solenoids are classified as “ON - OFF” types and are “normally closed”. They can be tested using a 12 -volt power supply. In contrast, the SLT, SL1 and SL2 solenoids function as pressure regulators and operate on a “Duty cycle” system. Duty cycle is the fraction of a time period a system or signal is active, or “on,” compared to its total period, typically expressed as a percentage. Testing for these solenoids must be conducted on a specialised bench that simulates the operational conditions within the gearbox, with a frequency of 300 Hz.

To that effect, two regulators were selected from a total of six solenoids acquired from two different gearboxes. Both gearboxes exhibited mechanical defects, including worn clutches that required repair. It should be pointed out that the valve body of the first gearbox showed no unacceptable wear, while the bore of the “solenoid modulator” valve in the second gearbox was significantly worn. The control and improvement of designs depend on our experience in maintenance, failures and repairs of previous generations of automatic transmissions [12], [14].

 Experimental Results:

The solenoids were tested on the bench- [6] under two conditions: with cold oil and with oil at operating temperature. The results of pressure [bar] tests are summarised in Tables II and III, and the corresponding graphs are illustrated in Figures 4 and 5.

Practical experience indicates that the transmission performs relatively well with cold oil.  Conclusion

The failure of these gearboxes may be attributed to several factors, including the selection of an unsuitable filter, the use of oil that does not meet the specifications for this transmission, or an incorrectly set oil level - either insufficiently low or excessively high.

When replacing the valve body, it is crucial to install a valve body that is specifically compatible with the specifications of this particular transmission model.

The valve body should be thoroughly cleaned and inspected for wear using a vacuum test. Any worn components should be repaired to ensure optimal performance.

Should there be delays or challenges in shifting into reverse and the second or third gears, it is advisable to consider repairing the transmission control module if feasible, or replacing it with a new unit. This proactive measure can prevent potential mechanical failures within the transmission.

Pressure regulator solenoids must be bench-tested to verify their proper functionality. Any malfunctioning solenoid ought to be mended if possible or replaced with new ones to ensure system reliability.

Adhering to proper maintenance protocols and executing appropriate repairs on these transmissions will ensure a longlasting and trouble-free service for the vehicle owner.

The article is devoted to the study of the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the innodiversification approach. The authors cluster the correlation coefficients of indicators for the period 2014-2023, identifying linear and nonlinear dependencies. The paper presents mathematical models constructed using linear and nonlinear regression, which make it possible to predict the dynamics of sustainability and efficiency indicators. The results of the study confirm the hypothesis of a significant influence of the time factor on the activities of consumer cooperatives. Quantitative analysis revealed that 54.7 % of all inter-indicator correlations and 61.3 % of indicator-time correlations were high or very high ($\mathbf{R} \geq \mathbf{0. 7}$). The constructed models, with an average approximation error not exceeding 5%, demonstrate high predictive accuracy.

 consumer cooperation sustainability efficiency innovation and diversification approach correlation analysis linear regression mathematical modeling forecasting Introduction

The relevance of the research is due to the need to develop modern approaches to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation in a dynamically changing economic environment [1]. Consumer cooperation plays an important role in the socio-economic development of regions, and understanding the factors influencing its sustainability is key to making managerial decisions [2]. Existing studies often focus on either financial stability [7], [8] or investment efficiency [5] in isolation. However, an integrated approach that combines these aspects is lacking. The innodiversification approach proposed by the authors, which integrates principles of innovation and diversification, allows for a comprehensive analysis by considering both direct and indirect dependencies between a wide array of indicators [3], [4]. This makes the study particularly significant.

The purpose of the research is to develop and test an innodiversification approach to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the analysis of correlation dependencies and the construction of mathematical models for predicting the dynamics of key indicators.

 Research Methodology

The study is based on official annual statistical data for the Novosibirsk Region consumer cooperation societies for the period 2014-2023. The dataset comprises 44 indicators (36 for sustainability, Ni, and 8 for efficiency, Ei) across 10 time periods, resulting in 946 unique correlation pairs. The variables cover key aspects of organizational performance, including fixed assets (Y1-Y3), financial metrics (Y5, Y14, Y16, Y19), human resources (Y6, Y8, Y9), market activity (Y10, Y22-Y30), environmental responsibility (Y31-Y33), and social impact (Y34-Y36). Efficiency indicators (E1-E8) encompass productivity, turnover, and profitability.

All calculations and model constructions were performed using the Python programming language (v 3.11) with the Scikit-learn library for clustering and the Statsmodels library for regression analysis. This environment was chosen for its robustness in statistical modeling and reproducibility.

The paper uses methods of correlation analysis (Pearson's coefficient), data clustering (K-means algorithm for grouping correlation magnitudes), and regression analysis (linear and nonlinear). The authors apply the technology of approximating the function of one variable to construct mathematical models. The "innodiversification approach" is conceptualized as a hierarchical framework for classifying indicators based on the strength and nature of their dependencies. Indicators with a strong direct linear relationship with time (correlation $\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) are assigned to the first level of direct influence. Indicators with weaker direct links but strong correlations with other first-level indicators are assigned to a second level of indirect influence, modeled via multivariate regression. This provides a deeper, multilayered understanding of the system's dynamics [7], [8].

 Correlation Analysis

To calculate the correlation coefficients between sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ti) indicators for the period 2014-2023, the Pearson correlation coefficient can be used. The Pearson correlation coefficient measures the linear relationship between two variables and takes values from -1 to 1, where [ 5], [6 ]:

1 means a perfect positive linear relationship,

-1 means a perfect negative linear relationship,

0 means there is no linear relationship.

The analysis of the obtained correlation coefficients suggests grouping them into five clusters (Table 1) [9]. Clustering of correlation coefficients of consumer cooperation activity indicators for the period 2014-2023

The correlation coefficients were grouped by absolute values for ease of analysis. To build predictive models, it is important to determine the impact of the time period on sustainability and efficiency. The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R}=0.7$) included more than half of the correlation coefficients $(54.7 \%)$, which confirms the hypothesis of the existence of a mathematical relationship between the indicators of consumer cooperation. Of particular interest for forecasting is the dependence of sustainability and efficiency indicators on the time period (Table 1) [9]. The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) included 27 correlation coefficients (61.3 %), which confirms the hypothesis that there is a linear dependence of most indicators of sustainability and efficiency on the time period of consumer cooperation.

According to a different diversification approach, these indicators should be attributed to the first level of direct influence of a multilevel mathematical model. As a result, a dynamic matrix was formed, consisting of sustainability and efficiency indicators that have a direct linear dependence on the time period.

 Regression Analysis

The results of approximation by a linear function of the dependence of dynamic indicators of consumer cooperation activity on the time period are shown in Table 3. Mathematical models of indicators of sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation activities

Linear regression is one of the most widely used methods for modeling the dependence of variables. In the context of consumer cooperation, linear regression allows us to establish a linear relationship between sustainability and efficiency indicators (Yi and Ei) and the time period (T). The main advantages of linear regression include:

Easy to interpret: Linear models are easy to interpret and allow you to clearly see the impact of time on performance.

Forecasting: the obtained models can be used to predict future values of indicators based on current data.

Significance assessment: linear regression coefficients allow us to assess the significance of each indicator and its contribution to the overall model.

The analysis of indicators of sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ei) of consumer co-operation activity shows that linear regression is a reasonable method for modeling the dependence of these indicators on time. The accuracy of the models was estimated by the Average Approximation Error (AAE) parameter, calculated as the mean absolute percentage error between the actual and predicted values. For all linear models presented in Table 3, the AAE did not exceed 5%, which confirms the statistical adequacy and high predictive quality of the selected models. The innodiversification approach makes it possible to justify the choice of each indicator, emphasizing their contribution to the stability and efficiency of consumer cooperation (Table 4) [10]. The choice of model form (linear, quadratic, cubic) was driven by the data structure and the goal of achieving the best fit. For instance, the relationship for the indicator Y4 (Average annual cost of working capital) was tested against Y1. While a linear model was significant, a quadratic and subsequently a cubic regression were also fitted. The cubic model, of the form Y4 $=a^{*} Y 1^{\wedge} 3+b^{*} Y 1^{\wedge} 2+c^{*} Y 1+d$, was ultimately selected as it provided the lowest AAE, capturing the nonlinear saturation effect where growth in fixed assets (Y1) leads to a disproportionately smaller increase in required working capital (Y4) after a certain point. This reflects a real economic phenomenon of optimizing asset structure.

 Conclusion

The practical significance of the research lies in the possibility of using the developed models for forecasting and planning the activities of consumer cooperation. The obtained results can be used by the management of cooperatives to increase the stability and efficiency of their work. The theoretical significance lies in the development of a methodology for assessing sustainability and efficiency through the prism of an innodiversification approach, which expands scientific understanding of the factors influencing the activities of cooperatives.

The authors conclude that more than half of the indicators of sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation have a high or very high correlation with the time factor... The authors also emphasize the need for further research of indirect dependencies between indicators using more complex modeling techniques, which can contribute to more accurate forecasting and increase the stability of cooperation. It is recommended to use the developed mathematical models for forecasting and managing key indicators. The authors also emphasize the need for further research of indirect dependencies between indicators, which can contribute to more accurate forecasting and increase the stability of cooperation. The use of an innodiversification approach and mathematical modeling makes the research relevant and practically applicable.

The article is devoted to the study of the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the innodiversification approach. The authors cluster the correlation coefficients of indicators for the period 2014-2023, identifying linear and nonlinear dependencies. The paper presents mathematical models constructed using linear and nonlinear regression, which make it possible to predict the dynamics of sustainability and efficiency indicators. The results of the study confirm the hypothesis of a significant influence of the time factor on the activities of consumer cooperatives. Quantitative analysis revealed that 54.7 % of all inter-indicator correlations and 61.3 % of indicator-time correlations were high or very high ($\mathbf{R} \geq \mathbf{0. 7}$). The constructed models, with an average approximation error not exceeding 5%, demonstrate high predictive accuracy.

 consumer cooperation sustainability efficiency innovation and diversification approach correlation analysis linear regression mathematical modeling forecasting Introduction

The relevance of the research is due to the need to develop modern approaches to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation in a dynamically changing economic environment [1]. Consumer cooperation plays an important role in the socio-economic development of regions, and understanding the factors influencing its sustainability is key to making managerial decisions [2]. Existing studies often focus on either financial stability [7], [8] or investment efficiency [5] in isolation. However, an integrated approach that combines these aspects is lacking. The innodiversification approach proposed by the authors, which integrates principles of innovation and diversification, allows for a comprehensive analysis by considering both direct and indirect dependencies between a wide array of indicators [3], [4]. This makes the study particularly significant.

The purpose of the research is to develop and test an innodiversification approach to assessing the sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation based on the analysis of correlation dependencies and the construction of mathematical models for predicting the dynamics of key indicators.

 Research Methodology

The study is based on official annual statistical data for the Novosibirsk Region consumer cooperation societies for the period 2014-2023. The dataset comprises 44 indicators (36 for sustainability, Ni, and 8 for efficiency, Ei) across 10 time periods, resulting in 946 unique correlation pairs. The variables cover key aspects of organizational performance, including fixed assets (Y1-Y3), financial metrics (Y5, Y14, Y16, Y19), human resources (Y6, Y8, Y9), market activity (Y10, Y22-Y30), environmental responsibility (Y31-Y33), and social impact (Y34-Y36). Efficiency indicators (E1-E8) encompass productivity, turnover, and profitability.

All calculations and model constructions were performed using the Python programming language (v 3.11) with the Scikit-learn library for clustering and the Statsmodels library for regression analysis. This environment was chosen for its robustness in statistical modeling and reproducibility.

The paper uses methods of correlation analysis (Pearson's coefficient), data clustering (K-means algorithm for grouping correlation magnitudes), and regression analysis (linear and nonlinear). The authors apply the technology of approximating the function of one variable to construct mathematical models. The “innodiversification approach” is conceptualized as a hierarchical framework for classifying indicators based on the strength and nature of their dependencies. Indicators with a strong direct linear relationship with time (correlation $\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) are assigned to the first level of direct influence. Indicators with weaker direct links but strong correlations with other first-level indicators are assigned to a second level of indirect influence, modeled via multivariate regression. This provides a deeper, multi-layered understanding of the system's dynamics [7], [8].

 Correlation Analysis

To calculate the correlation coefficients between sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ti) indicators for the period 2014-2023, the Pearson correlation coefficient can be used. The Pearson correlation coefficient measures the linear relationship between two variables and takes values from -1 to 1, where [5], [6]:

1 means a perfect positive linear relationship,

−1 means a perfect negative linear relationship,

0 means there is no linear relationship.

The analysis of the obtained correlation coefficients suggests grouping them into five clusters (Table 1) [9]. Clustering of correlation coefficients of consumer cooperation activity indicators for the period 2014-2023

Average correlation 0.30-0.49 132 14,00
low correlation −0.30-0.29 151 16,00
Total от -1.0 до 1.0 946 100,00

The correlation coefficients were grouped by absolute values for ease of analysis. To build predictive models, it is important to determine the impact of the time period on sustainability and efficiency. The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R}=0.7$) included more than half of the correlation coefficients $(54.7 \%)$, which confirms the hypothesis of the existence of a mathematical relationship between the indicators of consumer cooperation. Of particular interest for forecasting is the dependence of sustainability and efficiency indicators on the time period (Table 1) [9]. The range of high and very high correlation ($\mathrm{R} \geq 0.7$) included 27 correlation coefficients (61.3 %), which confirms the hypothesis that there is a linear dependence of most indicators of sustainability and efficiency on the time period of consumer cooperation.

According to a different diversification approach, these indicators should be attributed to the first level of direct influence of a multilevel mathematical model. As a result, a dynamic matrix was formed, consisting of sustainability and efficiency indicators that have a direct linear dependence on the time period.

 Regression Analysis

The results of approximation by a linear function of the dependence of dynamic indicators of consumer cooperation activity on the time period are shown in Table 3. Mathematical models of indicators of sustainability and efficiency of consumer cooperation activities

Linear regression is one of the most widely used methods for modeling the dependence of variables. In the context of consumer cooperation, linear regression allows us to establish a linear relationship between sustainability and efficiency indicators (Yi and Ei) and the time period (T). The main advantages of linear regression include:

Easy to interpret: Linear models are easy to interpret and allow you to clearly see the impact of time on performance.

Forecasting: the obtained models can be used to predict future values of indicators based on current data.

Significance assessment: linear regression coefficients allow us to assess the significance of each indicator and its contribution to the overall model.

The analysis of indicators of sustainability (Yi) and efficiency (Ei) of consumer co-operation activity shows that linear regression is a reasonable method for modeling the dependence of these indicators on time. The accuracy of the models was estimated by the Average Approximation Error (AAE) parameter, calculated as the mean absolute percentage error between the actual and predicted values. For all linear models presented in Table 3, the AAE did not exceed 5%, which confirms the statistical adequacy and high predictive quality of the selected models. The innodiversification approach makes it possible to justify the choice of each indicator, emphasizing their contribution to the stability and efficiency of consumer cooperation (Table 4) [10]. The results presented in Table IV were obtained through a qualitative analysis based on the innodiversification framework. For each indicator with a established linear relationship to time (from Table III), we provide a dual justification: its intrinsic “Validity” for assessing sustainability/efficiency and its role from the perspective of the “Innodiversification Approach” (i.e., how it contributes to innovation and diversification of the cooperative's activities).

Table 4 presents the results of an analysis using the innodiversification approach of indicators of sustainability (Yi) and effectiveness (Yi) of consumer cooperation activities depending on the time period, including the validity of the choice of each indicator.

According to the innodiversification approach, indicators that are not included in the group of the first level (linear relationship with the time period) can be attributed to the second (indirect) level of influence. Thus, the average annual cost of working capital (Y4) has a correlation coefficient with a time period of only 0.19 and belongs to the cluster “very low correlation”, which indicates the absence of linear dependence. However, it has a correlation coefficient of 0.63 (average correlation) with the average annual cost of buildings and structures (Y1) and the probability of their relationship is three times higher than that of the time period. The practical significance of the research lies in the possibility of using the developed models for forecasting and planning the activities of consumer cooperation. The obtained results can be used by the management of cooperatives to increase the stability and efficiency of their work. Nearly all insurance operations are dependent on risk prediction, the accuracy and fairness of which is paramount. Traditional models of underwriting and risk assessment generally fail to exhibit the complexity and interrelationships associated with an interconnected portfolio. This paper proposes the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) an innovative machine learning architecture that employs graph neural networks, causal inference, and transformer based attention to allow for a more holistic and interpretable consideration of insurance risk. Whereas conventional models assume policyholders represent independent risks, the GCT augments its models of risk by directly modeling relationships between policyholders, assets, and their context, such as supply chains or relative distance. It also distinguishes true causal drivers (or influences) from false correlations. The GCT is evaluated using a very large scale auto insurance dataset augmented with alternative datasets. The GCT model performed better than Generalised Linear Models, XGBoost, Graph Neural Networks and Actuarial Transformers depending upon the performance metric employed, namely AUC, Gini, loss-ratio uplift and fairness. The GCT provides a high degree of interpretability through SHAP based local explanations and causal path insights, therefore supporting those firms who need to comply with emerging regulations associated with the rise of AI. The findings of this study demonstrate that the GCT provides predictive performance benefits while enhancing transparency and governance whilst making it an important candidate for future algorithmic underwriting applications in regulated insurance markets.

 —Insurance Risk Prediction Machine Learning Graph Neural Networks Transformers Causal Inference Explainable AI Fairness Algorithmic Underwriting \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{2193}{\ifmmode\downarrow\else{$\downarrow$}\fi} Introduction

One of the most important things in the insurance industry is the ability to correctly predict the risks. Insurers use Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) and regression-based resilience (and related) actuarial methods as their main tools to measure the risk of policyholders. Besides the recognition of the great support among regulators of GLM frameworks, insurers appreciate the perspective during the auditing process, but GLMs are restricted in linearity having constraints on combining features and static relationships which are tending to err with the complex nature of the insurance data available. He insurance sector is undergoing a digital transformation and the insurers can now access a wide variety of new data streams - telematics data on your driving; The Internet of Things with all the sensors coming from your smart home (or smart device); satellite images; and even social media and behavioral data [1]. The availability of the new data creates a wealth of opportunities as well as challenges for the insurers.

Machine learning algorithms like gradient boosting, neural nets, and transformers have been found to be more powerful than the conventional frameworks to make predictions. Machine learning top three barriers are the lack of interpretability, lack of fairness protections, and difficulty in modeling dynamic (or relational) structures of risk [2].

The predictive modeling used in the current predictive methods have a major drawback which is that the treatment of policyholders or assets as single entities dominates the most part of the modeling practice.Risk is not treated this way and often has interconnectedness; proximity, vendors, social connections, and supply chain. In fact, after a natural disaster, hundreds of policyholders will suffer simultaneous losses. In observance of this risk interconnectedness, we intend to emphasize risk interdependence in The Protection Gap Model - an interconnected framework of this nature could create a better consideration of risk both at the level of individual assets and across vehicles/assets within geographies[3]. These relational patterns are ignored in traditional tabular models because systems thinking cannot anticipate systemic exposures or cascading losses. Another contributing factor is the increasing scrutiny, demands for model governance, and transparency, especially given new regulations like the EU AI Act. Increasingly, decision-makers are responsible for explainability, fairness, and accountability when using algorithmic decision making [4], especially with high-stakes applications.

In light of these knowledge gaps, this article proposes a new architecture, the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), as we formalize three breakthroughs in modern machine learning: graph neural networks to model relationships between entities, causal inference to separate true drivers of risk vs. coincident correlations, and transformer attentional mechanisms to propagate feature interactions. Our overall modeling framework, will help enhance not only predictive performance but also fairness, interpretability and ease of adoption in operational insurance systems. This paper begins with a review of related work in traditional actuarial modeling and recent advances in ML for insurance applications. We then present the GCT methodology in detail, describing its core components and training procedures. Next, we evaluate the model on a real-world dataset and benchmark its performance against industry-standard baselines. Following this, we discuss the practical implications of our findings, including interpretability, regulatory compliance, and scalability. Finally, we conclude by summarizing key insights and outlining avenues for future research.

 Related Works

Risk prediction in insurance has advanced quickly in the past 20 years, moving away from traditional actuarial methods to a future with more data and algorithmically driven approaches. This section summarises the broad classes of models that have been part of this evolution and discuss how they are useful for the various challenges this paper seeks to address.

The backbone of insurance risk modeling has also been the Generalised Linear Models (GLM) and Generalised Additive Models (GAMs). The strength of these models is transparency and interpretability which is good for regulators and audits [5]. They are still popular for base pricing, reserving and risk segmentation. The linear/additive assumptions prevent GLMs or GAMs from capturing the complex interactions and nonlinearities in high-dimension actuarial data [6].

The new growth of tree-based ensemble methods, namely Random Forests and Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDTs) have shown to be a substantial improvement in predictive power over linear models. Tree-based ensemble methods do not require any specification of non-linear relationships or characterisation of feature interactions [7]. XGBoost has emerged as a popular tool among insurers primarily due to its efficiency, accuracy, and tendency to outperform many similar models in terms of overfitting risk. Nevertheless, these models operate under the independence assumption and neglect structured, relational, or timing dependencies. Each data point is still treated independently even if the underlying relationship structure is a concern in areas like fraud detection or catastrophe modeling [8].

More recently, deep learning architectures, such as fully connected networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), have been applied to insurance challenges in the document classification, image inspection, and voice triage realms [9], [10]. While these models can identify representations of unstructured or semi-structured information very well, this black-box architecture creates many challenges socially (usability and acceptability) and by regulators who embrace a more fiduciary duty and want some degree of model transparency.

With increasing demands for accuracy and transparency to support decision making, researchers have recently introduced the Actuarial Transformer (AT) [11] as a specific application of a transformer architecture, developed for insurance scenarios and has been specifically customized for tabular insurance data. AT models utilize attention and they are powerful because they allow modelers to capture relationships in global feature space simultaneously, while enabling some interpretability given researchers can evaluate the attention weights or SHAP explanations. Still, these models make the same i.i.d. independence assumption and they treat observations with structured relationships independently in the same general spirit as the deep learning models before. To progress the state of relational dependencies, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have gained traction for use in insurance applications (again, specifically in fraud detection and supplychain risk modeling [12]). GNNs use graphs to model data such that nodes are entities and edges represent relationships (e.g., co-claims, proximity, shared service providers). Historically, researchers have shown GNNs outperform traditional methods in identifying patterns of fraud that occur in clusters or rings [13]. However, existing methods, including GNNs, generally lack the capacity for causal reasoning, which is particularly important for fairness and generalizability.

Alongside the development and application of graph learning methods, causal inference has gained traction in the insurance context, particularly in fairness-aware modeling and reducing bias. As examples, double machine learning, structural equation modeling, and causal discovery algorithms (e.g., NOTEARS) have been proposed and operationalized to help distinguish between spurious correlations and causal drivers of risk [14], [15]. The distinction between correlation and other variables or causal factors is important because, in cases where regulators specify that insurers cannot take certain variables into consideration (e.g., gender, zip code), insurers must demonstrate model behavior that goes beyond simply being statistically significant. However, there is no single model that currently combines adversarial structures, causal reasoning, and deep attention mechanisms in a humanly interpretable model for risk prediction. This paper seeks to fill that gap by proposing the Graph-CausalTransformer (GCT), a new model that we have developed which attempts to remedy the limitation of current methods and provide an adaptable, interpretable, regulation-friendly framework for modern insurers.

 Methodology

In this section, we present the proposed Graph-CausalTransformer (GCT) architecture to capture the complex, relational, and dynamic nature of insurance risk. The GCT combines graph learning, causal inference, and attentionbased deep learning in a single architecture. The GCT incorporates local interactions (e.g., individual policyholder behavior) and global interactions (e.g., systemic or cascading risks across networks); it also leverages interpretability and fairness within regulatory constraints.

We are representing the portfolio as a graph $\mathrm{G}=(V, E)$ with $\vert\mathrm{V}\vert=\mathrm{N}$ nodes (policyholders/assets/actors) and $\vert\mathrm{E}\vert$ edges (relations). Node features $\mathrm{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d 0 X}$.with $d$, the graph has depth $L$, and attention heads $H$. These symbols are used to denote the complexity of the model below.

Graph-Causal-Transformer (Gct) Architecture

The Guided Collaborative Transformer (GCT) architecture consists of three modules that are tightly coupled. Each module defines a unique facet of the model's predictive and interpretive capabilities:

Graph-causal-transformer(gct)overview.

1)Graph Construction Layer: The first layer depicts the core entities of insurance as nodes in the graph, which is constructed dynamically. To illustrate, nodes in the graph represent policyholders, insured items, third parties, brokers, and corporate ties. Two nodes are connected with an edge, which is defined by the various relations we have foundfamilial ties, same addresses, same vendors, supply-chain associations, or behavior, depending on telematics. Data streams (event logs, IoT telemetries, claims activity) are processed almost re. With the help of a distributed streaming platform like Apache Kafka, the model can now have a live graph with minimum latency. The dynamic closed relational networks depicted in the graph allow the model to be very sensitive to relationships and risk aggregations that are hidden when traditional tabular approaches are used,Graph message passing. \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(0)}=W_{0} x_{\mathcal{V}}, \quad m_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=\sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(\mathcal{V})} \alpha_{u \mathcal{V}}^{(k)} W_{m}^{(k)} h_{\mathcal{u}}^{(k-1)}, \\ h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=\sigma\left(W_{h}^{(k)}\left[h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k-1)} \vert m_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}\right]\right) \end{gathered} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

with attention weights $\alpha_{u v}^{(k)}=\operatorname{softmax}_{u}\left(a\left(h_{u}^{(k-1)}, h_{v}^{(k-1)}\right)\right)$

2)Causal Inference Module: In order to mitigate the adverse implications of spurious correlations and fairness concerns, our model utilizes a causal inference layer. This causal inference layer can use attachment approaches like Double Machine Learning (DML) to estimate causal effects or NOTEARS for structure learning directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational data [14]. Specifically, it identifies causally-relevant features and pathways especially when node features can vary by things like protected or regulated variables (i.e. gender, geography). The advantage of this structure is that it improves the model's generalizability across demographic changes and regulatory constraints, and it mitigates the risk of overfitting to historically biased data. Causal signal and fairness regulization. \begin{equation*} \mathcal{L}_{\text {causal }}=\sum_{j}\left(\widehat{\mathcal{T}}_{j}+\mathcal{T}_{j}^{*}\right)^{2}+\gamma \operatorname{Dep}(f(X), Z \text { protected }) \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Where $\quad \widehat{\mathcal{T}}_{j} \quad$ are treatment-effect estimates from DML/NOTEARS-derived structure, and Dep ($\cdot$) penalizes dependence on protected attributes or their proxies.

3) Transformer-Based Encoder-Decoder: The predictive engine is based on a transformer architecture that has been specifically adapted to incorporate graph-aware tabular data. In the encoder phase, raw node embeddings based solely on the underlying graph structure are augmented by causal relevance scores and passed through a multi-head selfattention layer. This allows the model to prioritize and combine information from both the local node features and the relational context of the node itself. Furthermore, the general architecture supports multitask learning modes, and thus, allows the model to simultaneously learn jointly to predict several targets that are important in insurance operations:

Claim severity, trained using Tweedie loss (appropriate for zero-inflated, right-skewed data).

Claim frequency, modeled with Poisson or Negative Binomial loss.

Churn propensity, framed as a binary classification task using cross-entropy loss.

Graph-aware attention. \begin{equation*} \begin{array}{r} \mathcal{Q}=X W_{Q}, K=X W_{K}, V=X W_{V}, Z=\operatorname{MHAttn}(Q, K, V) \\ =\text { Concat }_{h} \operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{Q_{h} K_{h}}{\sqrt{d / H}}\right) V_{h} \end{array} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

We augment token/node embeddings with $\tilde{h}_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}$ before attention.

The multi-head self-attention component adds explainability by clarifying the most relevant features and relationships for each prediction.

Multi-task objective. \begin{equation*} \mathcal{L}=\lambda_{1} \mathcal{L}_{\text {severity }}^{\text {Tweedie }}+\lambda_{2} \mathcal{L}_{\text {frequency }}^{\text {Poisson } / \text { NegBin }}+\lambda_{3} \mathcal{L}_{\text {churn }}^{\text {CE }}+\lambda_{4} \mathcal{L}_{\text {causal }} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

 4)Fairness & Metric Definitions

We report Demographic Parity Gap (DPG): \begin{equation*} D P G=\vert\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Z=1]-\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Z=0]\vert \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Where Z is a protected-group indicator and $\hat{Y}$ the predicted risk /decision score. \begin{equation*} \tilde{h}_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}+R_{\text {causal }}(\mathcal{V}), \tag{1} \end{equation*}

Training Pipeline

The process of training GCT entails four distinct phases with the goal of getting the most out of data while promoting model robustness, interpretability, and fairness:

Data Preprocessing: The training pipeline consumes five years of multimodal insurance (policy and claims) data including structured records produced during the insurance transaction (e.g., policy and claims data), unstructured text (e.g., claim adjuster notes), and sensor-generated time-series data such as vehicle telemetry and smart home devices, as well as external context datasets (e.g., weather patterns, crime indices, and economic indicators). The records are normalized, encoded, aligned temporally, and represented as graphs.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: Before supervised learning, the model is pretrained through self-supervised tasks that include a means of obtaining structural or semantic information via edge prediction (making predictions using missing or future links between nodes), and node feature reconstruction (predicting node attributes of a node based on the node's neighborhood). This feature is more likely to allow the model to generalize and is a way to helpthe potential data sparsity and other issues.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: The model is initially trained with self-supervised learning frameworks followed by supervised learning. The self-supervised learning tasks were centered on the extraction of structural and semantic features, for instance, edge prediction (i.e., predicting missing or new edges between nodes) and node feature reconstruction (i.e., predicting node features from neighborhood distributions). This phase opens up the path for more generalization from the data while resolving problems such as data sparsity.

 Experimental Results

Describing the experimental design to evaluate the performance of the new Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) model, this section includes the characteristics of the dataset used, baseline models, and the evaluation results presented not only in one but in several important metrics. The model will be measured against its predictive accuracy, fairness, explainability, and operational consideration.

 Dataset

The experiments were conducted on the CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) Auto Insurance Claims Dataset, a large-scale dataset, created in collaboration with the CAS (Casualty Actuarial Society), the dataset is known as a benchmark for risk modeling in the auto insurance domain, fraud detection, and telemetry pricing, used widely in both academia and the industry for benchmarking purposes. OpenWeatherMap provided the weather data, while Eurostat provided the economic indicators; these and other publicly available data sources were used to further enrich the data.

Size: 1.2 million unique policy records, including approximately 80,000 claims.

Features: 180 structured tabular features (e.g., demographics, vehicle info, policy terms), 15 timeseries telematics fields (e.g., acceleration, speed, braking patterns), and geospatial metadata (e.g., region codes, traffic risk zones).

Data Splitting: A stratified 80-10-10 train-validationtest split was applied. To ensure statistical stability, all metrics are averaged over three runs with different random seeds.

Data Source: CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) by the Casualty Actuarial Society, supplemented with OpenWeatherMap and Eurostat datasets for external feature enrichment.

Baseline Models

The To benchmark the performance of the GCT model, we compare it against four commonly used models in the insurance industry:

GLM (Generalized Linear Model): The actuarial standard, included for baseline interpretability.

XGBoost: A popular gradient boosting method, known for its performance and flexibility.

Actuarial Transformer: A transformer-based model optimized for structured tabular data, incorporating attentionbased feature learning [3].

Graph Neural Network (GNN): A relational learning model that leverages graph topology but lacks explicit causal or attention mechanisms.

Each model was trained on the same dataset and evaluated using five metrics: AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve), Gini coefficient, uplift in loss ratio over the GLM baseline, fairness (measured as demographic parity gap), and explanation latency using SHAP (in milliseconds). Benchmark results on casd
Model AUC (Claims) Gini Loss-Ratio Uplift Demographic Gap ↓ SHAP Latency (ms)
GLM 0.78 0.56 Baseline 0.00 5
XGBoost 0.85 0.70 +5.1% 0.07 12
Actuarial Transformer 0.88 0.76 +7.3% 0.06 55
GNN 0.86 0.72 +6.0% 0.05 23
GCT (proposed model) 0.91 0.80 +10.4% 0.02 61
Observations:

Predictive Performance: GCT achieves the highest AUC (0.91) and Gini coefficient (0.80), indicating superior discriminatory power in identifying high-risk policyholders.

Fairness: The demographic parity gap is significantly lower in GCT (0.02) compared to other ML models, suggesting that the causal module successfully mitigates bias introduced by correlated demographic features.

Business Impact: GCT improves portfolio loss ratio by 10.4 % relative to GLM, outperforming all baselines.

Explainability: While GCT incurs slightly higher explanation latency due to its layered architecture, it remains within acceptable operational thresholds ($<100 ~\text{ms}$), and the added interpretability from causal-path tracing offers substantial governance value.

These results demonstrate that the proposed architecture provides a well-balanced trade-off between accuracy, fairness, and explainability-making it suitable for high-stakes, regulated insurance environments.

Computational Complexity & Optimizations.

Per batch, GNN message passing is $\mathcal{O}(M d)$ per layer; transformer self-attention is $\mathcal{O}\left(n^{2} d\right)$ for token length nnn; causal regularization $\mathcal{O}(N d)-\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ depending on the estimator. Optimizations: mini-batch graph sampling (GraphSAGE/Cluster-GCN), sparse/linear attention, parameter sharing, mixed precision, quantization/distillation for edge/real-time use, and GPU batching for SHAP. This keeps GCT deployable for daily scoring while reserving heavier causal audits for batch windows.

 Discussion

This part initially discusses the consequences of the experimental findings by highlighting the benefits of the proposed Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) alongside some of the practical issues in its deployment in the insurance field.

Strengths of the Gct Model

The GCT model is enriched with several benefits that give it an edge over the previous methods adopted in the literature. These benefits are further discussed in terms of relational modeling, fairness, and operationalization.

Relational Reasoning: The GCT model takes into account the relationships that exist among different conditional independence structures and develops a latent structure that acknowledges the overlap between policyholders, assets, and contextual variables as one single unit. This was a feature that made it superior compared to traditional univariate models that consider all risks as independent observations. The GCT model is the most appropriate for cases where there are systematic exposures (e.g., regional weather, supply chain risks, fraud rings). In such instances, the GCT model will be instrumental in modeling both cascading and accumulation risks, which is a great advantage in portfolios that are increasingly sensitive to climate and exposed to cyber risks.

Fairness and Regulatory Compliance: Introducing a causal inference module enables GCT to analyze and potentially remove some reliance on proxy variables that may incur observed bias. This has potential benefits in terms of fairness of predictions, particularly regarding protected attributes such as age, gender or location. The causal modeling capability of the model allows it to be better positioned for conforming to any emerging regulations related to AI, such as the EU AI Act and NAIC Model Law 687, which both require proof of protections against algorithmic discrimination.

Operational Readiness: Even though I anticipate that GCT's architecture will be somewhat complicated as far as its logic and structure are concerned, it was conceived to be scalable to deployment. Dynamic graph partitioning enables modifications to the graph structure that are caused by new claims, policy changes, or telemetry signals to take place in a localized manner and be communicated quickly. Besides, well-organized model documentation (e.g., model cards) with integrated explainability has the potential of assisting governance both internally and externally which, in turn, will be the approval of the model in the regulated insurance environment.

 Practical Considerations

Despite GCT having its fair share of advantages, its introduction in the actual world is laden with various hiccups. These challenges need to be solved first if GCT is to perform as expected.

Data Latency and Integration: A significant portion of the graph's success depends on the timely ingestion and integration of event data, such as claims and telematics streams. To keep the graph's freshness and prediction relevance, a reliable data pipeline with real-time stream processing (e.g., Apache Kafka or similar) would be required.

Computational Efficiency: The computation of the inference within GCT is more complicated than in the case of traditional ensemble methods due to the use of attention mechanisms and graph embeddings. Nevertheless, there are some opportunities for performance optimization (e.g., parameter sharing, attention pruning, and model quantization), but there will still be a certain amount of extra computing resources required. In the case of high-throughput applications such as usage-based insurance and $\mu$ underwriting, we can easily use the lightweight versions of GCT which have the reduced data on-edge or are accelerated with GPUs.

Interpretability and User Trust: The success of any ML system in insurance is not only based on the system's performance but also on user trust. These GCT explore builtin explainability tools, but their worth is contingent upon appropriate usability. Visualization tools (like SHAP-based dashboards, causal path diagrams, etc.) can be co-designed with actuaries and underwriters, - given that model outputs are familiar for accessible interpretation and can be consistent with domain expertise. We must include frontline users in this process to gain widespread acceptance.

 Conclusion

This research put forward a new architecture of risk prediction in insurance namely the Graph-CausalTransformer (GCT), an amalgam of graph-based learning, causal inference, and transformer-based attention mechanisms, which serves as an interpretable model. The policyholders are traditional models that isolate, GCT instead exploits the relational aspect of an insurance portfolio and identifies conditional and structural dependencies not only between the entities but also between the supply chains, geographical clusters, and behavioral networks. As GCT provides the structural information, it is able to incorporate causal reasoning, thus increasing predictive accuracy as well as promoting fairness and regulatory accountability.

We built a large-scale and industry-relevant dataset to carry out empirical experiments, and the results showed that the GCT outperforms several baseline methods, including GLMs, gradient boosting, graph neural networks, and actuarial transformers, on different aspects-predictive performance (AUC and Gini) for business value (uplift on loss ratios), and for ethical practice (differential disparity in demographic variables). Thus, we have gone a long way towards making the first, second, and third comparisons stronger without losing the potential for the model's explainability, which, in the regulated insurance field, is a crucial feature.

Streaming data. The explainability component of the model relies on SHAP values and causal path tracing. This explainability layer is also available for the use of internal governance, regulatory audits, and underwriting trust.

In terms of future research and applied improvement, GCT creates many paths forward. For example, GCT can extend the architecture into multi-line or reinsurance situations, use foundation models to comprehend document level understanding, or apply reinforcement learning to provide dynamic pricing options. As the insurance industry algorithmically assisted underwriters, models like GCT represent an essential leap to make risk management more intelligent, potentially adaptive, and responsible.
