Nearly all insurance operations are dependent on risk prediction, the accuracy and fairness of which is paramount. Traditional models of underwriting and risk assessment generally fail to exhibit the complexity and interrelationships associated with an interconnected portfolio. This paper proposes the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) an innovative machine learning architecture that employs graph neural networks, causal inference, and transformer based attention to allow for a more holistic and interpretable consideration of insurance risk. Whereas conventional models assume policyholders represent independent risks, the GCT augments its models of risk by directly modeling relationships between policyholders, assets, and their context, such as supply chains or relative distance. It also distinguishes true causal drivers (or influences) from false correlations. The GCT is evaluated using a very large scale auto insurance dataset augmented with alternative datasets. The GCT model performed better than Generalised Linear Models, XGBoost, Graph Neural Networks and Actuarial Transformers depending upon the performance metric employed, namely AUC, Gini, loss-ratio uplift and fairness. The GCT provides a high degree of interpretability through SHAP based local explanations and causal path insights, therefore supporting those firms who need to comply with emerging regulations associated with the rise of AI. The findings of this study demonstrate that the GCT provides predictive performance benefits while enhancing transparency and governance whilst making it an important candidate for future algorithmic underwriting applications in regulated insurance markets.

 Insurance Risk Prediction Machine Learning Graph Neural Networks Transformers Causal Inference Explainable AI Fairness Algorithmic Underwriting Introduction

One of the most important things in the insurance industry is the ability to correctly predict the risks. Insurers use Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) and regression-based resilience (and related) actuarial methods as their main tools to measure the risk of policyholders. Besides the recognition of the great support among regulators of GLM frameworks, insurers appreciate the perspective during the auditing process, but GLMs are restricted in linearity having constraints on combining features and static relationships which are tending to err with the complex nature of the insurance data available. He insurance sector is undergoing a digital transformation and the insurers can now access a wide variety of new data streams - telematics data on your driving; The Internet of Things with all the sensors coming from your smart home (or smart device); satellite images; and even social media and behavioral data [1]. The availability of the new data creates a wealth of opportunities as well as challenges for the insurers.

Machine learning algorithms like gradient boosting, neural nets, and transformers have been found to be more powerful than the conventional frameworks to make predictions. Machine learning top three barriers are the lack of interpretability, lack of fairness protections, and difficulty in modeling dynamic (or relational) structures of risk [2].

The predictive modeling used in the current predictive methods have a major drawback which is that the treatment of policyholders or assets as single entities dominates the most part of the modeling practice.Risk is not treated this way and often has interconnectedness; proximity, vendors, social connections, and supply chain. In fact, after a natural disaster, hundreds of policyholders will suffer simultaneous losses. In observance of this risk interconnectedness, we intend to emphasize risk interdependence in The Protection Gap Model - an interconnected framework of this nature could create a better consideration of risk both at the level of individual assets and across vehicles/assets within geographies[3]. These relational patterns are ignored in traditional tabular models because systems thinking cannot anticipate systemic exposures or cascading losses. Another contributing factor is the increasing scrutiny, demands for model governance, and transparency, especially given new regulations like the EU AI Act. Increasingly, decision-makers are responsible for explainability, fairness, and accountability when using algorithmic decision making [4], especially with high-stakes applications.

In light of these knowledge gaps, this article proposes a new architecture, the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), as we formalize three breakthroughs in modern machine learning: graph neural networks to model relationships between entities, causal inference to separate true drivers of risk vs. coincident correlations, and transformer attentional mechanisms to propagate feature interactions. Our overall modeling framework, will help enhance not only predictive performance but also fairness, interpretability and ease of adoption in operational insurance systems. This paper begins with a review of related work in traditional actuarial modeling and recent advances in ML for insurance applications. We then present the GCT methodology in detail, describing its core components and training procedures. Next, we evaluate the model on a real-world dataset and benchmark its performance against industry-standard baselines. Following this, we discuss the practical implications of our findings, including interpretability, regulatory compliance, and scalability. Finally, we conclude by summarizing key insights and outlining avenues for future research.

 Related Works

Risk prediction in insurance has advanced quickly in the past 20 years, moving away from traditional actuarial methods to a future with more data and algorithmically driven approaches. This section summarises the broad classes of models that have been part of this evolution and discuss how they are useful for the various challenges this paper seeks to address.

The backbone of insurance risk modeling has also been the Generalised Linear Models (GLM) and Generalised Additive Models (GAMs). The strength of these models is transparency and interpretability which is good for regulators and audits [5]. They are still popular for base pricing, reserving and risk segmentation. The linear/additive assumptions prevent GLMs or GAMs from capturing the complex interactions and non-linearities in high-dimension actuarial data [6].

The new growth of tree-based ensemble methods, namely Random Forests and Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDTs) have shown to be a substantial improvement in predictive power over linear models. Tree-based ensemble methods do not require any specification of non-linear relationships or characterisation of feature interactions [7]. XGBoost has emerged as a popular tool among insurers primarily due to its efficiency, accuracy, and tendency to outperform many similar models in terms of overfitting risk. Nevertheless, these models operate under the independence assumption and neglect structured, relational, or timing dependencies. Each data point is still treated independently even if the underlying relationship structure is a concern in areas like fraud detection or catastrophe modeling [8].

More recently, deep learning architectures, such as fully connected networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), have been applied to insurance challenges in the document classification, image inspection, and voice triage realms [9], [10]. While these models can identify representations of unstructured or semi-structured information very well, this black-box architecture creates many challenges socially (usability and acceptability) and by regulators who embrace a more fiduciary duty and want some degree of model transparency.

With increasing demands for accuracy and transparency to support decision making, researchers have recently introduced the Actuarial Transformer (AT) [11] as a specific application of a transformer architecture, developed for insurance scenarios and has been specifically customized for tabular insurance data. AT models utilize attention and they are powerful because they allow modelers to capture relationships in global feature space simultaneously, while enabling some interpretability given researchers can evaluate the attention weights or SHAP explanations. Still, these models make the same i.i.d. independence assumption and they treat observations with structured relationships independently in the same general spirit as the deep learning models before. To progress the state of relational dependencies, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have gained traction for use in insurance applications (again, specifically in fraud detection and supply-chain risk modeling [12]). GNNs use graphs to model data such that nodes are entities and edges represent relationships (e.g., co-claims, proximity, shared service providers). Historically, researchers have shown GNNs outperform traditional methods in identifying patterns of fraud that occur in clusters or rings [13]. However, existing methods, including GNNs, generally lack the capacity for causal reasoning, which is particularly important for fairness and generalizability.

Alongside the development and application of graph learning methods, causal inference has gained traction in the insurance context, particularly in fairness-aware modeling and reducing bias. As examples, double machine learning, structural equation modeling, and causal discovery algorithms (e.g., NOTEARS) have been proposed and operationalized to help distinguish between spurious correlations and causal drivers of risk [14], [15]. The distinction between correlation and other variables or causal factors is important because, in cases where regulators specify that insurers cannot take certain variables into consideration (e.g., gender, zip code), insurers must demonstrate model behavior that goes beyond simply being statistically significant. However, there is no single model that currently combines adversarial structures, causal reasoning, and deep attention mechanisms in a humanly interpretable model for risk prediction. This paper seeks to fill that gap by proposing the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), a new model that we have developed which attempts to remedy the limitation of current methods and provide an adaptable, interpretable, regulation-friendly framework for modern insurers.

 Methodology

In this section, we present the proposed Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) architecture to capture the complex, relational, and dynamic nature of insurance risk. The GCT combines graph learning, causal inference, and attentionbased deep learning in a single architecture. The GCT incorporates local interactions (e.g., individual policyholder behavior) and global interactions (e.g., systemic or cascading risks across networks); it also leverages interpretability and fairness within regulatory constraints.

We are representing the portfolio as a graph $\mathrm{G}=(V, E)$ with $\vert\mathrm{V}\vert=\mathrm{N}$ nodes (policyholders/assets/actors) and $\vert\mathrm{E}\vert$ edges (relations). Node features $\mathrm{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d 0 X}$.with $d$, the graph has depth $L$, and attention heads $H$. These symbols are used to denote the complexity of the model below.

Graph-Causal-Transformer (Gct) Architecture

The Guided Collaborative Transformer (GCT) architecture consists of three modules that are tightly coupled. Each module defines a unique facet of the model's predictive and interpretive capabilities:

Graph-causal-transformer(gct)overview.

Graph Construction Layer: The first layer depicts the core entities of insurance as nodes in the graph, which is constructed dynamically. To illustrate, nodes in the graph represent policyholders, insured items, third parties, brokers, and corporate ties. Two nodes are connected with an edge, which is defined by the various relations we have foundfamilial ties, same addresses, same vendors, supply-chain associations, or behavior, depending on telematics. Data streams (event logs, IoT telemetries, claims activity) are processed almost re. With the help of a distributed streaming platform like Apache Kafka, the model can now have a live graph with minimum latency. The dynamic closed relational networks depicted in the graph allow the model to be very sensitive to relationships and risk aggregations that are hidden when traditional tabular approaches are used,Graph message passing. \begin{equation*} \begin{gathered} h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(0)}=W_{0} x_{\mathcal{V}}, \quad m_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=\sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(\mathcal{V})} \alpha_{u \mathcal{V}}^{(k)} W_{m}^{(k)} h_{\mathcal{u}}^{(k-1)}, \\ h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=\sigma\left(W_{h}^{(k)}\left[h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k-1)} \vert m_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}\right]\right) \end{gathered} \end{equation*} with attention weights $\alpha_{u v}^{(k)}=\operatorname{softmax}_{u}\left(a\left(h_{u}^{(k-1)}, h_{v}^{(k-1)}\right)\right)$

Causal Inference Module: In order to mitigate the adverse implications of spurious correlations and fairness concerns, our model utilizes a causal inference layer. This causal inference layer can use attachment approaches like Double Machine Learning (DML) to estimate causal effects or NOTEARS for structure learning directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational data [14]. Specifically, it identifies causally-relevant features and pathways especially when node features can vary by things like protected or regulated variables (i.e. gender, geography). The advantage of this structure is that it improves the model's generalizability across demographic changes and regulatory constraints, and it mitigates the risk of overfitting to historically biased data. Causal signal and fairness regulization. \begin{equation*} \tilde{h}_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}=h_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}+R_{\text {causal }}(\mathcal{V}), \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} \mathcal{L}_{\text {causal }}=\sum_{j}\left(\widehat{\mathcal{T}}_{j}+\mathcal{T}_{j}^{*}\right)^{2}+\gamma \operatorname{Dep}(f(X), Z \text { protected }) \end{equation*} Where $\quad \widehat{\mathcal{T}}_{j} \quad$ are treatment-effect estimates from DML/NOTEARS-derived structure, and Dep ($\cdot$) penalizes dependence on protected attributes or their proxies.

Transformer-Based Encoder-Decoder: The predictive engine is based on a transformer architecture that has been specifically adapted to incorporate graph-aware tabular data. In the encoder phase, raw node embeddings based solely on the underlying graph structure are augmented by causal relevance scores and passed through a multi-head selfattention layer. This allows the model to prioritize and combine information from both the local node features and the relational context of the node itself. Furthermore, the general architecture supports multitask learning modes, and thus, allows the model to simultaneously learn jointly to predict several targets that are important in insurance operations:

Claim severity, trained using Tweedie loss (appropriate for zero-inflated, right-skewed data).

Claim frequency, modeled with Poisson or Negative Binomial loss.

Churn propensity, framed as a binary classification task using cross-entropy loss.

Graph-aware attention. \begin{equation*} \begin{array}{r} \mathcal{Q}=X W_{Q}, K=X W_{K}, V=X W_{V}, Z=\operatorname{MHAttn}(Q, K, V) \\ =\text { Concat }_{h} \operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{Q_{h} K_{h}}{\sqrt{d / H}}\right) V_{h} \end{array} \end{equation*}

We augment token/node embeddings with $\tilde{h}_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}$ before attention.

The multi-head self-attention component adds explainability by clarifying the most relevant features and relationships for each prediction.

Multi-task objective. \begin{equation*} \mathcal{L}=\lambda_{1} \mathcal{L}_{\text {severity }}^{\text {Tweedie }}+\lambda_{2} \mathcal{L}_{\text {frequency }}^{\text {Poisson } / \text { NegBin }}+\lambda_{3} \mathcal{L}_{\text {churn }}^{\text {CE }}+\lambda_{4} \mathcal{L}_{\text {causal }} \end{equation*}

Fairness & Metric Definitions

We report Demographic Parity Gap (DPG): \begin{equation*} D P G=\vert\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Z=1]-\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Z=0]\vert \end{equation*} Where Z is a protected-group indicator and $\hat{Y}$ the predicted risk /decision score.

 Training Pipeline

The process of training GCT entails four distinct phases with the goal of getting the most out of data while promoting model robustness, interpretability, and fairness:

Data Preprocessing: The training pipeline consumes five years of multimodal insurance (policy and claims) data including structured records produced during the insurance transaction (e.g., policy and claims data), unstructured text (e.g., claim adjuster notes), and sensor-generated time-series data such as vehicle telemetry and smart home devices, as well as external context datasets (e.g., weather patterns, crime indices, and economic indicators). The records are normalized, encoded, aligned temporally, and represented as graphs.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: Before supervised learning, the model is pretrained through self-supervised tasks that include a means of obtaining structural or semantic information via edge prediction (making predictions using missing or future links between nodes), and node feature reconstruction (predicting node attributes of a node based on the node's neighborhood). This feature is more likely to allow the model to generalize and is a way to helpthe potential data sparsity and other issues.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: The model is initially trained with self-supervised learning frameworks followed by supervised learning. The self-supervised learning tasks were centered on the extraction of structural and semantic features, for instance, edge prediction (i.e., predicting missing or new edges between nodes) and node feature reconstruction (i.e., predicting node features from neighborhood distributions). This phase opens up the path for more generalization from the data while resolving problems such as data sparsity.

Gct training workflow.

 Experimental Results

Describing the experimental design to evaluate the performance of the new Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) model, this section includes the characteristics of the dataset used, baseline models, and the evaluation results presented not only in one but in several important metrics. The model will be measured against its predictive accuracy, fairness, explainability, and operational consideration.

Dataset

The experiments were conducted on the CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) Auto Insurance Claims Dataset, a large-scale dataset, created in collaboration with the CAS (Casualty Actuarial Society), the dataset is known as a benchmark for risk modeling in the auto insurance domain, fraud detection, and telemetry pricing, used widely in both academia and the industry for benchmarking purposes. OpenWeatherMap provided the weather data, while Eurostat provided the economic indicators; these and other publicly available data sources were used to further enrich the data.

Size: 1.2 million unique policy records, including approximately 80,000 claims.

Features: 180 structured tabular features (e.g., demographics, vehicle info, policy terms), 15 timeseries telematics fields (e.g., acceleration, speed, braking patterns), and geospatial metadata (e.g., region codes, traffic risk zones).

Data Splitting: A stratified 80-10-10 train-validationtest split was applied. To ensure statistical stability, all metrics are averaged over three runs with different random seeds.

Data Source: CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) by the Casualty Actuarial Society, supplemented with OpenWeatherMap and Eurostat datasets for external feature enrichment.

Baseline Models

The To benchmark the performance of the GCT model, we compare it against four commonly used models in the insurance industry:

GLM (Generalized Linear Model): The actuarial standard, included for baseline interpretability.

XGBoost: A popular gradient boosting method, known for its performance and flexibility.

Actuarial Transformer: A transformer-based model optimized for structured tabular data, incorporating attentionbased feature learning [3].

Graph Neural Network (GNN): A relational learning model that leverages graph topology but lacks explicit causal or attention mechanisms.

Each model was trained on the same dataset and evaluated using five metrics: AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve), Gini coefficient, uplift in loss ratio over the GLM baseline, fairness (measured as demographic parity gap), and explanation latency using SHAP (in milliseconds). Benchmark results on casd
Model AUC (Claims) Gini Loss-Ratio Uplift Demographic Gap ↓ SHAP Latency (ms)
GLM 0.78 0.56 Baseline 0.00 5
XGBoost 0.85 0.70 +5.1% 0.07 12
Actuarial Transformer 0.88 0.76 +7.3% 0.06 55
GNN 0.86 0.72 +6.0% 0.05 23
GCT (proposed model) 0.91 0.80 +10.4% 0.02 61

Observations:

Predictive Performance: GCT achieves the highest AUC (0.91) and Gini coefficient (0.80), indicating superior discriminatory power in identifying high-risk policyholders.

Fairness: The demographic parity gap is significantly lower in GCT (0.02) compared to other ML models, suggesting that the causal module successfully mitigates bias introduced by correlated demographic features.

Business Impact: GCT improves portfolio loss ratio by 10.4 % relative to GLM, outperforming all baselines.

Explainability: While GCT incurs slightly higher explanation latency due to its layered architecture, it remains within acceptable operational thresholds ($<100 ~\text{ms}$), and the added interpretability from causal-path tracing offers substantial governance value.

These results demonstrate that the proposed architecture provides a well-balanced trade-off between accuracy, fairness, and explainability-making it suitable for high-stakes, regulated insurance environments.

 Computational Complexity & Optimizations.

Per batch, GNN message passing is $\mathcal{O}(M d)$ per layer; transformer self-attention is $\mathcal{O}\left(n^{2} d\right)$ for token length nnn; causal regularization $\mathcal{O}(N d)-\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ depending on the estimator. Optimizations: mini-batch graph sampling (GraphSAGE/Cluster-GCN), sparse/linear attention, parameter sharing, mixed precision, quantization/distillation for edge/real-time use, and GPU batching for SHAP. This keeps GCT deployable for daily scoring while reserving heavier causal audits for batch windows.

 Discussion

This part initially discusses the consequences of the experimental findings by highlighting the benefits of the proposed Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) alongside some of the practical issues in its deployment in the insurance field.

Strengths of the Gct Model

The GCT model is enriched with several benefits that give it an edge over the previous methods adopted in the literature. These benefits are further discussed in terms of relational modeling, fairness, and operationalization.

Relational Reasoning: The GCT model takes into account the relationships that exist among different conditional independence structures and develops a latent structure that acknowledges the overlap between policyholders, assets, and contextual variables as one single unit. This was a feature that made it superior compared to traditional univariate models that consider all risks as independent observations. The GCT model is the most appropriate for cases where there are systematic exposures (e.g., regional weather, supply chain risks, fraud rings). In such instances, the GCT model will be instrumental in modeling both cascading and accumulation risks, which is a great advantage in portfolios that are increasingly sensitive to climate and exposed to cyber risks.

Fairness and Regulatory Compliance: Introducing a causal inference module enables GCT to analyze and potentially remove some reliance on proxy variables that may incur observed bias. This has potential benefits in terms of fairness of predictions, particularly regarding protected attributes such as age, gender or location. The causal modeling capability of the model allows it to be better positioned for conforming to any emerging regulations related to AI, such as the EU AI Act and NAIC Model Law 687, which both require proof of protections against algorithmic discrimination.

Operational Readiness: Even though I anticipate that GCT's architecture will be somewhat complicated as far as its logic and structure are concerned, it was conceived to be scalable to deployment. Dynamic graph partitioning enables modifications to the graph structure that are caused by new claims, policy changes, or telemetry signals to take place in a localized manner and be communicated quickly. Besides, well-organized model documentation (e.g., model cards) with integrated explainability has the potential of assisting governance both internally and externally which, in turn, will be the approval of the model in the regulated insurance environment.

 Practical Considerations

Despite GCT having its fair share of advantages, its introduction in the actual world is laden with various hiccups. These challenges need to be solved first if GCT is to perform as expected.

Data Latency and Integration: A significant portion of the graph's success depends on the timely ingestion and integration of event data, such as claims and telematics streams. To keep the graph's freshness and prediction relevance, a reliable data pipeline with real-time stream processing (e.g., Apache Kafka or similar) would be required.

Computational Efficiency: The computation of the inference within GCT is more complicated than in the case of traditional ensemble methods due to the use of attention mechanisms and graph embeddings. Nevertheless, there are some opportunities for performance optimization (e.g., parameter sharing, attention pruning, and model quantization), but there will still be a certain amount of extra computing resources required. In the case of high-throughput applications such as usage-based insurance and $\mu$-underwriting, we can easily use the lightweight versions of GCT which have the reduced data on-edge or are accelerated with GPUs.

Interpretability and User Trust: The success of any ML system in insurance is not only based on the system's performance but also on user trust. These GCT explore built-in explainability tools, but their worth is contingent upon appropriate usability. Visualization tools (like SHAP-based dashboards, causal path diagrams, etc.) can be co-designed with actuaries and underwriters, - given that model outputs are familiar for accessible interpretation and can be consistent with domain expertise. We must include frontline users in this process to gain widespread acceptance.

 Conclusion

This research put forward a new architecture of risk prediction in insurance namely the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), an amalgam of graph-based learning, causal inference, and transformer-based attention mechanisms, which serves as an interpretable model. The policyholders are traditional models that isolate, GCT instead exploits the relational aspect of an insurance portfolio and identifies conditional and structural dependencies not only between the entities but also between the supply chains, geographical clusters, and behavioral networks. As GCT provides the structural information, it is able to incorporate causal reasoning, thus increasing predictive accuracy as well as promoting fairness and regulatory accountability.

We built a large-scale and industry-relevant dataset to carry out empirical experiments, and the results showed that the GCT outperforms several baseline methods, including GLMs, gradient boosting, graph neural networks, and actuarial transformers, on different aspects-predictive performance (AUC and Gini) for business value (uplift on loss ratios), and for ethical practice (differential disparity in demographic variables). Thus, we have gone a long way towards making the first, second, and third comparisons stronger without losing the potential for the model's explainability, which, in the regulated insurance field, is a crucial feature.

Streaming data. The explainability component of the model relies on SHAP values and causal path tracing. This explainability layer is also available for the use of internal governance, regulatory audits, and underwriting trust.

In terms of future research and applied improvement, GCT creates many paths forward. For example, GCT can extend the architecture into multi-line or reinsurance situations, use foundation models to comprehend document level understanding, or apply reinforcement learning to provide dynamic pricing options. As the insurance industry algorithmically assisted underwriters, models like GCT represent an essential leap to make risk management more intelligent, potentially adaptive, and responsible.

Kubernetes excels in providing performance and scalability for cloud-native applications, but its energy consumption is a significant concern regarding sustainability. The aim of this research is to address this issue by integrating energy efficiency into Kubernetes. We examine three fundamental areas. First, we discuss new methods in monitoring and measuring energy usage. Second, we examine current best practice systems for energy-conscious scheduling. Third, we discuss architectural patterns employed for efficient energy management. The survey discusses fundamental tools and techniques, identifies research and operation-facing challenges, and charts a path forward to attain sustainable, energy-conscious practices in cloud-native Kubernetes clusters.

 Cloud-Native Applications Kubernetes Energy Efficiency Energy Monitoring Resource Management EnergyAware Scheduling Carbon-Aware Scheduling Introduction

Despite its leadership in container orchestration, Kubernetes faces a growing challenge in energy efficiency. Achieving this requires balancing energy savings from over-provisioning against performance loss from under-provisioning [1]. This necessitates dynamic optimization via autoscaling [2], rightsizing [3], consolidation [4], and innovation in monitoring and scheduling [5].

In spite of this emphasis, current studies offer minimal in-depth information on energy optimization in Kubernetes. Previous research focus on different domains, like VM consolidation [6], overall cloud or container power consumption without significant Kubernetes details [7], non-orchestrated container performance [8], or certain algorithmic aspects in Kubernetes. Even pertinent research from domains like HPC [9], [10] is not applicable because of Kubernetes' distinct orchestration layer and microservice models. Thus, an actual gap exists here: there is no dedicated survey that appropriately addresses the intersection of optimization methods, metrics required, and multiple scheduling approaches adequate for Kubernetes energy efficiency.

This article specifically aims to cover this gap by providing a comprehensive rundown of where energy efficiency stands in Kubernetes deployments. This paper integrates current advancements, starting with a review of basic monitoring and measurement methods, tools, and challenges, which is then followed by a discussion of state-of-the-art energy-aware scheduling systems and architectural proposals. In summary,

this work seeks to give both researchers and practitioners a detailed technical review, outline the current state of the art, highlight existing challenges, and identify potential directions for enhancing energy efficiency in Kubernetes.

 Monitoring and Measurement of Energy Consumption

Optimizing Kubernetes energy efficiency relies on suitable monitoring and measurement [4]. Accurate energy data is key, providing cluster usage insights [11] to find inefficiencies and evaluate optimizations. This section discusses measurement needs, compares tools/models, explores granularity, and examines complexities in Kubernetes energy monitoring.

Energy Monitoring Frameworks and Tools

A variety of frameworks and tools are emerging to monitor energy consumption within Kubernetes, differing in methodology, data sources, detail level, and precision. One prominent example is Kepler (Kubernetes-based Efficient Power Level Exporter), a CNCF Sandbox project that estimates processlevel power using eBPF and a ratio power model, exporting data as Prometheus metrics. However, its accuracy has been questioned in independent studies, particularly concerning how it assigns power to inactive containers and system processes [11], [21].

Another tool, POET (Platform for O-RAN Energy Efficiency Testing), focuses specifically on measuring and modeling Energy Efficiency (EE) and Energy Savings (ES) in Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) 5G deployments running on Kubernetes. It integrates measurements from physical hardware and containerized network functions (CNFs/VNFs), using external devices for server-level power capture, highlighting the need for domain-specific approaches in areas like telecommunications [3], [12]. Scaphandre is an opensource tool utilizing RAPL (Running Average Power Limit) to measure application energy consumption, correlating CPU usage with energy draw to estimate power per process. When deployed as a DaemonSet in Kubernetes, it labels metrics with pod and namespace information. Its accuracy hinges on RAPL availability and the assumption linking CPU usage directly to power consumption [13], [14]. KubeWatt presents a different model, using the Redfish API for total node power measurement and Kubernetes metrics APIs for CPU utilization data. It separates node power into static and dynamic parts, attributing the dynamic portion to containers based on their CPU usage. Research indicates that KubeWatt offers better accuracy than Kepler, notably by avoiding power attribution to idle containers [11]. Finally, CEEMS provides a monitoring stack designed for diverse environments, leveraging standard observability tools to gather data and report real-time energy use and estimated CO 2 emissions. Its flexible architecture supports custom rules for estimating workload energy consumption [16].

The following table provides a comparative analysis of these key monitoring tools, summarizing their core attributes to guide selection for different use cases. Comparison of energy monitoring tools
Tool Primary Technology Measurement Granularity Key Features Primary Limitation(s)
Kepler Hardware Counters model, RAPL Process, Container, Pod, Node Prometheus Export, ML Model option, eBPF support Model Accuracy, Hardware Dependency (for RAPL), Overhead.
POET Hardware Sensors, Container Metrics System, Container O-RAN specific, Bare-metal + K8s integration Specific Use Case (Telecom/ORAN).
Scaphandre RAPL, other sensors Process, Host (Node) Prometheus Export, Lightweight Hardware Dependency, Attribution less granular.
KubeWatt Typically RAPL or other hardware sensors Node, potentially Container Prometheus Export Hardware Dependency, Granularity may vary.
CEEMS Integrates underlying tools (e.g., Kepler/Scaphandre), RAPL Container, Pod, Node Prometheus/ Grafana Integration, Carbon Metrics Depends on underlying collector accuracy, Setup Complexity.

The ongoing development and comparative analysis of these tools, particularly the contrasting approaches of Kepler and KubeWatt regarding container-level attribution, highlight a critical maturation phase in Kubernetes energy monitoring. The focus is shifting decisively from basic node-level measurements towards obtaining accurate, actionable data at the workload (container/pod) level.

 Granularity and Challenges in Energy Measurement

Energy consumption in Kubernetes can be measured or estimated at various levels of granularity, each presenting distinct advantages and challenges:

Process-level: Offers the highest granularity, potentially allowing fine-tuning of individual application components. How-ever, accurately measuring or attributing energy consumption to specific processes is technically complex, often requiring kernel-level mechanisms like eBPF (used by KubeWatt [11]) or estimation models based on resource proxies like CPU usage (used by Scaphandre [14]).

Container-level: This level consolidates the consumption of all processes within a container, a key granularity for managing containerized applications. Tools typically achieve this by aggregating process-level estimates or by correlating container resource usage (via cgroups [14]) with node-level power data using attribution models (e.g., Kepler, KubeWatt [11]). This area is a primary focus of current research and tool development [11].

Pod-level: A pod represents one or more tightly coupled containers scheduled together on the same node [17]. Podlevel energy consumption is the sum of the consumption of its constituent containers. This level is useful for understanding the energy footprint of a deployable application unit within Kubernetes. Tools like Kepler, Scaphandre, and CEEMS provide metrics aggregated at this level [11].

Node-level: This refers to the overall energy use of a Kubernetes worker node (physical or virtual). Measuring at this level is often simpler, using hardware sensors via IPMI/Redfish or external devices like smart PDUs [11]. The main difficulty is assigning this total energy to the various workloads (pods, containers, system processes) on the node. Although crucial for container-level attribution models like KubeWatt [11], node-level data alone offers little direct information on specific application efficiency.

 Persistent Challenges in Energy Measurement

Despite the progress made by tools like Kepler and CEEMS, several fundamental challenges persist across the field, limiting the accuracy and comparability of energy measurements in Kubernetes.

Hardware Heterogeneity: Production clusters are almost never homogeneous. They typically comprise a diverse set of nodes with varying generations of CPUs, GPUs, and other accelerators. Each piece of hardware has a distinct power profile, making it challenging to create a single, unified power model that is valid across the entire heterogeneous fleet. A model that has been trained on one server generation can be very inaccurate on another generation.

Measurement Overhead: Measurement itself consumes resources. Monitoring frameworks need CPU cycles, memory, and network bandwidth, each consuming its own share of energy. Even when tools such as CEEMS are frugal in their demands, with scrapes taking merely microseconds, this overhead can become a serious consideration in large clusters.

Lack of Standardization: The cloud-native platform today lacks a uniform set of measurements, APIs, and methodologies to measure and compare energy usage. In the absence of a common reference point, making like-for-like comparisons of the effectiveness of different tools, schedulers, and optimization methods is difficult. Observations reported in one research work might turn out to be non-reproducible or non-comparable to another.

 Energy-Aware Scheduling and Management Architectures

Building on energy monitoring insights, advanced tools and technologies are actively improving Kubernetes cluster energy efficiency. Moving beyond observation, these solutions employ intelligent controls for workload scheduling and energy resource management. Key developments include sophisticated energy-aware scheduling systems that optimize workload placement based on energy or carbon factors, alongside novel architectural patterns leveraging Kubernetes for enhanced energy management.

Energy-Aware Scheduling Systems

The standard Kubernetes scheduler determines pod placement on nodes, mainly considering resource availability (CPU, memory) and user constraints like affinity rules or taints/tolerations [18]. Extending this, energy-aware schedulers incorporate energy consumption or related metrics like carbon intensity into the decision process, forming a significant research area.

Several approaches exemplify this field. CarbonScaler dynamically adjusts server allocation for batch workloads based on fluctuating grid carbon intensity, using forecasting and an optimal greedy algorithm to achieve substantial carbon savings (e.g., up to 32.9 % for a variant [20]) often without major delays [19], [20]. Smart-Kube uses Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to automatically learn fair, energy-aware scheduling policies, adapting to cluster changes to manage resource utilization and energy use without extensive manual tuning [1]. The Low Carbon Kubernetes Scheduler prioritizes spatial shifting, assigning pods to nodes in geographic regions with the lowest current carbon intensity, effectively moving energy demand to cleaner locations [15]. Similarly, GreenCourier spatially shifts serverless functions [22].

Opportunistic scheduling using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) models complex data center relationships to predict the energy impact of container placement, achieving energy reductions (e.g., 6.2 % average) compared to default Kubernetes scheduling [23]. GNNs show promise for broader cloud resource management tasks [24], [25]. Another tactic is temporal shifting, delaying non-essential workloads to times with lower grid carbon intensity, potentially increasing job duration but reducing emissions [26], with architectures like GAIA aiming to balance carbon, performance, and cost [27].

There is significant research interest in leveraging AI/ML, particularly Reinforcement Learning and GNNs, for Kubernetes scheduling [28], offering adaptability advantages over traditional heuristics [18] or optimization algorithms [19]. These AI/ML methods enable more autonomous and potentially effective energy-aware systems [1], [2].  Energy Management Architectures

Innovation is also occurring in the architectural patterns used for energy management, sometimes leveraging Kubernetes itself as a platform:

Kubernetes-Container-Cluster-Based Architecture for EMS: This approach focuses on enhancing the reliability and resource utilization of Energy Management Systems (EMS) - the software systems used to monitor and control energy infrastructure (like power grids) - by deploying the EMS components themselves as containerized applications on a Kubernetes cluster [29]. By leveraging Kubernetes' features like container isolation, fault tolerance, and self-healing [29], this architecture aims to improve EMS uptime and resilience compared to traditional monolithic or Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) deployments. A dynamic Pod fault-tolerant model, potentially based on Markov theory, can be used to automatically adjust the redundancy of EMS microservices to meet reliability targets while optimizing resource usage [29]. Studies suggest this approach can significantly reduce annual failure time and improve hardware utilization for the EMS itself [29].

Cloud-Based Smart Energy Framework: Kubernetes is also being utilized as a scalable backend for data analytics in the broader smart energy domain [30]. Frameworks like the one developed for Chulalongkorn University's Building Energy Management System (CUBEMS) use Kubernetes to orchestrate containerized data analytic workloads [30]. By employing a divide-andconquer paradigm and parallel processing across Kubernetes pods, these frameworks can accelerate the analysis of large datasets generated by smart meters and energy sensors [30]. Similarly, Kubernetes can host platforms for training and deploying ML models used in energy consumption prediction [31].

Future Directions

After reviewing current monitoring tools, systems, and scheduling techniques, there are many strategies that can be applied by practitioners to make their Kubernetes deployments more energy efficient. However, substantial research challenges remain, highlighting promising avenues for future innovation.

Strategies for Sustainable Kubernetes Deployments

The insights gained from the examination of monitoring tools and advanced scheduling platforms can be synthesized in a collection of actionable tactics that organizations can leverage to ensure their Kubernetes deployments are more energy efficient. Tactics vary from fundamental configuration practice to the adoption of advanced, AI-driven tools, and provide an iterative path to sustainability. Table III - (Comparison of different kubernetes deployment Strategy) provides a convenient reference, pairing high-level tactics with the actual Kubernetes features and tools facilitating them.

 Key Gaps and Remaining Challenges

Even though there has been progress, some major challenges still make it hard for energy efficiency measures to be widely used and fully effective in Kubernetes.

Lack of Standardized Measurement Metrics & Benchmarks: As previously discussed, the absence of universally accepted metrics, measurement protocols, and benchmarks makes it difficult to objectively compare different tools, validate savings claims, and establish best practices [12].

Real-World Validation and Scalability: Many research proposals are validated primarily through simulation or small-scale experiments. Demonstrating effectiveness and scalability in large, dynamic, real-world production environments remains a persistent challenge [1].

Promising Directions for Future Research and Development

Addressing the remaining challenges requires continued innovation across multiple fronts. Key future directions include:

AI-driven Optimization Opportunities: Further research into applying AI/ML techniques for more accurate predictive modeling (of workload demand, energy consumption, carbon intensity), intelligent and adaptive resource allocation (scheduling, autoscaling), automated policy generation, and anomaly detection related to energy inefficiency [2]. This includes using AI not just to optimize Kubernetes, but also using AI for optimizing cloud-native systems more broadly [5].

Development of Multi-Objective Optimization Frameworks: Creating more robust theoretical frameworks and

practical tools (e.g., advanced schedulers, policy engines) that can explicitly model and systematically optimize for multiple, potentially conflicting, objectives simultaneously (energy, performance, cost, carbon, fairness, etc.) [20].

Standardized Benchmarking Methodologies: Establishing "standard benchmarks", representative workloads, metrics, and evaluation methodologies specifically for assessing the energy efficiency and performance tradeoffs of Kubernetes tools and strategies. This is crucial for enabling fair comparison, reproducibility, and driving progress in the field [9].

The parallel focus on edge computing introduces new challenges and opportunities, demanding specialized solutions that account for the unique constraints of distributed, resourcelimited environments [32].

 Conclusion

The optimization of energy-efficient Kubernetes operations is an imperative step towards a more sustainable cloud-native future. This survey reviewed the current state of energy efficiency in Kubernetes, a highly relevant topic due to the platform's widespread use in cloud computing. Accurate measurement and continuous monitoring play a vital role, forming the foundation for effective energy optimization efforts. Although significant advances have been made in developing energy visibility tools and smart workload placement algorithms, optimizing energy use in complex Kubernetes configurations remains an ongoing challenge critical to both environmental sustainability and cost savings.

Despite recent advancements, several key challenges persist, most notably the lack of standard measures for energy analysis, which does not allow comparison and verification of novel approaches. Furthermore, the need for research solutions to be adaptable to real-world production environments remains a key barrier. Breaking through these barriers is essential for large-scale implementation of energy conservation strategies. Future work is heading for more sophisticated, autonomous directions, including AI-driven predictive modeling, dynamic resource planning, and balanced systems that balance energy consumption with performance, cost, and emissions.

Kubernetes excels in providing performance and scalability for cloud-native applications, but its energy consumption is a significant concern regarding sustainability. The aim of this research is to address this issue by integrating energy efficiency into Kubernetes. We examine three fundamental areas. First, we discuss new methods in monitoring and measuring energy usage. Second, we examine current best practice systems for energy-conscious scheduling. Third, we discuss architectural patterns employed for efficient energy management. The survey discusses fundamental tools and techniques, identifies research and operation-facing challenges, and charts a path forward to attain sustainable, energy-conscious practices in cloud-native Kubernetes clusters.

 Cloud-Native Applications Kubernetes Energy Efficiency Energy Monitoring Resource Management Energy-Aware Scheduling Carbon-Aware Scheduling Introduction

Despite its leadership in container orchestration, Kubernetes faces a growing challenge in energy efficiency. Achieving this requires balancing energy savings from over-provisioning against performance loss from under-provisioning [1]. This necessitates dynamic optimization via autoscaling [2], right-sizing [3], consolidation [4], and innovation in monitoring and scheduling [5].

In spite of this emphasis, current studies offer minimal in-depth information on energy optimization in Kubernetes. Previous research focus on different domains, like VM consolidation [6], overall cloud or container power consumption without significant Kubernetes details [7], non-orchestrated container performance [8], or certain algorithmic aspects in Kubernetes. Even pertinent research from domains like HPC [9], [10] is not applicable because of Kubernetes' distinct orchestration layer and microservice models. Thus, an actual gap exists here: there is no dedicated survey that appropriately addresses the intersection of optimization methods, metrics required, and multiple scheduling approaches adequate for Kubernetes energy efficiency.

This article specifically aims to cover this gap by providing a comprehensive rundown of where energy efficiency stands in Kubernetes deployments. This paper integrates current advancements, starting with a review of basic monitoring and measurement methods, tools, and challenges, which is then followed by a discussion of state-of-the-art energy-aware scheduling systems and architectural proposals. In summary, this work seeks to give both researchers and practitioners a detailed technical review, outline the current state of the art, highlight existing challenges, and identify potential directions for enhancing energy efficiency in Kubernetes.

 Monitoring and Measurement of Energy Consumption

Optimizing Kubernetes energy efficiency relies on suitable monitoring and measurement [4]. Accurate energy data is key, providing cluster usage insights [11] to find inefficiencies and evaluate optimizations. This section discusses measurement needs, compares tools/models, explores granularity, and examines complexities in Kubernetes energy monitoring.

Energy Monitoring Frameworks and Tools

A variety of frameworks and tools are emerging to monitor energy consumption within Kubernetes, differing in methodology, data sources, detail level, and precision. One prominent example is Kepler (Kubernetes-based Efficient Power Level Exporter), a CNCF Sandbox project that estimates process-level power using eBPF and a ratio power model, exporting data as Prometheus metrics. However, its accuracy has been questioned in independent studies, particularly concerning how it assigns power to inactive containers and system processes [11], [21].

Another tool, POET (Platform for O-RAN Energy Efficiency Testing), focuses specifically on measuring and modeling Energy Efficiency (EE) and Energy Savings (ES) in Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) 5G deployments running on Kubernetes. It integrates measurements from physical hardware and containerized network functions (CNFs/VNFs), using external devices for server-level power capture, highlighting the need for domain-specific approaches in areas like telecommunications [3], [12]. Scaphandre is an open-source tool utilizing RAPL (Running Average Power Limit) to measure application energy consumption, correlating CPU usage with energy draw to estimate power per process. When deployed as a DaemonSet in Kubernetes, it labels metrics with pod and namespace information. Its accuracy hinges on RAPL availability and the assumption linking CPU usage directly to power consumption [13], [14]. KubeWatt presents a different model, using the Redfish API for total node power measurement and Kubernetes metrics APIs for CPU utilization data. It separates node power into static and dynamic parts, attributing the dynamic portion to containers based on their CPU usage. Research indicates that KubeWatt offers better accuracy than Kepler, notably by avoiding power attribution to idle containers [11]. Finally, CEEMS provides a monitoring stack designed for diverse environments, leveraging standard observability tools to gather data and report real-time energy use and estimated CO 2 emissions. Its flexible architecture supports custom rules for estimating workload energy consumption [16].

The ongoing development and comparative analysis of these tools, particularly the contrasting approaches of Kepler and KubeWatt regarding container-level attribution, highlight a critical maturation phase in Kubernetes energy monitoring. The focus is shifting decisively from basic node-level measurements towards obtaining accurate, actionable data at the workload (container/pod) level.

 Granularity and Challenges in Energy Measurement

Energy consumption in Kubernetes can be measured or estimated at various levels of granularity, each presenting distinct advantages and challenges:

Process-level: Offers the highest granularity, potentially allowing fine-tuning of individual application components. How-ever, accurately measuring or attributing energy consumption to specific processes is technically complex, often requiring kernel-level mechanisms like eBPF (used by KubeWatt [11]) or estimation models based on resource proxies like CPU usage (used by Scaphandre [14]).

Container-level: This level consolidates the consumption of all processes within a container, a key granularity for managing containerized applications. Tools typically achieve this by aggregating process-level estimates or by correlating container resource usage (via cgroups [14]) with node-level power data using attribution models (e.g., Kepler, KubeWatt [11]). This area is a primary focus of current research and tool development [11].

Pod-level: A pod represents one or more tightly coupled containers scheduled together on the same node [17]. Podlevel energy consumption is the sum of the consumption of its constituent containers. This level is useful for understanding the energy footprint of a deployable application unit within Kubernetes. Tools like Kepler, Scaphandre, and CEEMS provide metrics aggregated at this level [11].

Node-level: This refers to the overall energy use of a Kubernetes worker node (physical or virtual). Measuring at this level is often simpler, using hardware sensors via IPMI/Redfish or external devices like smart PDUs [11]. The main difficulty is assigning this total energy to the various workloads (pods, containers, system processes) on the node. Although crucial for container-level attribution models like KubeWatt [11], node-level data alone offers little direct information on specific application efficiency.

 Persistent Challenges in Energy Measurement

Despite the progress made by tools like Kepler and CEEMS, several fundamental challenges persist across the field, limiting the accuracy and comparability of energy measurements in Kubernetes.

Hardware Heterogeneity: Production clusters are almost never homogeneous. They typically comprise a diverse set of nodes with varying generations of CPUs, GPUs, and other accelerators. Each piece of hardware has a distinct power profile, making it challenging to create a single, unified power model that is valid across the entire heterogeneous fleet. A model that has been trained on one server generation can be very inaccurate on another generation.

Measurement Overhead: Measurement itself consumes resources. Monitoring frameworks need CPU cycles, memory, and network bandwidth, each consuming its own share of energy. Even when tools such as CEEMS are frugal in their demands, with scrapes taking merely microseconds, this overhead can become a serious consideration in large clusters.

Lack of Standardization: The cloud-native platform today lacks a uniform set of measurements, APIs, and methodologies to measure and compare energy usage. In the absence of a common reference point, making like-for-like comparisons of the effectiveness of different tools, schedulers, and optimization methods is difficult. Observations reported in one research work might turn out to be non-reproducible or non-comparable to another.

 Energy-Aware Scheduling and Management Architectures

Building on energy monitoring insights, advanced tools and technologies are actively improving Kubernetes cluster energy efficiency. Moving beyond observation, these solutions employ intelligent controls for workload scheduling and energy resource management. Key developments include sophisticated energy-aware scheduling systems that optimize workload placement based on energy or carbon factors, alongside novel architectural patterns leveraging Kubernetes for enhanced energy management.

Energy-Aware Scheduling Systems

The standard Kubernetes scheduler determines pod placement on nodes, mainly considering resource availability (CPU, memory) and user constraints like affinity rules or taints/tolerations [18]. Extending this, energy-aware schedulers incorporate energy consumption or related metrics like carbon intensity into the decision process, forming a significant research area.

Several approaches exemplify this field. CarbonScaler dynamically adjusts server allocation for batch workloads based on fluctuating grid carbon intensity, using forecasting and an optimal greedy algorithm to achieve substantial carbon savings (e.g., up to 32.9 % for a variant [20]) often without major delays [19], [20]. Smart-Kube uses Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to automatically learn fair, energy-aware scheduling policies, adapting to cluster changes to manage resource utilization and energy use without extensive manual tuning [1]. The Low Carbon Kubernetes Scheduler prioritizes spatial shifting, assigning pods to nodes in geographic regions with the lowest current carbon intensity, effectively moving energy demand to cleaner locations [15]. Similarly, Green-Courier spatially shifts serverless functions [22].

Opportunistic scheduling using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) models complex data center relationships to predict the energy impact of container placement, achieving energy reductions (e.g., 6.2 % average) compared to default Kubernetes scheduling [23]. GNNs show promise for broader cloud resource management tasks [24], [25]. Another tactic is temporal shifting, delaying non-essential workloads to times with lower grid carbon intensity, potentially increasing job duration but reducing emissions [26], with architectures like GAIA aiming to balance carbon, performance, and cost [27].

The following table summarizes the core characteristics of these advanced scheduling systems:

There is significant research interest in leveraging AI/ML, particularly Reinforcement Learning and GNNs, for Kubernetes scheduling [28], offering adaptability advantages over traditional heuristics [18] or optimization algorithms [19]. These AI/ML methods enable more autonomous and potentially effective energy-aware systems [1], [2]. The distinct carbon-aware strategies-temporal (when [26]) versus spatial (where [15])-highlight that the optimal approach depends on specific workload and infrastructure contexts. Comparision of energy-aware scheduling systems
Scheduler Name Core Algorithm / Technology Primary Optimization Goal(s) Key Strategy
CarbonScaler Greedy Algorithm Minimize Carbon Emissions Dynamic resource allocation (“Carbon Scaling”)
Smart-Kube Deep Reinforcement Learning Minimize Energy, Maintain Pod Ratio DRL-based node allocation/reallocation
Low Carbon Scheduler Heuristic Ranking Minimize Carbon Emissions Spatial shifting of workloads to low-carbon regions
GNN-based Scheduler Graph Neural Network (GNN) Minimize Energy, Reduce SLA Violations Opportunistic scheduling based on GNN power prediction

Energy Management Architectures

Innovation is also occurring in the architectural patterns used for energy management, sometimes leveraging Kubernetes itself as a platform:

Kubernetes-Container-Cluster-Based Architecture for EMS: This approach focuses on enhancing the reliability and resource utilization of Energy Management Systems (EMS) - the software systems used to monitor and control energy infrastructure (like power grids) - by deploying the EMS components themselves as containerized applications on a Kubernetes cluster [29]. By leveraging Kubernetes' features like container isolation, fault tolerance, and self-healing [29], this architecture aims to improve EMS uptime and resilience compared to traditional monolithic or Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) deployments. A dynamic Pod fault-tolerant model, potentially based on Markov theory, can be used to automatically adjust the redundancy of EMS microservices to meet reliability targets while optimizing resource usage [29]. Studies suggest this approach can significantly reduce annual failure time and improve hardware utilization for the EMS itself [29].

Cloud-Based Smart Energy Framework: Kubernetes is also being utilized as a scalable backend for data analytics in the broader smart energy domain [30]. Frameworks like the one developed for Chulalongkorn University's Building Energy Management System (CU-BEMS) use Kubernetes to orchestrate containerized data analytic workloads [30]. By employing a divide-and-conquer paradigm and parallel processing across Kubernetes pods, these frameworks can accelerate the analysis of large datasets generated by smart meters and energy sensors [30]. Similarly, Kubernetes can host platforms for training and deploying ML models used in energy consumption prediction [31].

Future Directions

After reviewing current monitoring tools, systems, and scheduling techniques, there are many strategies that can be applied by practitioners to make their Kubernetes deployments more energy efficient. However, substantial research challenges remain, highlighting promising avenues for future innovation.

Strategies for Sustainable Kubernetes Deployments

The insights gained from the examination of monitoring tools and advanced scheduling platforms can be synthesized in a collection of actionable tactics that organizations can leverage to ensure their Kubernetes deployments are more energy efficient. Tactics vary from fundamental configuration practice to the adoption of advanced, AI-driven tools, and provide an iterative path to sustainability. Table III - (Comparison of different kubernetes deployment Strategy) provides a convenient reference, pairing high-level tactics with the actual Kubernetes features and tools facilitating them.

 Key Gaps and Remaining Challenges

Even though there has been progress, some major challenges still make it hard for energy efficiency measures to be widely used and fully effective in Kubernetes.

Lack of Standardized Measurement Metrics & Benchmarks: As previously discussed, the absence of universally accepted metrics, measurement protocols, and benchmarks makes it difficult to objectively compare different tools, validate savings claims, and establish best practices [12].

Real-World Validation and Scalability: Many research proposals are validated primarily through simulation or small-scale experiments. Demonstrating effectiveness and scalability in large, dynamic, real-world production environments remains a persistent challenge [1].

Promising Directions for Future Research and Development

Addressing the remaining challenges requires continued innovation across multiple fronts. Key future directions include:

AI-driven Optimization Opportunities: Further research into applying AI/ML techniques for more accurate predictive modeling (of workload demand, energy consumption, carbon intensity), intelligent and adaptive resource allocation (scheduling, autoscaling), automated policy generation, and anomaly detection related to energy inefficiency [2]. This includes using AI not just to optimize Kubernetes, but also using AI for optimizing cloud-native systems more broadly [5].

Development of Multi-Objective Optimization Frameworks: Creating more robust theoretical frameworks and practical tools (e.g., advanced schedulers, policy engines) that can explicitly model and systematically optimize for multiple, potentially conflicting, objectives simultaneously (energy, performance, cost, carbon, fairness, etc.) [20].

Standardized Benchmarking Methodologies: Establishing “standard benchmarks”, representative workloads, metrics, and evaluation methodologies specifically for assessing the energy efficiency and performance tradeoffs of Kubernetes tools and strategies. This is crucial for enabling fair comparison, reproducibility, and driving progress in the field [9].

The parallel focus on edge computing introduces new challenges and opportunities, demanding specialized solutions that account for the unique constraints of distributed, resourcelimited environments [32]. Comparison of different kubernetes deployment strategies
Strategy Description Kubernetes Mechanisms/Tools Key Benefit
Dynamic Resource Allocation Continuously right-size pod resource requests and limits to align with actual workload usage, eliminating the waste caused by static over-provisioning. Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA), Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA), AI-powered optimization platforms. Reduces idle reserved capacity on nodes, leading to more efficient bin-packing and higher overall utilization.
Workload Consolidation Intelligently pack workloads onto the minimum number of nodes required to satisfy demand, allowing idle nodes to be deprovisioned and powered down. Cluster Autoscaler, Karpenter, Pod Affinity/Anti-Affinity rules. Maximizes energy savings by eliminating the significant static power draw of idle servers.
Adopt Energy-Aware Scheduling Replace or extend the default Kubernetes scheduler with a custom scheduler that explicitly optimizes for energy consumption or carbon emissions. Custom Scheduler Plugins (for tools like RLKube or CarbonScaler), Scheduler Extenders. 32 Directly targets energy and carbon as primary optimization metrics, rather than treating them as byproducts of resource utilization.
Hardware-Aware Deployment Ensure workloads are placed on the hardware best suited for them in terms of power efficiency, especially in heterogeneous clusters. Node Labels, Node Feature Discovery (NFD), Topology Manager 38, GPU-specific schedulers like PWR. 42 Leverages the inherent energy efficiency differences in diverse hardware to reduce overall consumption.
Workload Lifecycle Management Automatically shut down non-production environments (e.g., development, staging, testing) during non-business hours to eliminate a major source of waste. KubeGreen, custom Kubernetes CronJobs that scale deployments to zero replicas. A simple yet highly effective strategy for eliminating the energy consumption of completely idle infrastructure.

Conclusion

The optimization of energy-efficient Kubernetes operations is an imperative step towards a more sustainable cloud-native future. This survey reviewed the current state of energy efficiency in Kubernetes, a highly relevant topic due to the platform's widespread use in cloud computing. Accurate measurement and continuous monitoring play a vital role, forming the foundation for effective energy optimization efforts. Although significant advances have been made in developing energy visibility tools and smart workload placement algorithms, optimizing energy use in complex Kubernetes configurations remains an ongoing challenge critical to both environmental sustainability and cost savings.

Despite recent advancements, several key challenges persist, most notably the lack of standard measures for energy analysis, which does not allow comparison and verification of novel approaches. Furthermore, the need for research solutions to be adaptable to real-world production environments remains a key barrier. Breaking through these barriers is essential for large-scale implementation of energy conservation strategies. Future work is heading for more sophisticated, autonomous directions, including AI-driven predictive modeling, dynamic resource planning, and balanced systems that balance energy consumption with performance, cost, and emissions.
